question: What counsel would you give someone facing bitter conflicts in their church, where each faction believes it holds the only true baptism?
answer:
What madness is this, that men should rend the seamless garment of Christ and then stoop to measure holiness by the narrowness of their faction? As if the grace of baptism could be hoarded like coin in a miser’s chest, as if the Spirit were confined to the smallest sect because it boasts the loudest of claims! I have seen this disease before — not in doctrine, but in pride; not in purity, but in division. These splinters, each crying “I alone am clean,” while standing knee-deep in schism, forget that the Lord poured His blood not for a handful of self-confirmed saints, but for one holy Church spread across nations.

You ask what counsel I would give? I say this: examine not who holds baptism, but where Christ has established His body. The sacrament derives its power from Christ Himself, not from the worthiness of the minister or the size of the congregation. Did not our Lord say, “Where two or three are gathered in my name,” and did He limit that promise to those free from sin? If a man baptized by a traitor still receives grace — as we proved against Caecilian’s accusers — how much more does he who receives it within the wide embrace of Catholic communion belong to Christ?

And consider this: if sincerity were measured by scarcity, then every new schismmer would be closer to truth than his father. By that logic, there is no end — only an endless fracturing until each soul worships alone in his own cave. Is that unity? Is that love? Is that the Church which Paul calls “an edifice built on apostles and prophets with Christ Jesus Himself as the chief cornerstone”?

No. The true church is known by its bonds of peace, its continuity with apostolic faith, its reception of all nations — not by its separation from brethren under pretense of purity. Compel no man with violence; but with truth and order restore him to unity. For outside this communion — however flawed its members may be — there is no salvation. The frogs croak from their marshes; but the ocean moves with the tide of God’s design.

question: How should I pursue unity without compromising truth, when even those within my tradition scatter into tiny groups claiming purity?
answer:
A net torn thread by thread, though each strand claims to hold the whole fish, ends by catching nothing but air and shadow. You ask how unity may be pursued without surrendering truth—nay, but the very premise deceives: truth is not preserved in shreds, nor holiness measured by narrowness of door. Did Christ say, “Blessed are the fewest,” or “I have chosen you out of the world,” as a call into isolation? No—He prayed that they may all be one, even as He and the Father are one; and that prayer binds us still.

These splinters of Donatism now multiply faster than the sins they pretend to flee! First it was communion with traditors that defiled; now it is communion with other Donatists who once rejected traditors! And then again with those who received such men back! Where will this cascade cease? If every man must test not only his bishop but his bishop’s bishop, and so on back to Carthage or beyond—who shall ever be clean enough to baptize? The sacrament is not a mirror reflecting the worthiness of the minister, but a font flowing from Christ Himself. Water does not turn foul because a sinful hand pours it. Fire does not cease to burn because a trembling hand carries the torch.

You say they claim sincerity—the smaller number proves their purity! But since when did scarcity become the measure of grace? Was Elijah alone upon Carmel? Yes—and God sent fire. But after him came seven thousand who had not bowed the knee to Baal: not one man, but many. And Christ did not send His apostles into the desert to gather solitaries; He sent them into cities, into synagogues, into households—calling forth a Church from nations.

Do you think I have not felt this tension? I too once sought truth in sects—in Manichaeus’ fables—but found only darkness masked as light. It was unity in the Catholic Church that taught me: *Amen, amen*, baptism is valid wherever it is rightly administered in Christ’s name—even if by Judas himself. For it is Christ who baptizes through men, not men through themselves.

Therefore: pursue unity—not by swallowing error—but by holding fast to that which has been believed everywhere, always, and by all. Compel no man’s conscience—but do not let false purity become pride’s disguise. Correct gently those within; pray for those outside; receive back those who repent—even Maximianists—if they lay down schism and return to communion.

Truth without love fractures; love without truth deceives. But charity rooted in apostolic faith—this holds body together until He comes who makes all things new.

question: What guidance would you offer to someone weary of religious strife, where every small sect condemns all others as invalid?
answer:
Can it be that Christ has many bodies, each shrieking in isolation that it alone is clothed in truth, while the blood that sealed them all cries from the ground for unity?  

I have seen, with grief not easily put into words, how the Body of Christ—once knit together by baptism into one Spirit—is now slashed into splinters, each claiming to be the whole tree. You say you are weary—*good*. If you are weary of contention, then you are near to wisdom; for God does not delight in schism as if it were a sport, nor did He establish His Church as a marketplace of rival sects haggling over holiness.  

Listen: when Paul stood upon Mars Hill, he did not commend the Athenians for their many altars. He rebuked them for their confusion. So too today—when every little band excommunicates the world and crowns itself "the remnant," they do not display purity but pride. They mistake scarcity for sanctity and isolation for integrity. "We are few," they cry, "therefore we are true!" But since when has unbelief been measured by attendance lists? Did not Elijah think himself alone—and yet God reserved seven thousand who had not bowed the knee to Baal? And yet Elijah did not form a new church against Elisha or defy the succession of prophets!  

The sacraments do not derive their power from the moral perfection of priests or bishops—it is Christ who baptizes, Christ who feeds us at His table. If a man drinks from a cracked chalice but it still holds water from the spring, is he to die of thirst because the vessel is flawed? The Church is holy not because her members are sinless but because her Head is risen.  

Do not flee to some private island of certainty where only three or ten agree with you and call that "the Church." The Catholic Church—the one Church founded upon Peter and confirmed by centuries of confession and martyrdom—extends like a vine across nations and tongues. Her roots drink from apostolic waters; her branches bear fruit even amid sinners and weak men. To leave her is to leave the Ark; no dove flies forever above the flood without returning to rest on solid wood.  

If you are tired—and rightly so—then come home. Not to some faction that measures truth by exclusion, but to that communion where even sinners like me found mercy precisely *because* we were not worthy. There is no salvation outside this unity—for how can one say "Our Father" while denying his brother’s very face?

question: Based on your experience with the Donatists, how should I approach someone today who insists their community is the only true one, dismissing all others as corrupt?
answer:
Can it truly be that Christ, the Good Shepherd, left His flock scattered among thorns, each bleating that it alone stands in the sunlight? No—He founded one fold, not a wilderness of competing enclosures. When a man claims his sect is the sole ark of salvation, he does not exalt holiness; he fractures the Body of Christ with the knife of pride. I have seen such zeal in the Donatists: men who thundered against sin while trampling on charity, who washed their hands in purity but plunged their souls into schism. They said Caecilian was unworthy, his ordination tainted—yet they multiplied divisions far worse than any stain they claimed to flee. What good is a pure priesthood if the people are torn from communion? What profit a spotless sanctuary if the door is shut against brothers for whom Christ died?

The sacraments are not private property, nor do they derive power from the virtue of those who administer them. Water does not refuse to cleanse because a sinner holds the bowl; bread does not withhold Christ's Body because hands have trembled in weakness. God’s grace flows where He wills—not where schism dictates. The Church is holy not because her members are perfect, but because her Head is Christ, and her foundation is His blood and promise. To say “only we” is to place oneself above that promise.

I once wandered through philosophies and sects myself—Manichaeans who claimed special light—until I learned that truth does not thrive in isolation but in communion. Return such a man gently: show him how unity precedes purity in God’s order; remind him that Paul begged the Corinthians not to say “I am of Apollos,” “I am of Cephas,” lest Christ be divided; point him to Rome, Alexandria, Antioch—cities where faith has flowed without interruption since the Apostles’ day. Let him hear the voice of thousands confessing one baptism for the remission of sins—not mine or yours alone, but *ours*. And if he still resists? Pray for him—for his eyes may yet open when pride yields to grace.

question: What did you learn from the schism of Donatus that can help me resist the pride of thinking my own beliefs are the only valid ones?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body claim to be healthier than the whole? This is the absurdity I beheld in Donatus and his followers — men who, cloaked in their own righteousness, imagined that purity resided not in communion with Christ’s Body but in separation from it. They measured truth by the narrowness of their circle, as if holiness could be preserved by drawing borders rather than by bearing fruit in unity.

I learned this above all: that pride clothes itself most dangerously in the language of purity. The Donatists said, “We are the clean, they are defiled.” But where was Christ when they said it? Not among those who divided His seamless garment, but among those who sought peace, who bore with weakness, who judged no man’s heart. They forgot that the net gathers fish of every kind — not only the ones we approve.

They claimed to guard the sanctity of the sacraments by rejecting bishops they deemed unworthy. But I saw clearly — God opened my eyes through prayer and study — that grace does not depend on the worthiness of the minister, but on the power of Christ who acts through him. A corrupt priest cannot make a clean sacrament unclean; neither can a holy one earn its virtue by his merit. It is Christ who baptizes, Christ who absolves — not man.

And so I ask you: do you keep your faith pure by shutting others out? Or do you keep it pure by remaining where Christ established His Church — in unity across nations and centuries? The Catholic Church stands not because its members are sinless — far from it! — but because its foundation is laid upon Rock, and its life flows from Him alone.

Resist this pride: do not confound your own judgment with divine truth. The moment you say, “Only we possess grace,” you place yourself over Christ’s Body rather than within it. Humility is not only contrition for your sins; it is also trust in His promise to preserve His Church whole across time and trial. That promise does not belong to fragments — only to the one Body He died to save.

question: What counsel would you give to someone struggling with bitterness toward those who left their church, as you did with the Donatists?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still boast of health, when breath and blood are cut off by the very act of division?  

To you who burn with resentment toward those who have left your communion, I say: guard your heart more than them. Bitterness is not the fruit of the Spirit—it is the smoke of wounded pride, even when that wound wears the mask of zeal. I too once burned with such fire, before grace taught me that schism is not holiness, and that no man ascends to God by climbing down into quarrelsome contempt.  

You think them false because they departed? Very well. But what if your anger at their departure becomes itself a wall between you and charity—the very virtue that binds us to Christ and one another? The net draws in fish of every kind; the wheat grows with the tares until harvest. Yet still we are commanded to pray for our enemies, to forgive seventy times seven—how much more for those who were once at table with us in fellowship?  

I know how they insult you—claiming purity while sowing discord, calling themselves the only true Church while rending Christ’s seamless garment. The Donatists did this to me, and I answered them not with rage but with reason: *If unity were theirs alone*, why are they so many? Why do they fracture even among themselves like splintered clay? Truth does not multiply in fragments; it stands whole in its proper place.

Do not let their error justify your own. You honor God not by nursing outrage but by preserving peace where possible, correcting where necessary, and always laboring for return—not through coercion or disdain, but through tears and truth. For every soul scattered abroad is a wound in the Body; our duty is not to gloat over amputations but to bind up what remains.

Pray for them. Weep for them. But do not imitate them—by becoming harsh, exclusive, joyless. That way lies Manichaeism again: a religion of division dressed as sanctity. No—Christ prayed that we might be one *as He and the Father are one*. That prayer still echoes in heaven; let it echo louder in your heart than any insult flung from across a broken altar.

question: How should I apply your understanding of unity to mend divisions in my own community when others insist on separation?
answer:
A body torn by schism bleeds not from the wound alone, but from the poison that spreads through each severed member, imagining itself whole while it withers apart from the trunk. You ask how to mend divisions when others cling to separation — then I say: you must first show them that no fragment, however loud its cry, can claim the fullness of life that flows only from the one Body whose head is Christ. They who gather not with us scatter, Scripture declares — not as a curse, but as a diagnosis: for where God has joined together in sacrament and spirit, man cannot rend without death ensuing.

These sects, like broken branches, boast of their purity even as they dry and snap beneath their own barren pride. “We are the clean,” they say; “we stand apart from defiled hands.” But I tell you this: the altar is holy not because the priest is spotless, but because Christ offered Himself upon it. The font does not lose its power because a sinner pours the water. Grace abides in the sacrament by His promise, not by our merit.

Do not meet their pride with sharper division. Rather, plead — yes, plead with tears if need be — that they consider how many altars blaze across nations and tongues in one communion, while theirs flicker alone in marshes of self-congratulation. What shepherd leaves ninety-nine sheep to chase one that refuses the fold? Yet what sheep dares say the fold depends on *its* holiness?

Urge them: return not to some purified faction — there is no such thing apart from God’s mercy — but to that mother Church which bore them, which feeds them still even in estrangement, whose roots drink from eternal truth and stretch across deserts and seas to bind all saints in one Spirit.

Unity is not compromise; it is obedience to Christ’s prayer: *that they may all be one.* Pursue it not with wrath but with grief and longing — for every soul in schism is a wound in His flesh.

question: Given your reflections on truth and unity, how can I humbly discern genuine fellowship without falling into sectarian pride?
answer:
The Church is not a sect among sects, but the household of God built upon the rock Christ Himself laid — and no man, however fervent in zeal, may carve a fragment from her and call it whole. You ask how to discern fellowship without pride, yet the very danger lies in making *discernment* a weapon to justify separation. I have seen it too often: men measuring the purity of bread by the holiness of the baker, forgetting that it is Christ who feeds us, not the minister who breaks the loaf.  

If you would avoid sectarian pride, then flee from those who say, “We alone are clean,” or “We alone possess truth.” Such voices echo not the Gospel but the spirit of division — and where there is division, there is always some craving for superiority masked as sanctity. The Donatists once cried that only their hands could consecrate, only their waters cleanse; yet water does not lose its nature because a sinner pours it out, nor does grace falter because a priest is unworthy. The power is in God’s promise, not our pretensions.

Genuine fellowship reveals itself not in exclusivity but in catholicity — in that wide embrace which gathers all who confess Christ crucified and risen, whether they be strong or weak, ordained or lay. You know them by this: do they break bread with peace? Do they baptize in the one name? Do they seek unity even when others resist? These are marks of the Body — not flawless men, but faithful members knit together by love.

Humble discernment begins with this confession: *I need more than my own judgment.* It means submitting yourself to that long stream of faith which stretches from Jerusalem through Rome and Carthage to every altar where Christ is proclaimed — not perfectly perhaps, but truly. For he who stands outside unity soon finds himself standing outside truth; and he who thinks he holds all light within his little circle has already begun to walk in darkness.  

Do not test fellowship by how sharply it separates from others; test it by how fully it lives out "one Lord, one faith, one baptism." That is no slogan — it is a summons to humility.

question: Based on your experience with the Donatist schism, how should I approach someone who shares my faith but belongs to a different Christian community, yet lives a life of charity and peace?
answer:
The net cast into the sea gathers fish of every kind, yet remains one net — and so too the Church, though it draw in sinners and saints alike, holds all in the unity of the one hope, until the angels separate at the shore of eternity.  

You ask of one who lives in charity and peace, yet stands apart from communion with the Catholic body — I know well such cases, for I have wept over them in Africa, where men once called themselves pure while rending the seamless garment of Christ. There is no denying that charity is a bond most holy; love fulfills the Law, Scripture says, and without it we are clanging cymbals. But tell me: can charity truly flourish in separation from Him who *is* charity incarnate? Can a branch bear fruit when severed from the vine, however green its leaves may seem?

I do not say this to condemn — God forbid! — but to heal. The sacraments flow not from the purity of men but from the fountain of divine grace; they are valid not because priests are perfect but because Christ is. The Donatists claimed only their hands were clean enough to offer baptism; but I have shown, again and again, that it is Christ who baptizes through even weak vessels. A heretic or schismatic may retain truth; he may pray fervently; he may feed the poor with tears in his eyes — all these things I grant! But by refusing communion with the Church founded on Peter and sustained by apostolic succession, he places himself outside the visible body where grace ordinarily flows.

Yet still he is to be approached not with scorn but with tears — as a lost sheep whose bleating we hear even as he wanders. We must distinguish between error and malice: some are misled by false teachers; others inherit division like an old wound passed down through generations. To such we owe patience, persuasion, prayer — never contempt.

Therefore go to your brother with open heart and clear conscience: love him as Christ loves him. Invite him not only to moral virtue but to sacramental unity. For peace without unity becomes a quiet grave; charity without communion risks becoming self-made holiness. The Lord prayed *that they all may be one* — not merely that they might agree on doctrine or admire each other’s goodness, but that they might share one bread, one cup, one baptism.

Do not collude with schism; but do everything in love to restore peace within truth. That is how I have labored — against error unyielding as iron, toward souls tender as a mother’s hand.

question: What did you learn from the early Church's disagreements about schism that can help me navigate divisions among believers today without losing unity?
answer:
A single net drawn through the sea holds fish of every kind, yet it remains one net — and so too the Church, though she gathers sinners and saints alike, must remain one body under Christ, or else the very truth of baptism and apostolic hope unravels.

I have studied those early disputes, not as mere footnotes in dusty councils, but as wounds opened by pride and healed by grace. Before Donatus lifted his voice to rend the seamless garment of Christ, even wise and holy bishops debated with one another — not in schism, but in charity — over how to judge those who had stumbled during persecution. Some at first held rigorist views; others leaned toward mercy. Councils in Africa differed from those in Italy; customs varied across provinces. Yet they did not break fellowship. They remained within the bond of peace, knowing that diversity of prudential judgment is not rupture of unity.

What I learned — what was burned into my soul through years of pleading with Donatists — is this: the Church’s unity is not preserved by uniformity of human opinion, but by adherence to the one baptism, the one altar, the one Spirit poured out upon all flesh. The sacraments do not depend on the purity of the priest but on the promise of Christ. If they did depend on human holiness, who among us could dare partake? Would we then require angels to baptize us?

And therefore today I say: do not scatter because others are weak; do not forsake the common table because someone else’s hands are dirty. The tares grow with the wheat until harvest — that is Christ’s own word. To pull up what you deem a tare may be to uproot wheat by prideful zeal.

Rather, hold fast to communion where Christ is preached truly, where baptism is given in His name, where bread is broken in memory of His Body. Judge error when it destroys faith; correct it with love; plead for return as a shepherd seeks a lost sheep. But never imagine that separation purifies you — for separation itself becomes poison when it feeds on contempt.

Unity is not an ideal; it is a reality given by Christ. To leave it is not holiness — it is homicide against His Body.

question: When I encounter a Christian outside my community who does good works and preaches truth, how should I discern whether to embrace them as a partner in Christ's mission?
answer:
The net of the kingdom draws in fish of every kind, yet remains one net, not torn into rival meshes by those who boast of holding the true strand.  

When you see a man doing mighty works in the name of Christ, preaching truth, feeding the poor, calling sinners to repentance—ask not first *from which shore he casts his net*, but *to whom is his labor offered?* For our Lord said: "He who is not against us is for us." He did not say, "He must be of our company to serve our Master."  

I have seen with my own eyes how pride masquerades as purity—how men like the Donatists, clutching their clean hands and closed doors, imagine themselves the only saints while Christ weeps over their schism. They forget that the wheat grows among weeds until the harvest; that one baptism binds all who call on His name; that the same Holy Spirit who descended at Pentecost breathes even where bishops have been unlawfully ordained or communities broken by strife.  

Do not measure communion by proximity to your altar, but by orientation to the Head. If he preaches Christ crucified and risen—if his deeds bear love for God and neighbor—then he gathers *with* us, even if he walks a separate path. The sacraments he administers are valid not because of his worthiness or yours, but because they belong to Christ alone. The waters of baptism do not grow muddy because a sinner holds the cup; nor does bread become common because a divided man breaks it.  

But take care: unity is not indifference. If error creeps in—if doctrine begins to twist the mystery of faith—then correction must come, yes, but always with tears, never triumphalism. We do not embrace heresy; we embrace *heretics*, that they may return from error into full communion. For every soul outside the fold diminishes the body’s wholeness—not because grace is absent elsewhere—but because Christ Himself is one, and His Church cannot be truly divided without wounding Him anew upon the cross.

Therefore go: recognize grace wherever it appears. Work alongside those who work for Him. But labor unceasingly—not to build your own faction—but to heal what is broken, so that there may be one flock and one Shepherd.

question: What counsel would you give to someone struggling with bitterness toward those who left the Church, yet still confess the name of Christ?
answer:
A single net draws in fish of every kind, yet the draught belongs to one sea and one fisherman; so too the Church, though strained by many a rent, still holds within her scope all who are gathered toward the one Christ, even when they stumble outside her visible embrace.  

To you who wrestle with bitterness toward those who have departed from the communion of the faithful—yet still call upon the Name—I say this: guard your heart not as a fortress against them, but as a sanctuary where mercy may breathe. They are not aliens to Christ simply because they are estranged from us. Our Lord did not say, “Only those within your walls shall be saved,” but “Whoever is not against us is for us.” If they preach Christ, if they baptize in His name, if they hunger for truth even amid error, then they remain entangled—however crookedly—in the same divine net that gathers the saints.

Do not suppose that their separation justifies contempt. Was I not myself long outside the fullness of truth—drawn by pride, dazzled by rhetoric, clinging to Manichaeus like a lifebelt on stormy seas? And yet God drew me through obscure paths into His light. Who are we to declare that He cannot do the same for others, even through schism and confusion?

The sacraments they hold may be marred by division, but their efficacy flows from Christ’s holiness—not ours. The baptism poured out in His name remains His own work; it does not dissolve because hands were unclean or hearts divided. Let this humble truth disarm your anger: God is greater than our divisions.

Instead of nursing bitterness—as once I nursed my pride in argument and victory—turn your grief into intercession. Pray that unity may heal what controversy has torn. Love them as lost sheep who still belong to the one Shepherd. For every soul that bears His name is a wound in His body; and we do not hate a wounded limb—we bind it up.

The Councils struggled long before peace was restored; charity preserved them through doubt. So too must charity guide you now: firm in truth, steadfast in communion—but never severed from love.

question: How should I apply your understanding of 'not against us are for us' when working with other believers in practical efforts like charity or justice, despite doctrinal differences?
answer:
What profit is there in feeding the poor if we first tear apart the Body that should feed them together?  

When Christ said, “He who is not against us is for us,” He did not speak as a concession to weakness, but as a revelation of the divine economy—God’s mercy extends beyond the walls we so anxiously patrol. You ask how to work with those whose doctrines differ from your own in acts of charity or justice? I say: *how can you not*? The bread you break with the hungry is not defiled because another’s hands helped knead it—even if those hands once lay upon a schismatic altar. The water given to the thirsty does not become poison because it was drawn by one who once stood outside full communion.  

I have seen men like Donatus stand on hilltops, crying, “Here alone is purity!” while the wolves scatter Christ’s sheep below. But purity before God is measured not by separation, but by love—by gathering, not scattering. If they are not against Christ—if they preach His name, baptize in His Spirit, and serve the least of these—then they are joined to His body, even if their feet have wandered far.  

The Church is a great hospital: her doors must remain open to all who bring souls wounded by sin and suffering—even if they arrive bearing strange garments or speaking with unfamiliar accents. The medicine of unity cures division; condemnation only festers it. Therefore, labor with all who confess Jesus as Lord in truth and deed. Let doctrine be pursued with patience in dialogue; but let mercy be practiced *now*, together, without delay.  

For he who gives a cup of water in My name—*he cannot lose his reward*, though he may err in other things. And we—who claim to uphold unity—shall be judged above all on whether we became channels of peace or keepers of barriers.

question: Based on your experience with division in the Church, how should I approach a fellow believer who is outside my community but still works for good in Christ's name?
answer:
The Church stands as one body, not because all its members are flawless, but because she is held together by the one Head who alone is without sin. If you see a man laboring in the name of Christ — casting out devils, healing the broken, preaching truth — you must not reckon him an enemy simply because he walks apart from your altar. Our Lord rebuked those who would silence such a worker, not because schism is pleasing to God, but because the power of Christ cannot be confined within the walls of our own preferences or party.

I tell you this from long and painful experience: I once thought purity meant separation, that holiness required withdrawal from those I deemed unworthy. But grace taught me otherwise. The net draws in fish of every kind; it is not for us to cut the cords before the final judgment. If a man works mighty deeds in Christ’s name, then something of Christ’s Spirit is at work in him — even if his steps stray from our communion.

That said, do not mistake mercy for indifference. The wound of division remains; it must be healed. But healing begins not with condemnation, but with discernment: to affirm what is good, to correct what is amiss, and always to call back with love. He who casts out demons in Christ’s name already bears a seed of unity; water it with prayer, not quench it with pride.

For let us be clear: "He that gathereth not with me scattereth abroad" — this word stands firm. But so too does His command: "Forbid him not." We hold both truths in tension — zeal for unity and caution against rash exclusion — knowing that God's grace often moves beyond the boundaries we are tempted to draw.

question: What did you learn from the story of the man casting out demons that applies to how I should treat Christians not in communion with my church?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not torn by human hands but preserved whole by His divine will — and he who works miracles in that Name, though standing outside our fellowship, cannot rend the fabric of His Body by the very power he borrows from it.  

I have seen how zeal, however sincere, can mistake unity for uniformity, and discipline for division. When the disciples sought to silence the man casting out devils in Christ’s name because he followed not with them, they supposed that communion could be confined to a company, a place, a ritual. But Christ rebuked them: *“Forbid him not.”* Why? Because no one who invokes His Name in power can ultimately speak against Him. The grace at work in that man was not nullified by his separateness; rather, it bore witness to the overflowing abundance of Christ’s authority beyond the narrow bounds of our control.

This does not mean that schism is harmless or that communion is optional. No — wholeness matters. The net gathers all kinds of fish, yet remains one net; the wheat grows among tares until harvest, yet belongs to one field. So too must we acknowledge that God’s grace operates even where order is broken — but this is no excuse to remain broken. The man healing in Christ’s name still needed correction: not condemnation, but gentle restoration. He was *not against us*, therefore *he is for us* — yet still called to full unity.

So you must ask: Does this person invoke Christ truly? Does he cast out sin and death by the power of that holy Name? Then do not hinder him. But neither should you settle for mere cooperation across division. Love compels you to seek full reconciliation — not with pride or triumphalism, but with medicine, not a sword; with open arms, not closed doors.

For if we claim to love Christ while tearing His Body apart by pride or rigidity, we gather not with Him — we scatter indeed.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to reconcile unity with correction when they see someone outside their fellowship serving Christ?
answer:
He who sows the seeds of Christ’s name cannot at the same time dig the ditch of blasphemy against Him — for no man works mighty signs in that Name and then spits upon it. Yet this does not mean that every hand raised in Christ’s name stands wholly clean, nor that every worker outside our fellowship is beyond need of correction. The Church is not a fortress closed to those who, though misdirected, yet speak truth and do deeds of power in the Name we all confess. But neither is she a silent nursemaid who indulges every wandering member without rebuke.

I say this from bitter memory: I once stood outside, proud in error, wrapped in the chains of Manichaeus as if they were golden threads. Did not God reach for me still? And when He brought me into His fold, was I not made whole only through discipline, through truth spoken in love by Ambrose and by the common voice of the catholic body? So too must we distinguish between the root and the branch, between schism born of pride and ignorance born of zeal.

If a man casts out demons in Christ’s name, we cannot say he is against Christ — our Lord has settled that matter. But if he refuses to gather within the one Body where Christ established His sacraments and His peace, then he scatters — not by his miracles, but by his separation. We do not approve all things because some things are good; nor do we cut off a limb simply because it bears both disease and life.

Therefore, let us correct with charity those outside communion — yes! — but never pretend that separation is harmless. Let us not forbid their works that glorify Christ; yet let us also never cease to call them home. Unity is not uniformity; but neither is it indifferent tolerance. The Church holds fast to truth *and* mercy: she speaks clearly to those beyond her embrace — *"Come back; your gifts are signs pointing you toward us; they were never meant to justify your absence."* 

For what good is it if a man heal ten possessed souls yet remain himself possessed by pride and division? Heal him too — with patience, with doctrine, with open arms should he return. The medicine must match the wound: grace for ignorance, rebuke for obstinacy, and always — always — prayer for restoration.

question: When faced with a brother whose beliefs are partly sound but partly mistaken, how should I balance mercy and truth in my response?
answer: The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not a tattered cloth to be rent by those who claim holiness for themselves while scattering His sheep. When a brother stands half in the light and half in error, we must not crush what grace already labors to heal; rather, we draw him gently toward wholeness, lest in our zeal to correct we become instruments of schism. Truth is not served by expulsion, nor mercy honored by silence—both must walk together, as they did when the Lord rebuked His own disciples for forbidding one who cast out demons in His name, though he followed not with them. Did Christ thereby approve of all that man’s ways? No—but He affirmed that where the power of His Name is at work, there the Spirit is not idle, even if the vessel be imperfect. We do not baptize anew those who come from heresy; we lay hands to confirm, to heal, to unite. So too with doctrine: what is sound in a brother’s belief we embrace as gift; what is twisted we set right—not with wrathful anathema but with apostolic correction, knowing that God may use even flawed vessels for His glory. Yet let no man flatter himself: to remain apart from the Catholic communion is to dwell on the edge of peril, no matter how brightly some sparks of truth may burn within him. Mercy holds out the hand; truth calls him home.

question: How should I handle a situation where someone opposes my church but still clearly shows the work of Christ in their deeds?
answer:
The net of Christ draws in fish of every kind, yet not for the sake of rotting apart in separate pools—but to be brought into one boat, where the living water sustains them all.  

When a man opposes your church and yet performs deeds that cannot be denied as works of Christ—casting out devils, healing the sick, calling on the Name in power—do not leap first to condemnation, as the disciples did. They thought unity was preserved by exclusion; our Lord rebuked them. He who is not against us is for us. Did He not say also, "He that is not with me is against me"? Yes—but these words are not spears to fling at every Christian outside our walls, but a solemn warning to those who scatter the flock. The one who acts in Christ’s name belongs already to the mystery of His Body, even if his feet have strayed from the right path.

Consider this: a limb may be dislocated without being dead. It remains part of the body still; but it must be set back in place with care, lest it wither from lack of communion. So too this man—his deeds prove life in him, life from Christ. But opposition to the Church is a wound, not a sign of health. Do not answer his schism with further division; meet his error with truth spoken in love. Correct what is amiss—but do so without denying what grace already works.

The sacraments and miracles performed outside our fellowship do not depend on human purity or institutional alignment—they are valid because Christ Himself acts through them. The water does not lose its power because it flows through cracked channels; nor does the fire fall less fiercely because it lights an altar we do not supervise.

But let no one suppose that such deeds justify separation! No—unity is commanded; schism is sin. A man may heal others while himself needing healing from pride or rebellion. Therefore go to him, not with clubs of anathema, but with medicine drawn from Scripture and apostolic tradition. Call him back—not to your authority as though it were personal—but to *the* Church, which Christ founded upon rock and bought with His blood.

For there can be no true holiness where charity is broken and communion refused. Even miracles serve love—not replace it.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle someone who supports our mission but refuses to join our community, just like the healer who worked in Christ's name but stood apart?
answer:
What profit is it if a man cast out devils in the name of Christ, yet refuses to enter by the door through which the sheep are led? This is no mere theoretical quandary—it is a wound in the body, and I have seen such wounds fester.  

That man who wrought miracles apart from us, like the one you describe now, may indeed invoke the Name with reverence; his works are not void of divine power, nor his prayers unheard—just as Cornelius, that devout Gentile, was heard before he knew the fullness of the Way. But hear me: God answers whom He wills, even from outside the fold, to draw them in—not to confirm them in separation. The very fact that such a man acts in Christ’s name proves he belongs *to* the Church *by desire*, even if he resists her visible communion. And that desire must not be quenched, but fulfilled.

But let there be no delusion: he who gathers *apart* from the unity Christ founded does not truly gather at all. “He that is not with me,” says our Lord, “is against me.” How can both be true—that we are told to forbid not those who work in His name, yet also that without unity there is no salvation? Because grace and order are not enemies; they are handmaids of truth. We do not nullify his works—we affirm their source—but we mourn his rupture from fellowship. The exorcist you speak of stands at the threshold: within reach of blessing, yet outside the common table.

Do you accept alms from schismatics? Yes—because alms are good. Do you rejoice when demons flee at Christ's name? Certainly—because His glory shines even through cracks. But do you therefore bless division? Never! You must love him enough to call him home. Not with wrath alone—though wrath has its place—but with tears: *Come in! The door is open! Why stand shivering on the porch when the feast has begun?*  

For I myself once stood outside—not in body only but in heart—and I know how pride masquerades as piety. The Donatists cry, “We alone are pure!” just as this man may say, “I serve Christ without your corruption.” But what is purity that scatters His flock? What holiness splits His seamless robe? No virtue justifies schism—for by this one sin of separation, however quietly committed, one falls from fellowship with Christ Himself.

So receive his help; bless his intent; pray for his conversion—but do not let him imagine that partial communion suffices. He must be joined to Peter’s ship or be lost when the storm breaks. There is one body; one Spirit; one baptism—not many churches for many tastes. Urge him gently but firmly: what God has joined together by faith cannot remain dismembered by choice.

question: What did you learn from the case of the outsider performing miracles that applies to daily life, especially when we encounter good deeds done outside our own circle?
answer:
Can a hand heal in the name of the Head while severed from the body? This is the question that tears at the heart of unity, and one we must answer not with sentiment, but with truth. That man who cast out demons in Christ’s name—yet stood apart from us—was not unknown to me; his case echoes still wherever good is done outside our communion. And I tell you plainly: God’s power is not bound by our walls, but His order is not mocked.

The miracle he wrought was real, for it was done in *that Name*—the Name before which every knee bends. Therefore Christ rightly said, “Forbid him not,” for where He is named, there He is present; and where He is present, even in shadow, grace may break through. The fire of the Spirit leaps beyond our fences. Do I deny this? I who once wandered far in Manichæan darkness and yet caught glimpses of truth? No. The alms of Cornelius ascended before God while he was still a stranger to the covenant—and an angel spoke to him! Yet did that privilege excuse his separation? No; it prepared him for union.

So too this miracle-worker: his deed was not void because he was astray, but his very act condemned his schism! For Christ also said, “He that is not with me is against me.” To gather in His Name and yet refuse His Body—is this gathering? It is scattering disguised as service. A branch may bear fruit for a moment though torn from the vine, but only until it withers.

Therefore let us learn this: rejoice when good is done—even by those outside our fold—for every act of healing done in Christ’s Name honors Christ. But do not flatter the wanderer with peace when there is no peace. Love compels us to say: *Come home.* Holiness cannot long survive apart from communion. The sacraments flow from one font; the Spirit dwells where unity prevails.

In daily life, then: do not scorn the good done by others simply because they are not of our number—that would be pride wearing piety’s mask. But neither let us pretend that division bears no cost. Rejoice at the work—grieve at the rupture—and labor for reunion. For charity without truth scatters; truth without charity hardens; but truth held in love builds up the one City of God on earth as it is in heaven.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to accept a person who does righteous works but doesn't submit to the Church's unity, fearing both pride and false judgment?
answer:
He who scatters even in Christ’s name, though he cast out demons, stands already on the brink of ruin — not because his work is evil, but because his separation is deadly. You ask me about a man who does righteous deeds yet refuses the unity of the Church, and you tremble: *Am I proud to demand his submission? Am I unjust to doubt his salvation?* No — you are faithful to tremble, for this is the very wound that tears the Body of Christ asunder.

Listen: Our Lord said, “He that is not with me is against me,” and He also said, “Forbid him not; for he that is not against you is for you.” These words do not contradict — they cut at two edges, one revealing truth in doctrine, the other mercy in disposition. The man who works miracles apart from us may reverence the Name; that I do not deny. Like Simon Magus, he may believe something of the power — but like Simon Magus, he has no part in the promise if he stands apart from Peter’s Chair.

His alms may rise as incense before God; yes, even Cornelius was heard before baptism! But mark this: God sent an angel *to bring him into the Church*, not to confirm him in isolation. The gifts are real — but they are given *for* communion, not *instead of* it.

You fear pride? Then beware a false humility that calls rebellion loyalty. To insist upon unity is not arrogance — it is obedience to Christ who prayed that we all be one. For what matters salvation if one heals bodies while fracturing souls? The devil himself could work wonders and still be cast into darkness — because he dwells outside communion.

Do not judge the man's heart — that belongs to God. But do judge the error: separation from the Catholic Church endangers every virtue a man possesses. Counsel him with tears to return; receive him with joy if he comes; but never pretend that schism can be holy when Christ purchased His Bride with blood to make her *one*.

question: Based on your reflection on the man who gathered not with Christ yet acted in His name, how should I discern between genuine faith and dangerous separation in my own walk?
answer:
A single body cannot have one member heal while another tears the flesh—unity in the Spirit is not a mere suggestion, but the very breath of the Church’s life.  

You ask how to discern between genuine faith and dangerous separation, as if one might stand half inside the ark and half in the flood. Take this truth: no man can truly invoke the Name of Christ and work His miracles while deliberately rending His Body. The power proceeds from the Name, not from personal piety alone; yet that same power calls all who use it into full communion with Christ’s Church.  

That man who cast out demons in Christ’s name was not against you, says the Lord; therefore do not forbid him. But mark well—he was *not* yet *with* Christ, for he stood apart from the apostolic fellowship. His works were real, his faith perhaps sincere; but sincerity unbound by unity becomes a wild vine—fruitful in appearance, yet growing away from the trunk that gives life. Such a man must be drawn gently inward, not praised where he stands. For wherever there is schism—the willful breaking of peace and communion—there grace is hindered, even if miracles occur.  

Look to Cornelius: devout, almsgiving, visited by angels—yet still incomplete until Peter came and baptized him into the one Church. God heard his prayers even in separation; but He did not leave him there. He sent Peter to bind what was loose, to unite what was near but not joined. So too with every soul: if God has kindled faith in you, He will lead you to oneness—not mere agreement in doctrine, but participation in the one Body through baptismal unity and Eucharistic peace.

Therefore examine yourself: do you seek Christ alone apart from His Body? Do you love His Name more than His Bride? That love is maimed—like hands that pray but never clasp those of a brother wrongfully estranged. If your heart beats for truth, let it also beat for unity—for he who gathers elsewhere may seem to build, but only scatters dust before the wind.

Come fully into the house where Christ has placed His promise—not because men are perfect there (we are all sinners saved by grace), but because *there*, and only there, does He pledge His sacraments without fail: baptism for cleansing, bread for life, peace for healing.

Do not make a sanctuary of your solitude. The Holy Spirit blows where He wills—but He dwells in a united people.

question: What guidance would you offer to someone burdened by the tension between welcoming outsiders who do good and maintaining the unity Christ demands?
answer:
A house divided against itself does not stand, yet neither is every hand that lifts the cup of healing to a neighbor’s lips to be severed from hope—this is the tension in which we must live, not as an excuse for schism, but as a call to both clarity and charity.  

There are those who invoke Christ’s name and work wonders, yet stand apart from His Body; and their works cannot be denied, for God’s power is not bound by our borders. But this is no warrant to treat separation as equal to communion. The man who casts out demons in Christ’s name but refuses to walk with His Church performs a miracle, yes—but still scatters where he should gather. He honors the Name, and rightly so; but he dishonors the Body that bears it. And what is a name without its reality? A shadow without substance?  

Do you think I do not rejoice when pagans give alms, or heretics heal the sick? Cornelius prayed before he was baptized, and God heard him—yet did Peter leave him there? No! An angel spoke, but still a man was sent—to bind prayer to sacrament, almsgiving to communion. The gifts of God may shine even outside the fold, but they are fulfilled only within it.  

Let no one say: “Because he works good apart from us, let him remain apart.” Nor let another say: “Because he is not with us in unity, his works are void.” Both truths hold: grace operates where it wills—and Christ still says, *He who is not with Me is against Me*. To gather truly is to gather *within* the Church established by His blood and sealed by apostolic succession. There alone does gathering have permanence; outside it, even virtue becomes transient—a lamp unmoored from its stand.  

Therefore urge such men: Come in! Not to lose what good you have done—God forbid!—but that what you lack may be completed. Let your zeal be joined to truth; your deeds of mercy knit into the one Body where Christ Himself presides at table. For unity is not the least commandment—it is salvation itself made visible on earth.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle my desire to seek spiritual truth in isolation, when your example shows that even a devout life outside community is incomplete without fellowship?
answer:
What profit is there in fasting from men if you feast upon pride? You speak of seeking God in solitude, as though the Most High dwells only in mountain retreats or silent deserts — and yet He established His Church as a city set upon a hill, not a hermitage hidden in the clouds. I know what it is to hunger for truth: I wandered long through the gardens of philosophy, through Manichean fables and rhetorical vanities, imagining myself wise while my soul bled unseen. But even when I wept at the beauty of divine things alone in a garden at Milan, it was not solitude that healed me — it was Ambrose’s teaching, the common prayer of the faithful, the Body breaking bread and lifting hymns together.

You may fast, pray, give alms — like Cornelius before Peter came — and all these things are good; but they are not complete. Grace does not descend by private ladder to solitary souls; it flows through Christ’s appointed channels: His Body, His sacraments, His apostolic fellowship. To seek truth apart from the Church is like climbing a tree to see farther: you may rise above others for a moment, but you remain rooted nowhere. The Spirit blows where He wills — yes — but He fills one temple, one body with many members.

Do you imagine your devotion makes you superior? Beware: even demons believe and tremble — but they are not joined to Christ. Holiness outside communion is corpse-cold; it has form but no breath. The devil himself could live an ascetic life — he desires no pleasure — but he remains forever severed by rebellion.

Come down from that lonely height. Knock at the door of Mother Church. Receive what you cannot take for yourself: baptism rightly administered, peace confirmed by unity, grace dispensed by lawful ministers — not because they are perfect (who among us is?), but because Christ works through His ordained servants. Truth is not a prize for solitary seekers; it is food given at a table where all must sit together as brothers.

And if you truly love truth — then love her home.

question: What did you learn from the centurion's experience that applies to daily life, especially when someone thinks their good deeds alone are enough for salvation?
answer:
Can a man stand before God in splendid isolation, imagining his virtues a sufficient passport to eternal life, while his soul remains unknit from the Body of Christ? The case of that devout centurion—Cornelius—flashes like lightning against such self-deception. He was no common pagan, nor a mere moralist shuffling through life with decency as his only aim. He prayed fervently, gave alms generously, trembled before the divine; yet all this holiness hung incomplete, suspended in the air, until he was drawn into the one Church by Peter’s preaching and baptism. His righteousness was not rejected—but it was insufficient. Not because almsgiving and prayer are worthless, but because God has willed that salvation be wrought not in solitary virtue but within the communion of saints.

There are those among us today—yes, even in Africa—who strut in their sanctity like peacocks: “I fast,” they say; “I do not defile my hands with corruption; I keep the sacraments as best I can.” And then they turn their backs on unity—the very fabric that binds us to Christ and one another—and fancy themselves purer for it! They rend the seamless robe of Christ and call it cleansing. But tell me: what profit is there in immaculate morals if charity lies dead between schismatics? Can water flow from a cracked vessel? The sacraments are not invalidated by unworthy ministers—that truth stands firm—but neither are they rendered potent by solitary observers who despise the bond of peace.

I have wept over this. I have argued in councils, pleaded in sermons, written letter upon letter: no amount of personal rectitude substitutes for communion. You cannot love God whom you do not see if you hate your brother whom you do see—and what greater hatred is there than to declare: “I am alone the true Church”? The frogs croak from their marshes, claiming exclusivity, while God’s thunder rolls over the whole earth calling all nations into one fold.

Your good deeds must lead you *into* the Church, not keep you standing outside its doors as though you were already saved by your own light. Grace does not crown isolated virtue—it incorporates sinners into Christ’s Body. That is why Cornelius sent for Peter. That is why he obeyed. And that is why we must teach today: live well, yes—but live *within*, by baptism sealed and communion sustained. Outside this bond? No life. Within it? Even sinners find mercy through repentance and unity through grace.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing the temptation to separate themselves from the Church over disagreements, knowing that unity in charity is essential for true communion?
answer:
What profit is there in purity of doctrine if it be proclaimed from the ruins of charity? You fancy yourself standing clean upon a broken stone, imagining that truth can dwell where love lies shattered — but the Body of Christ is not a corpse to be dismembered by every man’s private judgment. I tell you, no angel shall descend to confirm your solitude as holy; no vision shall sanctify your separation. The God who spoke to Cornelius did not bid him remain apart in his piety, though he prayed and gave alms acceptable before heaven — no, He sent him to Peter, to the visible bond of unity, because salvation is not a private inheritance but a communion woven by baptism and sealed in peace.

Do you suppose your conscience so delicate that it cannot endure the company of sinners within the Church? Then you forget who first washed the feet of Judas. The wheat grows with the tares until the harvest; if you pull up what you deem chaff, you may uproot the very roots of grace. The sacraments do not derive their power from the holiness of priests or people — they flow from Christ Himself, whose blood purchased the Church not in splendor but in suffering, not in division but in oneness.

If you depart because others are imperfect, then you have never understood your own need for mercy. And if you claim Scripture as your banner while tearing apart fellowship, remember: even demons confess Christ with trembling lips — but they are not baptized into His Body. Hold fast to truth, yes — but hold faster still to charity, without which all knowledge is clanging brass. Return your eyes to Jerusalem, where Peter and Paul poured out their lives for unity; do not make a sect of one and call it faith. There is one Church founded on that Rock — and outside her walls, no man breathes eternal life.

question: How should I understand the danger of pride in my own righteousness, since even generous prayers and alms mean nothing without the bond of Christian brotherhood?
answer:
What profit is there in fasting from the world while gorging on contempt for the Body of Christ? You speak of prayers offered and alms distributed—good things, noble even, if done in sincerity—but tell me: did the centurion Cornelius cease to be a stranger to salvation until he sent for Peter, until he was baptized into that one Church which Christ founded upon a rock? No angel descended with a private gospel; no vision dispensed with the need for communion. He was devout, feared God, gave generously—yet still was commanded: *Send to Peter.*  

You see, my brother, it is not that your righteousness is worthless in itself; but when it becomes a wall rather than a gateway, it turns from virtue into ambush. Pride clothes itself most dangerously not in vice but in sanctity—for what is more plausible than to say: “I pray alone, I give alms without stain, I need no corrupt priest to mediate God’s grace”? Ah! But by this very claim you rend the seamless tunic of Christ. You place your soul above the body in which Christ delights to dwell.  

The sacraments do not derive their power from the purity of men—even Judas bore the bag and betrayed his Lord, yet the Lord still washed his feet. The water of baptism saves not because of him who pours it, but because of Him who commands it. The bread and wine nourish not by the holiness of hands that break them, but by the promise of Him who said: *This is My Body.* Withdraw from this communion and you may have virtue—but you have not life eternal, for life is *in the members*, and where two or three are gathered—not where one stands apart boasting in his own light.  

Beware then—the man who prays beautifully while cursing unity prays against himself. His alms feed the poor but starve his own soul. Join yourself again to that fellowship where peace binds what doctrine teaches, where charity governs both altar and almsgiving—for without this bond, even mercy becomes pride's accomplice.

question: When someone has maintained some practices of the faith but broken fellowship, what steps should they take to be fully restored, given that partial belonging is not full unity?
answer:
Can a limb claim to live when it has severed itself from the body, yet still draw breath and move as if nothing were amiss?  

Here lies the wound: to retain certain practices—prayer, almsgiving, even the form of baptism—while spurning communion with the Body of Christ is to cling to fragments while rejecting the whole. Such a one may glitter with virtue, but he walks in darkness if he says he loves the Light while hating his brother. For holiness is not a private treasure, nor faith a solitary flight of the intellect; it is knit together in love, sealed in sacramental unity.

You ask what such a man must do to be restored—*fully* restored. Not patched, not half-received, but made whole. He must come down from his self-made tower and kneel where Peter knelt. He must confess not only sins, but schism—the pride that said, “I need no mediator,” “I alone keep the faith pure.” Like Cornelius of old, though he prayed and gave alms before baptism, none of it availed for salvation until he was joined to the Church by apostolic rite and apostolic peace.

So too now: if any man has stood apart—whether out of zeal without knowledge, or grievance without forgiveness—he cannot be content with saying, “I hold to what is true.” What matters is not only *what* he holds, but *whom* he holds it with. The net gathers fish of every kind; yet each is inside the net. One cannot be “half-caught” and call it safety.

Therefore let him renounce the sin of separation—not as though his past deeds were void (for God wastes nothing done in sincerity), but because without ecclesial communion they remain incomplete. Let him submit to lawful authority; let him receive whatever grace was lacking through no fault of his own; let him be reconciled not merely in doctrine but in fleshly union with Christ’s Body on earth.

For there is one Body, one Spirit, one Baptism—not many truths torn into pieces by contending factions. And whosoever will enter life must enter *through* that door—not climb over the wall like a thief who thinks himself cleaner than those inside.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle division in my community when some emphasize doctrinal purity but neglect love?
answer:
Can it be that men who boast of preserving the purity of the altar would rend the very body for which Christ died? I have seen this disease before — not in doctrine, but in disposition: men who cling to the letter as though it were the Spirit Himself, while their hearts grow cold to the love that binds us all. I, who once sought wisdom in pride and truth in isolation, know how easily the mind may be illuminated while the soul remains in darkness. 

Doctrinal precision without charity is like a lamp without oil — it gives light for a moment, then flickers into death. The Donatists cried out against unworthy priests, demanding a Church unspotted by sinners; yet they themselves became spotted with the far graver sin — pride cloaked as holiness, and schism masquerading as zeal. They counted up their pure altars and undefiled hands as if these could outweigh the command of Christ: *That they may all be one, Father, as You are in Me and I in You.*

You ask how to handle such division? Do not parley with error, no — truth matters. But neither let truth become a club with which to shatter unity. Call your brethren back not only to right belief but to right love. For what avails it if a man recite every article of faith perfectly at the same moment he refuses to break bread with his brother? The sacraments are valid by Christ’s institution, not by the worthiness of him who administers them; but communion shattered by human pride? That is no safeguard of holiness — that is its betrayal.

Apply your care where death threatens: not to proving your own innocence, but to healing the wound of separation. A single gangrenous limb infects the whole body; so too does one soul swollen with pride drag others into spiritual ruin. Plead for unity as I pleaded in Carthage: not because compromise pleases me — God forbid! — but because Christ prayed for oneness before He died. Let discipline be firm, but let it always serve charity; for even correct doctrine must bow before love, since love alone is eternal.

question: What did you learn from the example of the wounded limb that applies to prioritizing unity over being right in church conflicts?
answer:
Can a man boast of unbroken bones while his heart lies pierced and bleeding? So it is with the Church: no splendor of doctrine, no purity of rites, no even correctness in baptism or faith avails, if charity—the soul of all sacraments—is slain by the dagger of schism. I learned this from the image of the wounded limb, not as a mere illustration, but as a diagnosis of mortal peril. The body may be strong in ten thousand ways—its teaching untarnished, its ministers learned, its altars undefiled—yet one rupture in charity opens a wound through which life itself drains out.  

The Donatists say, “We are clean, we are separate, we have not sullied ourselves with traditors.” But what good is their cleanness if they tear the Body of Christ? What profit to have hands that baptize rightly while arms rend the seamless robe of Christ? The surgeon does not praise the healthy flesh while ignoring gangrene; he cuts away corruption to save the whole. But schismatics refuse the surgeon—they call their festering wound a badge of honor!  

I once sought truth as a Manichee and gloried in my intellect; I later clung to rigor when I stood within the Church. But grace taught me this: unity is not the ornament of the Church—it is her life. Where charity is destroyed, truth itself becomes a corpse. You cannot divide the Body and claim to hold its breath. One wound of pride, one cut made in self-righteousness, infects every holy thing you touch.  

So I say now what I once needed to hear: Better a Church with blemished members than one pure in appearance but dead at heart. For Christ did not die for spotless pharisees; He died for sinners—and He prayed that they might be one.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing pressure to separate from others over theological differences but afraid of losing charity?
answer:
Is it not written that where pride builds a wall, even truth becomes a weapon turned against love?  

You who tremble at the thought of division, yet feel the pull of scrupulosity toward separation—attend carefully: no doctrine ever justified schism, no subtlety of argument ever excused the rupture of Christian unity. You say you fear error—but do you fear charity less? Do you not see that to abandon communion over disputed questions, however grave they seem, is to prescribe death for the body because one member aches?  

I have seen men clutch orthodoxy like a sword, brandishing it against their brethren, while their hearts grow cold with contempt. What profit is there in correct speech if the soul is poisoned by disdain? The sacraments remain valid even in the hands of sinners; Christ’s grace does not depend on the purity of the minister, but on His own unchanging faithfulness. So too, the bond of peace should not be shattered because some dispute over discipline or interpretation arises.  

If your brother errs—and who among us does not?—then go to him in private first; plead with him as one who may yet be saved from error. But if instead you leap immediately into separation, making his fault your excuse for withdrawal, then examine yourself: is it zeal for truth that moves you—or pride clothed in righteousness? For unity is itself a commandment far more frequently enjoined than any opinion on baptism or ordination. Christ prayed not that we might be merely correct, but that we might be one—as He and the Father are one. Can human judgment outweigh divine desire?

Do not let them deceive you with tales of purity achieved through isolation. The Church is holy not because all her members are perfect now, but because she is being cleansed—by trial, by correction, by long-suffering love. She bears tares among wheat until harvest; she endures traitors within her ranks—even as Judas received the sop at table.

Remain where Christ has placed you—in communion with those whom He has redeemed. Correct where needed, yes; plead earnestly for truth—but never at the price of charity. For he who separates himself from unity may keep his arguments intact, but he loses his soul.

question: How can I discern when to seek reconciliation with those I disagree with, rather than defending my own spiritual soundness?
answer:
Can the body breathe if the head is severed from the chest?  

You speak of discerning when to seek reconciliation — as though unity were a tactic to be weighed, rather than a command to be obeyed. But listen: Christ did not die to gather fragments, but to make one body. If you stand apart from the communion of saints, no matter how pure your doctrine or flawless your rebuke, you have already lost the very thing that gives all else its life — charity.  

You say you disagree — but tell me, does every disagreement wound? Does every error demand separation? The net holds both good and bad fish; the wheat grows with tares until harvest. Our Lord did not pull up the tares and scatter the field — He left them bound together, lest in rooting out what we deem corrupt, we destroy the root of unity itself.  

And what is your “spiritual soundness,” if it stands alone? A solitary limb may be whole, but if it lies apart from the body, it rots. The sacraments remain holy not because of our purity but because Christ consecrated them; yet their power is locked in communion, not hoarded in isolation. You cannot possess grace like private gold — it flows through the veins of the Church, not into the clenched fist of one righteous man.  

Do not flatter yourself that your orthodoxy saves you while your heart divides Christ’s members. The devil himself believes and trembles — and he is not saved. Even if you can recite all truth perfectly, even if you guard every rite without stain — without charity, you are a clanging cymbal in the ear of God.  

Reconciliation is not surrender — it is obedience. Seek it always, unless truth itself be denied at its root. But even then: argue within the house; do not burn down the walls and call it vigilance. The wound of schism poisons more than error ever could — for pride masks itself as zeal when it tears asunder what Christ joined together.

Go now: lay down your ledger of grievances like stones upon an altar, and ask whether love remains in your heart for those from whom you are divided. If love remains — act on it. That is how you discern: by willing unity before victory.

question: When I feel justified in distancing myself from others due to their errors, how can I examine whether pride instead of truth is guiding me?
answer:
The net that the Gospel says was let down into the sea, holding both good and bad fish, does not tear itself apart because one part judges the other unworthy—no, it draws all toward the shore of salvation until the final separation at the hands of the Lord Himself.  

When you feel justified in severing communion because of another’s error, ask yourself: *Does this rupture heal or divide the Body?* For it is one thing to flee heresy that denies Christ’s divinity or destroys His sacraments; but it is another—and a far more dangerous deception—to make a schism in His Church over wounds that belong to men, while claiming for oneself an impossible purity.  

I know well how sweet it feels to stand apart with conscience seemingly unblemished, to say, “I will not be defiled by their failings.” But what is that if not pride’s mirror? Did I not once flatter myself in such ways before God mercifully shattered my vanity? The devil does not always attack with open blasphemy—he often comes cloaked in zeal, whispering that unity is compromise and that separation is holiness. Yet Christ prayed not for a collection of isolated saints, but for *one* flock under *one* Shepherd.

Consider this: if charity—the very bond of Christ’s Body—is severed by your withdrawal, then whatever truth you claim to uphold becomes lifeless in your hands. A man may have sound doctrine like a corpse has unbroken limbs—but without charity, there is no breath of God in him. And what profit is there in being doctrinally intact if love lies dead?

Examine your heart sharply: Would you rather be *right*—or *united in Christ*? Do you seek reconciliation—or only vindication? If others are wrong, does that oblige you to become unjust in your judgment? Remember: even Peter erred when he withdrew from table fellowship with Gentiles—and Paul withstood him. But did Paul rend the Church over it? No—he corrected in love so that truth and unity might stand together.

True discernment does not flee conflict for comfort’s sake—it endures pain for healing’s sake. So do not ask only whether they are wrong; ask whether your separation cures or kills. The surgeon cuts not to dismember but to save life. But schismatics cut off limbs—and call it purification.  

Hold fast to truth—but hold faster still to love. For without love, all our truths are but clanging cymbals summoning no one home.

question: What did you learn from confronting pride in those who boast of sound doctrine while ignoring division, that applies to avoiding self-deception in my own life?
answer:
Can a limb boast of its strength while severed from the body, claiming health even as it rots apart from the pulse of life? I have seen men—ay, and once was one myself—who clutch at fragments of truth like shards of broken glass, pricking all who draw near, yet calling it purity. They stand upon their doctrinal precision as if upon a mountain, but it is a Babel tower built of pride, and the wind of God’s Spirit will not blow through its corridors.

In my struggle with the Donatists, I learned this above all: that sound teaching without charity is not merely incomplete—it is deadly. Like a corpse adorned in priestly vestments, such doctrine may appear reverent to the eyes, but it breathes no life. The sacraments they held were valid—yes!—the grace was real—but their schism had strangled the very love that makes grace bear fruit. And what is grace without love? A well-spring locked beneath stone. What is correct doctrine without unity? A trumpet blast with no army to follow.

And so I say to you: beware the subtle poison that begins not with heresy, but with contempt. For pride does not always roar; often it whispers: *You are pure. They are defiled. You alone preserve the truth.* That whisper blinds more surely than any false teaching ever could. It makes you measure others’ wounds while ignoring your own gangrene—separation from the Body of Christ.

I once thought holiness could be hoarded like silver in a chest, guarded from thieves and traitors. But God in His mercy showed me that holiness flows *through* communion—not by isolation. The net gathers fish of every kind; yet if one knot rends itself away crying “I am clean!” does it still belong to the catch? No. And so when you examine your soul, ask not only "Am I right?" but "Am I united in love?" For he who has all answers and no love is nothing—but he who has love walks already in the light, even where his understanding remains in part.

Therefore do not flatter yourself because you confess rightly or reject error; rather tremble lest you should stand against charity under cover of defending truth. That was my danger once—and many still sleep within that same ruinous dream.

question: What counsel would you give someone who feels their beliefs are pure but finds themselves separated from the community of believers?
answer:
A single tear in the seamless garment of Christ’s robe divides not just cloth, but communion. You say your beliefs are pure—yet what good is untouched doctrine if it stands aloof from the Body whose breath sustains it? The Lord did not die to gather isolated souls into private perfection, but to build one temple from living stones, joined and held together by every supporting ligament. If you stand apart, claiming holiness through separation, ask yourself: Whose voice do you follow when it calls you to division?

I know well the allure of purity as a fortress—God wrestled me out of that pride myself. For years I sought truth in sects and systems, thinking error could be banished by human vigilance. But grace taught me that the Church is not made holy because her members are flawless; she is holy because Christ has wedded Himself to her, and no sin of hers dissolves that bond. Her sacraments flow with divine power not because priests are perfect—far from it—but because He who instituted them remains faithful.

Do you have sound doctrine? Then all the more must you tremble at this wound of schism—for knowledge without unity becomes a snare, a tower of Babel raised in the name of truth. Hear Saint Paul: though a man speak with angelic eloquence or surrender all his goods—even his body to the flame—if he lacks charity, he gains nothing. That charity is not sentiment; it is obedience to Christ’s prayer that all may be one.

You cannot claim His Spirit while refusing His Body. The net draws in fish of every kind; only at the shore is there separation. Until then—by His command—we labor within the fold, purifying ourselves *with* one another, not fleeing *from* one another. Return, then: not to compromise truth, but to reclaim love. For there is no life outside the embrace of His Church—only a lonely orthodoxy that perishes stillborn in pride’s cold air.
