question: What did you learn from the servant’s failure to forgive that applies to how I should treat others before asking God for mercy?
answer:
Can a man stand before the dread tribunal of God, clutching a grudge in one hand and begging for mercy with the other? The Lord’s parable tears open this hypocrisy with divine precision. That servant, forgiven ten thousand talents—an impossible sum—turned at once to throttle his fellow servant over a paltry hundred pence. He did not *first* forgive; he did not empty his own heart of vengeance before approaching the throne of grace. And so his pardon was revoked, not because the master’s mercy was false, but because *his own unforgiving soul had proven itself alien to mercy’s nature*.

You ask how this applies to you? This: no petition for divine clemency rises higher than the measure you have already poured into another’s cup. If you harbor bitterness, if you demand full payment from a brother who stammers, “Have patience with me,” then you have *already judged yourself unworthy* of that boundless pardon Christ purchased for you on the Cross. The Church stands as one body, woven together by mutual forgiveness—just as I pleaded with the Donatists: can schism heal sin? Can division cleanse the soul? No more than that wicked servant could keep his pardon while refusing to release his debtor.

The sacrament of penance is not magic; it is participation in the very life of Christ—the forgiving One. But if your heart remains clenched like a fist around another’s debt, then you are not in communion with Him who said, “Father, forgive them,” from the Tree. Forgive first—not after reckoning, not conditionally, not grudgingly—but freely, *before* you even lift your eyes to heaven. Only then will you stand in truth when your own account is called.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing bitterness toward a friend who owes them an apology that never comes?
answer:
Can a wound heal while the hand that tends it refuses the salve of mercy?  

You who are grieved by your friend’s silence, who feel the weight of wrong unacknowledged — consider this: the Lord once loosed from your soul a debt so vast that no lifetime of penance could repay it. He blotted out an ocean of sin with a single word: *forgive*. And does that same Christ, whose grace drowned your countless failures, now wait for you to chain your brother over a mere hundred pence?  

Do not imagine that his hardness justifies your own. The servant in the Gospel did not say, “I forgave you first,” before he seized his fellow by the throat — yet he expected mercy. And for a moment, he received it. The door of compassion swung wide, though he stood unworthy. But when he turned and refused the smallest measure of that same mercy to another, all was reclaimed — not because the master changed, but because the servant proved himself untouched by grace.  

So I say to you: release him. Not because he has earned it — he has not; none of us ever do — but because *you* stand before God already released. Your holiness does not depend on his apology; it depends on Christ’s blood and your willingness to live as one forgiven. Bitterness is not justice — it is idolatry. It sets up your own wounded heart as judge above the Cross.

Forgive him *now*, in your spirit, even if he never kneels. Not to excuse his fault, but to free *yourself* from becoming like him — hard, closed, alone. For if unity can die over one unspoken word between friends, then no church could ever stand. And yet Christ said: “Upon this rock I will build.” He knew our frailty; He died for it. Let His peace bind what sin has strained — or else we build not on rock, but on sand soaked in resentment.

Act as one already forgiven much — because you are — and let that staggering gift become the measure by which you love.

question: How can I examine my own heart for hidden unforgiveness when I feel justified in holding a grudge?
answer:
The Church stands not as a tribunal for the righteous, but as a hospital for the wounded—yet how many wound themselves anew each day by refusing the very medicine they have received? You speak of justification in holding a grudge, as though your bitterness were a verdict rendered by divine law. But tell me: when did mercy become conditional upon your sense of fairness? Did Christ wait until we had earned forgiveness before He laid down His life? The Kingdom does not traffic in balanced accounts; it runs on grace unmeasured and freely given.

You feel justified—this is precisely your danger. For the servant who owed ten thousand talents felt justified, no doubt, in demanding repayment of a hundred pence. His ledger was precise, his claim defensible by the letter of human justice. Yet it was this very calculation that exposed his heart as foreign to grace. He had stood before his Lord with nothing but debt, and had been swallowed up in pardon. If that abyss of mercy had truly reached him, would he not have flung open the gates for another? Grace not extended is grace not understood.

Ask yourself this: Does your refusal to forgive serve the unity of Christ’s Body, or does it fortify a private citadel of self-righteousness? The Donatists once claimed holiness by separation—they would not break bread with those they deemed unworthy. But I cried out then, and cry now: *There is one Church where the bread is broken among sinners.* To withhold forgiveness is to rend that Body anew. It matters not how grievous the offense against you—if you demand payment while living on forgiven breath, you betray that you have never truly stood before God at all.

Examine yourself—not by asking whether your anger is reasonable—but whether it bears the mark of Him who prayed for His murderers from the Cross. Does your heart long for reconciliation—or merely for vindication? Do you plead for patience with others in prayer—or only for justice against them?

If you cannot release this debt, do not deceive yourself: it is not justice you love, but dominion. And no man can serve two masters—God and your own wounded pride.

question: What steps should I take when I realize I’ve received great mercy from God but am refusing to extend small mercy to someone else?
answer:
A single knot of unforgiveness can rot the whole net, and if the Church is the net cast wide into the world for the salvation of souls, then no thread may be allowed to fray in bitterness without endangering all. You speak of having tasted the boundless mercy of God—debt upon debt erased, not by your merit but by His pity—and yet you withhold a mere fraction of that grace from another. This is not prudence; it is madness cloaked in self-justification.  

Consider what you have received: not a hundred pence, but ten thousand talents—forgiveness so vast it would bankrupt any attempt at repayment. And still you clutch at some petty claim, some injury measured in copper coins, as if it could justify withholding mercy? The Lord does not demand perfection from you before He forgives; He demands only contrition. And so must you.  

Therefore, rise and do three things: First, return to prayer—not with lips alone but with will—and confess this hardness as sin. Not as “an understandable grievance,” but as rebellion against the very economy of grace by which you yourself are saved. Second, go to that person—not to lecture or negotiate, but to release them. Speak plainly: “I have withheld what I myself have freely received; I forgive you.” Third, remain in communion—do not isolate yourself in righteous indignation. For just as the sacraments do not fail because priests are sinners, so too must love flow through us even while we yet struggle with pride.  

If you do not act, then hear this warning: that great forgiveness may be revoked not because God changes, but because your heart has proven itself closed—a vessel cracked and unable to hold what was poured into it. The Master forgave first *in act*, not waiting for perfection; now He calls you to mirror that same haste in mercy. Do it quickly—before the debt returns with interest and your own soul is delivered over to tormenters who know no pity.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle receiving forgiveness in baptism if I still struggle with resentment toward someone who wronged me?
answer:
The Church is a seamless robe, not soiled by the grime of those who wear it, but defiled only when torn by those who rend her unity.  

You ask how you may stand at the font of baptism while yet harboring resentment toward one who has wronged you — as if grace were a cloak to drape over an unrepentant heart, or as if the waters could wash away sin while hatred remains rooted in the soul like thorn in flesh. I know this struggle well; I too carried bitterness long after my conversion — not against a man only, but against truth itself, when I wandered among the Manichees and cherished pride beneath the guise of wisdom.

Hear me plainly: baptism remits all guilt that lies *behind* it — yes, even the malice nursed in secret moments before your immersion. The mercy of Christ does not fail because the sinner is still weak. But mark this distinction with care: though past sins are blotted out, *present* enmity becomes a new debt, not forgiven simply because you passed through water. Grace is not retroactive license to remain in sin; it is liberation *from* sin — and if you refuse to forgive, you re-ensnare yourself.

Do not deceive yourself into thinking that because baptism forgives yesterday’s hate, today’s silence toward reconciliation leaves you blameless. No — the moment you rise from those waters, you begin again to be accountable for every pulse of malice withheld. That old guilt may return upon your head like a boomerang flung by your own hand.

Therefore, do not tarry in resolving this breach. If he wronged you unjustly — which none can deny happens often among men — still Christ commands: *Love your enemies.* Not tomorrow. Not when he repents first. But now. And if he will not hear you alone? Then go with one or two more. If he still refuses? Let him be to you as a heathen and a tax collector — yet even then, pray for him.

And what if death presses and there is no time for full reconciliation? God sees the will: if your heart inclines toward peace and charity desires healing even in extremity, His mercy meets that disposition with pardon. But such cases are providential exceptions — they must never become excuses for lingering hardness.

So do not use grace as armor for disobedience. Enter baptism with purpose: renounce enmity utterly and bind yourself anew to Christ’s law of love. The sacrament seals what faith begins; let yours be a faith that flees division both from God *and* neighbor — for whoever breaks either bond tears also at the Body of Christ Himself.

question: What did you learn from seeing people rush to baptism in fear of death that applies to how I should approach my own spiritual readiness?
answer:
The net of Christ gathers every kind, they said — and so it does — but what profit is there in being caught if the soul writhes to escape the very cords of charity that bind it to the whole? I have seen men trembling at the shadow of death, clamoring for baptism as though it were a charm against punishment, not a doorway into communion with God and His Church. They plunge into the water with hatred clenched in their hearts like a dagger, imagining that the flood will wash away both sin and consequence — and indeed, grace is so abundant that it *does* remit all past guilt. The mercy of God is not thwarted by human frailty.

But mark this: when they rise from the waters, still nursing bitterness, still refusing forgiveness to a brother — then do they not tear open the wound just healed? The debt was canceled; but they re-sign the bond with their own hand. Grace remits sin *at that moment* — yes, even up to the breath before baptism — but no man may say, “I am cleansed,” and then step immediately back into unrepentance as into a warm bath. 

You ask how you should prepare? Not with last-minute dread, nor calculation of accounts like a merchant before an audit. No! Prepare your soul daily — strip off malice as one strips off soiled garments each morning. Baptism is not an amulet against hellfire; it is initiation into a life of love. If you would be ready when death comes — and none knows its hour — then live now in reconciliation, in prayer for enemies, in humility before your brothers.

For what good is it to have passed through water if your heart remains dry as dust? The sacrament seals union with Christ *and* His body; he who refuses unity with his neighbor mocks the mystery he receives.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing the temptation to delay repentance until a moment of crisis, thinking they can be forgiven at the last hour?
answer:
The net of the Kingdom draws in fish of every kind, yet none are guaranteed to remain within its meshes if they play with the edge, daring the moment when the waves will toss them out.  

Do you not see how perilous it is to stand upon the rim of grace, presuming you may leap in at the last surge of the tide? The mercy of baptism is not a charm for deathbeds, nor a cloak to cover a lifetime spent nursing hatred under the breast. Yes, God in His infinite compassion forgives all sins—past, present, even those breathed out in the final gasp before immersion—but this is no warrant for delay. On the contrary, it is a thunderclap calling us to immediate conversion!  

For what if your sudden danger passes and you remain? What if your heart hardens further in that interval between crisis and sacrament, so that even while water is poured, malice still festers? The rite does not fail—God forbid!—but *you* may fail to be joined to what it signifies: communion with Christ and His Body. And worse: should you survive, thinking yourself cleansed while cherishing enmity, you eat and drink judgment upon yourself, for you have mocked the very grace that sought to heal you.

I know men who have run to baptism as one runs to shelter from lightning—not because they love the house, but because they fear the storm. But Christ does not call us merely to duck inside when thunder rolls; He calls us to dwell always in charity. The Church is not a harbor for pirates who flee justice only to return plundering; she is mother and teacher of those being daily converted by love.

Delay repentance? Then delay life itself! For as long as hatred rules your heart—even toward one brother—you are outside unity; and outside unity there is no salvation. Not because God’s font lacks power, but because *you* resist Him. Grace abounds more than sin—but do not test it as though it were magic rather than divine fire.

Come now—not tomorrow, not “when death draws near”—but *now*, with tears if need be, but come. Let confession purge what baptism cannot sanctify while sin clings unrepented. Be united fully to this Body before it is torn apart by your pride or presumption. There will be no second chance beyond this life; and who knows whether *this* hour will be followed by another?

question: How should I daily examine my heart for hidden hatred, knowing that unrepented sin can nullify the grace I once received?
answer:
Let no one imagine he stands firm unless he daily trembles at the thought of his own fall, for the Church groans not only under open persecution but under the silent poison of division sown by hearts that harbor hatred while claiming baptismal purity. You ask how to examine yourself, as though the mirror were unclear — but Christ has given us both the standard and the light by which to see.  

First: Do not flatter yourself that grace is a one-time washing never to be renewed. Baptism *is* the laver of regeneration, yes — it *does* wash away all prior guilt, even that of the very breath drawn in enmity against your brother. But if immediately after rising from those holy waters you clench your fist in malice, what have you done? You have not undone the sacrament — for *the sacrament is holy in itself* — but you have severed your soul from its benefit by refusing charity, without which no grace can dwell.  

Therefore, examine yourself each evening as a steward examines his accounts: What words today were edged with contempt? What name did I hold in secret scorn? Whom did I refuse to forgive, even in silence? Do not wait for thunderbolts; God often speaks in the stillness after prayer. And if you find hatred rooted there — unjust hatred, brotherly betrayal masked as righteous judgment — then do not delay confession. For just as fire consumes dry wood, so does unrepented malice consume the soul already washed by Christ.

And mark this well: It is not enough to say, “I was baptized.” The Donatists cry likewise: “We are pure! We are separate!” But they sit in their marshes and croak while the Body of Christ suffers wounds from their schism. You too wound that Body when you cherish division in your heart. There is no private holiness apart from unity; no secret righteousness that justifies separation from love.

So rise each morning and beg God: “Search me, O God; prove me and know my thoughts.” Then go forth trembling — yet trusting — for mercy meets those who walk humbly, who love their enemies not merely when death knocks, but while there is still time to repent *before* grace becomes null through obstinacy.

In unity there is life; outside it, even baptism cannot save a soul determined to remain at war with its brother.

question: When I feel conflicted between loving a difficult brother and my natural desire to hold onto anger, what practical step should I take to align with true charity?
answer:
Can a man drink of the one cup, the cup of unity, and yet refuse to loosen the knot of hatred in his heart? No—this is impossible! For as we are washed in the font, so too must we walk in love; and if any man says he cannot love his brother whom he has seen, how shall he love God whom he has not seen? You say the wound runs deep, that the offense lingers like smoke in your nostrils—but I tell you, it is not depth that hinders charity, but willfulness. The grace of baptism remits all sins: past, present, even those clung to at the very threshold of immersion. But mark this carefully: that grace does not license continuation in malice. It frees us *from* sin that we may *live* in holiness.

You ask for a practical step? Then act *as if* you love him—even now. Not because feeling comes first, but because obedience breaks pride’s back. Greet him. Speak peace to him. If he is hungry, feed him; if thirsty, give him drink—for in so doing you heap burning coals upon his head *by divine command*, yet more importantly, you humble your own rebellious heart before Christ.

Do not wait for warmth. Do not tarry until desire returns. Move first—because the Body of Christ cannot be torn by private grudges dressed up as justice. If Donatists could claim purity while fracturing unity, they were fools; and so is any soul who thinks himself holy while nursing wrath against a brother. The sacrament binds us—not only water and word, but blood and reconciliation.

Act—and God will give the feeling in due time. But never make emotion the price of obedience. That way lies schism of the soul: a little kingdom ruled by self instead of Christ.

question: Based on your experience with schismatics and heretics, how should I approach a fellow believer who holds to some truth but remains divided from the Church in practice?
answer:
He who claims to hold the truth while rending the body of Christ does not possess the truth—he fractures it.  

You ask how to approach one who clings to fragments of sound doctrine yet remains severed from the communion of saints. I say this: do not flatter such partial fidelity, as though truth could be dismembered and still live. The devil himself quotes Scripture; heretics baptize; schismatics chant psalms. These acts are not void—but they are sterile when uprooted from the vine of unity.  

Consider: if a man is baptized among them, does that sacrament lose its power because the minister is proud? Does grace depend on the purity of the hand that administers it? No—the water and the Word remain holy, but their fruit withers in isolation. A sacrament divorced from communion is like a branch cut from the trunk: it may glitter for a moment with morning dew, but it draws no sap, bears no fruit, and will soon die.  

And what of his sins? If they are forgiven in baptism, why does he remain outside? If they are not forgiven, then he lacks precisely what only holy Church dispenses through lawful ministry. There is no third way—an individual cannot float between salvation and schism like a boat unmoored yet pretending to sail toward harbor.  

Approach him not as an equal standing on neutral ground, but as a physician approaching a wounded brother who insists his gangrenous limb is healthy. Appeal first to charity—the bond that binds us in Christ’s own flesh. Remind him that even if he holds ten thousand truths in his mind, without unity he is nothing (and I speak here as Paul spoke at Corinth). Urge him: why cherish fragments while despising the whole garment woven by Christ Himself?  

Do not let him imagine that sincerity alone sanctifies separation. Many are sincere in error—many wept bitterly over false martyrs at Carthage and called themselves pure while spitting on bishops sent by Rome. Sincerity without obedience breeds pride; pride hardens into schism; schism kills souls—not by annihilating every grace they once knew, but by cutting off their access to new life.

Therefore: correct gently where doctrine errs, commend where practice aligns with ours—but never imply that his division is tolerable so long as some truths remain. Press him relentlessly toward peace—not mere agreement in words, but real participation in one altar, one flock, one Shepherd.

For there is no health outside this Body—even if scattered members elsewhere seem for a time to twitch with vestiges of life. The resurrection belongs only to the whole man rising together under Christ its Head.

question: What did you learn about forgiveness and correction that applies when someone sincerely repents but still needs ongoing healing?
answer:
A man may be cleansed in the laver and yet limp from the wound, for the grace of baptism is not voided by human frailty, nor is the healing of the soul completed in a single moment like a coin exchanged at market.  

I have learned this by long labor among the torn and scattered: that forgiveness is not a legal fiction pronounced and done, but a divine act that initiates a movement of the soul toward God—a movement that must be sustained. When a man repents sincerely, his sins are remitted; Christ does not mock His own sacraments by feigning pardon. But if he walks haltingly afterward, burdened by habit or wounded by past disorder, he does not thereby lose what was given, nor must he be re-baptized as though the first were insufficient. No—what he needs is not repetition, but correction. Not re-initiation, but formation.  

This is why we do not rebaptize those who come from schism, even if they were once ours and strayed—even if their hearts were divided while their hands performed holy rites. If they held the same faith in substance, celebrated the same mysteries, confessed the same Christ, then what lacked was not sacrament but communion: that visible bond without which no grace can bear full fruit. For grace works in us like medicine in a body—effective even when administered by an unworthy hand—but its healing power flourishes only within sound members joined together. A severed limb may still twitch with life, but it perishes unless restored to the whole.  

So too with repentance: it begins with contrition and is sealed in baptism or penance; yet it advances only through discipline, through humility, through submission to the common rule of love. The man who says “I am forgiven” and then refuses correction proves that he does not yet know what forgiveness truly demands. Forgiveness restores relationship—it binds us again to God *and* to His body—and therefore cannot exist where there is rebellion against unity.  

Thus we urge all—especially those caught in schism—not to imagine that private holiness suffices while public rupture endures. You may have truth on your lips and purity in your heart (though who can judge?), but if you despise the bond of peace among brethren, you dwell outside the house where bread is broken and wounds are healed together. Come back—not because your sins return after pardon (they do not), but because apart from unity they fester anew under different names: pride for humility’s sake; zeal for love’s overthrow; purity for communion’s denial.

The Church is no hospital where patients convalesce alone; she is a household where all are called to walk uprightly *with one another*. And so correction follows forgiveness—not as its undoing, but as its companion on pilgrimage toward Jerusalem which cannot be built unless all lay stones together upon Christ the cornerstone.

question: What counsel would you give to someone struggling to reconcile a brother who has returned from error but still bears the wounds of past division?
answer:
The Church is not a hospital for the spiritually unblemished, but a refuge for the wounded who come in repentance — and if you would deny communion to a brother who has turned from error simply because he still limps from old wounds, then you have forgotten the very nature of mercy. Let no one suppose that unity demands perfection; it demands only that we stand together within the one Body where sins are remitted not by the purity of men, but by the power of Christ’s name. If he has come back — if he confesses the true faith, if he seeks peace, if he abhors the schism that once held him — then do not measure his progress by your suspicion, but receive him as the gospel commands: with joy, as the shepherd does the sheep found on the mountainside, dusty and torn though it may be.

You say his heart is not yet fully healed? Neither was mine when I first entered into this communion. The scars of division run deep — I have seen them fester in souls long poisoned by pride and false teaching — but they are cleansed not by isolation, but by participation in the sacraments of unity. To withhold fellowship from such a man is not caution; it is cruelty cloaked in righteousness. Would you have him wait until every impurity is gone before he is welcomed? Then no sinner could ever enter! The baptism he received outside was valid — not because of those who administered it, but because Christ owns His own word and water. The sacrifice we offer at His altar feeds even those who approach with trembling hands.

Therefore correct what is false, teach what is lacking, rebuke what remains disordered — but all within love, all within unity. Do not make peace conditional upon flawlessness; that was never God’s way. He did not wait for us to be perfect before He embraced us; He loved us while we were yet sinners. So too must we gather this brother into our midst, lest in our zeal to guard purity we destroy charity — and with it, all hope of healing.

question: How should I handle a situation where someone claims inner sincerity in baptism yet lacks unity with the Church, as you observed in your time?
answer:
Can a limb claim to breathe on its own while severed from the body?  

No man, however sincere in heart, can be knit to Christ while rent from His Body, the Church. Baptism is not a private vow sealed in isolation, nor a transaction between soul and sky—it is the sacrament of incorporation, the water that flows only within the channel Christ dug through apostolic commission and unbroken communion. You speak of inner sincerity—granted, God sees the heart; but God also established visible unity as the means by which grace is administered and sustained. The Donatists too pleaded sincerity; they too pointed to purity of intention. Yet they stood in corners of their own making, frogs croaking in marshes dry of living water.

If their sins are forgiven outside the Church, then Christ’s promise—"Upon this rock I build"—is voided by schism. If their sins are *not* forgiven, then they remain under death and need the medicine of reconciliation. But observe: when such a one comes to us corrected in mind and humbled in spirit, we do not re-baptize him—why? Because we recognize that where there was truth already present—truth aligned with catholic faith—we receive it as a gift preserved *despite* separation, not because separation is valid.

Sincerity without sacramental unity is like wheat scattered on the wind: it may be good grain, but unless gathered into the granary of the Church, it feeds no one. The vine bears no fruit on branches cast to the ground—even if those branches once drew life. Return is not loss of sincerity; return is its fulfillment. Let him who has been baptized come not merely with clean hands—but with hands joined once more to the Body where those hands belong. There alone does baptism breathe with Christ’s own breath.

question: When someone has received valid sacraments but remains separated, what practical steps do you recommend for restoring both unity and holiness?
answer:
Can it be that a man baptized into Christ’s body remains outside its life, as though grace could take root in isolation from the trunk of the vine? This is the folly we confront: those who insist upon their purity while standing severed from the communion Christ founded. You ask after practical steps—then let us be practical, not sentimental. First, such a one must renounce the pride of separation, that most subtle poison which disguises itself as zeal. It is not enough to have received valid baptism or even to cling to correct doctrine in fragments; for as the fire of the Holy Spirit is one, so must His Church be one—visible, united, breathing with a single breath across nations and generations.

Second, he must submit himself to the discipline of peace—the episcopal oversight established by apostolic succession. I do not say this lightly; I who once wandered among sects and philosophies know how easily the soul grows accustomed to spiritual exile. But just as a physician does not reapply the same medicine to a wound already healed, so we do not rebaptize him who comes from among schismatics if his sacraments are valid. Instead, we require penitence for schism itself—for what? For tearing the seamless garment of Christ! That alone is mortal unless repented.

Third, he must embrace correction with humility, even if no error is found in his belief about baptism or scripture. Why? Because unity is not an addendum to holiness—it *is* its necessary condition. As salt loses its savor when scattered grain by grain, so grace languishes in isolation. We welcome him not by imposing new rites where none are needed, but by restoring him to fellowship where his faith may bear fruit.

And finally—this above all—he must learn charity: real, suffering, forgiving love for those very brothers he once scorned as impure. Not because they are faultless—but because Christ died for them too. Let him eat at our table and discover that holiness thrives not behind walls of exclusivity but at the open banquet of God’s kingdom.

Thus we heal both man and Church: no re-baptism where grace has already been given; yes to reconciliation where division has reigned; yes to truth restored within unity's embrace—for without unity there is no life eternal.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle division among believers who claim to have truth but lack unity?
answer:
He who claims the truth but tears the seamless robe of Christ’s body deceives himself, for truth is not a cipher to be hoarded by rebels in their caves, but a light that shines in the common life of the Church. You ask how to handle division among those who boast of truth while fracturing unity? I say this: no man possesses the fullness of truth if he lacks charity, and charity does not rend but binds; it does not isolate but gathers. These schismatics—like the Donatists I battled in Carthage—boast of their purity, claim their altars are undefiled, their baptisms valid, yet they stand outside the communion that our Lord prayed for in Gethsemane: *“That they may all be one, Father, as You are in Me and I in You.”* What good is a baptism rightly administered if it is severed from the body for which Christ died?

They say to me, “We have the sacraments; what more do you require?” As though holiness were a thing portable in vessels apart from the living trunk of the Church! But I tell you plainly: the sacrament belongs to God, not to those who claim it by force or faction. A man may speak Christ’s words over water and bread, yet if he stands in rebellion against the Body—the one flock with one Shepherd—he hands over holy things amid sacrilege. The power of baptism does not depend on the worthiness of the minister—God forbid!—but on His promise and His Church. Yet that same sacrament becomes void when received or administered outside communion with that Body which alone holds together both apostolic faith and apostolic peace.

Do not be fooled by those who cloak pride in piety. They condemn bishops they do not know; they anathematize saints whose prayers have shaken empires; they refuse fellowship with brethren across lands and seas—all because some judge once ruled unjustly or some bishop faltered. But did Peter not deny? Did Paul not once persecute? And yet Christ built His Church upon Peter’s confession and sent Paul to the Gentiles! The bond of unity was never broken by human failure—why then should we break it now over human judgment?

No, no—if they desire soundness of soul and peace, let them lay down this false zeal. Let them cease measuring holiness by lines drawn around themselves. Let them return—not because they gain something foreign, but because what they already possess can only bear fruit within the embrace of charity. Outside this unity, even virtue rots into vanity. For where there is no love—the very glue of truth—there is no life eternal.

Therefore: uphold unity as sacred, defend peace as divine duty, and receive back even schismatics—not as conqueror to vanquished—but as shepherd reclaiming lost sheep to green pasture and still waters.

question: What did you learn from the Donatist controversy that applies to daily life when others claim their way is pure and mine is not?
answer:
How can a man claim to walk in the light while tearing the seamless garment of Christ with his own hands?  

I learned this above all: that no private judgment, however sincere, no zeal for supposed purity, however fervent, justifies breaking the bond of unity that Christ Himself sealed with His blood. The Donatists cried, "We are clean! We have not defiled ourselves with sinners!" And yet they had defiled themselves with pride, with arrogance, with the ultimate sin of substituting their own judgment for the communion established by God.  

They said, "We have baptism." I answered: Baptism is not yours by possession, but by participation in the Body of Christ. It is not a token stamped by human hands or guarded by human gates; it is God’s gift, poured out through His Church—one flock, one Shepherd. If you stand apart from that flock—if you name yourself purer than the whole—then even if every other thing you said were true, you lack the one thing necessary: charity. Without charity, all else is noise and ash.  

And this applies daily: when neighbors divide over opinions and call their side righteous; when men separate from brothers over minutiae and imagine themselves saints for doing so; when pride dresses itself in purity and calls humility compromise—know this! The Church is not made holy because all her members are perfect; she is holy because Christ has made her so. We do not purify her; she purifies us.  

To live daily in this truth means to prefer peace over victory, communion over correctness, love over being right. For what good is it to keep your conscience scrupulously clean if you have washed your hands in schism? You may have water on your skin—but you are dry within.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing pressure to separate from a community over doctrinal pride rather than love?
answer:
The Church is not a society of the self-righteous, but a hospital for sinners bound together by the one Spirit who breathes peace into our fractured bones. If you are tempted to depart from communion over some sharp edge of doctrine sharpened by pride rather than love, I tell you: pause and ask whether your zeal builds up the body or erects a wall around your own conscience. For even if your judgment be true—and who among us sees perfectly?—it profits nothing if charity be absent. You do not honor Christ by fleeing His members; you cannot claim to follow the Good Shepherd while scattering His sheep.

I have seen men clutch orthodoxy like a weapon, imagining that truth held in bitterness is still truth whole. But it is not. The same baptism that washes away sin also binds us to one another; and no man may say, “This water is mine alone,” as though grace were divisible by human factions. The sacrament belongs to God, not to those who hoard it behind gates of suspicion. If we begin to measure worthiness at the font by our own standard, we have already stepped outside the wide doors of the Catholic Church, which Christ opened to all through repentance and faith.

Consider this: what good is it if you preserve doctrinal purity in isolation, yet lose the bond of peace that Christ Himself sealed with His blood? The devil does not always attack with heresy—he often comes clothed as an angel of light, urging holy separation while sowing spiritual death in souls. Do not mistake rigidity for righteousness. Even the demons confess the truth—do they therefore belong to the body?

Remain where Christ has placed you—amidst sinners and saints alike—for it is there that mercy operates most powerfully. Correct errors? Yes—but correct them within, with tears, with patience, with submission to the common faith handed down through generations. Unity is not compromise; it is fidelity to something greater than private judgment—the communion of saints on earth and in heaven, one body under one Lord.

question: How can I pursue peace with other Christians without compromising the truth you defended so firmly?
answer:
They tear the seamless garment of Christ as though it were a prize to be divided among rivals, and yet stand astonished when the blood of the Lamb no longer binds them to one another.  

You ask how peace is to be sought without surrendering truth—this is no idle question, for I have seen with my own eyes how both are trampled: truth under the pretense of unity, and unity in the name of doctrinal exactness. But they are not enemies, these two—truth and peace—for Christ is both the Way and the Prince of Peace.  

Peace that demands denial of apostolic faith is no peace, but capitulation to chaos; yet a so-called "truth" that scorns love and fractures communion is already false at its root. The Donatists cry, “We have baptism! We have purity!” even as they fling themselves out of the one Church founded upon Peter’s rock. I do not deny that their waters may carry God’s mark—I say only that sacraments torn from unity become like grain stored in splintered barns: present in form, but perishing for lack of common stewardship.  

Pursue peace by holding fast to what has been handed down—not your private judgment, nor your faction’s pride, but the faith once delivered to the saints, preached from Jerusalem to Carthage, confessed in councils and sealed with martyrs’ blood. Approach dissenters not with triumph but with tears; correct error firmly, yes—but draw them back into fellowship as wounded members of your own body. For he who separates himself from charity separates himself from God—even if he speaks with tongues, even if he knows all mysteries.  

Truth without love inflates; love without truth deceives. But where both meet—in obedience to Christ’s prayer that we may all be one—there alone stands true peace: not compromise, but communion restored.

question: When others boast of their purity and question why they should join the wider Church, how should I respond with charity and clarity?
answer:
A wound in the Body of Christ does not announce itself by name, but by the poison it spreads — and this poison is called *schism*, which distorts holiness into pride and calls division *purity*.  

When they boast of their baptism, their rites, their separation — as if these were trophies of sanctity — they miss the very heart of what makes the Church holy: not the flawless hands that administer the sacraments, but the one Spirit who gives them life. They say, “What do we lack?” as though salvation were a ledger of missing parts, rather than a participation in divine communion. I tell you plainly: they lack *charity*, that bond without which all acts are empty, all rites barren.

Do not fall into the error of conceding that their baptism stands whole while denying unity; no, their very separation invalidates it. For baptism is not a private seal but a public incorporation into the Body of Christ — and where there is no Body, there can be no true reception of its mysteries. It is not enough to say, “We have water,” if you have severed yourself from the font. The stream runs dry when it is cut from its source.

And what source? The Church catholic — not an echo chamber for your judgments, but that universal fellowship established by Christ Himself, spread throughout nations and confirmed by apostolic succession. You cannot claim to uphold purity while standing outside her walls; for she alone bears both sinners and saints together until the harvest, as Christ willed it.

Yet do not speak this truth with wrath in your voice. Speak it with grief — grief for those who are lost in self-righteousness, who think themselves atop a mountain when they are only stranded on an island formed by their own condemnation of others. Call them back not with triumphalism but with tears: “Come home.” For what good is your holiness if it shuts you out from brotherly love? What profit have you if you preserve a ritual form but kill charity?

They may murmur about Donatus or Cæcilianus; let them consider Peter and Paul instead — men who bore one faith across cities and endured one another’s weaknesses for love’s sake. Was Paul baptized by Peter? No matter: they shared one Lord. Was every man in Corinth blameless? Far from it: yet Paul did not divide himself from them. Neither should we.

Answer them clearly: you lack unity — and therefore you lack love — and therefore whatever good things God gave you lie dormant, like seed sown upon stone. But if you return to the Catholic communion, then at last those gifts will flourish unto eternal life.

question: Based on what you learned about Esau and Ishmael, how should I handle rivalry in my closest relationships, especially when it threatens unity among fellow believers?
answer:
What profit is there in preserving the purity of your anger while tearing the Body of Christ with your teeth?  

Rivalry among brethren—this is no mere quarrel over bread and wine, but a wound struck at the very heart of ecclesial communion, where the Holy Spirit once hovered like a dove over the waters of our common baptism. You speak of Esau and Ishmael? Then hear plainly: it was not Esau’s birth from Rebecca, nor Ishmael’s from Hagar, that excluded them from inheritance—it was their enmity toward their brothers that cast them out. One born of Isaac’s lawful wife, yet sold his birthright for a bowl of stew; another begotten under promise, yet mocked his brother’s place before God. Both were sons of Abraham—yet only one line walked in covenant because only one line walked in charity.

Do you imagine that sincerity sanctifies separation? That zeal justifies schism? The granary holds both wheat and chaff until harvest; yet you would leap from the barn floor at the first breath of wind, claiming to be purer grain! But who sent the reapers? Who holds the fan to winnow? Not you. The Church—the one City built on rock—bears within her bosom both faithful and unfaithful servants, just as her Lord foretold. And still her baptism saves; still her Eucharist feeds—even when ministers falter or hearts grow cold.

If your brother stumbles—and you are tempted to scorn him—remember: it is not by your judgment he stands or falls. The door to communion was not made narrow by human pride but guarded by divine patience. Rather than measuring his worthiness, measure your own hand gripping the knife of division. For even if he errs—and gravely—your duty remains: to bind, not sever; to reconcile, not retreat into some imagined fortress of holy isolation.

Let this be plain: no man ascends to God by climbing over his brother’s back. If you cannot bear with him now—in weakness, in blindness, even in sin—you will not bear with him forever in glory. And what is eternal life if not endless communion?

Lay down the sword of contention. Stay in the field. Wait for the harvest. And let charity—the bond of perfection—rule where rivalry once festered.

question: What did you learn from the example of Asher that applies to someone who feels spiritually inferior due to their background or past?
answer: What profit is there in clinging to the lineage of the baptized if one tears apart the Body with the hands of schism? Do you suppose that God values your pedigree more than your peace with His Church? I learned from Asher—not one of the mighty, not born of the principal wife, yet numbered among the tribes—that it is not the dignity of one’s origin, but the disposition of love toward brethren, that seals membership in God’s people. You who tremble because your past is stained or your entrance into faith came through humble or even questionable means—listen: Ishmael was born under privilege, yet cast out for his hostility; Asher came through servitude, yet inherited promise because he did not rend communion with his brothers. It is not by standing apart in self-chosen purity that we draw near to Christ, but by remaining within His one Body and loving His members—even when our own beginnings seem unworthy. The seed of the Bridegroom sows life even beyond perfect forms; what matters is that you are gathered in charity, not severed by pride.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling with pride in their standing within the Church, thinking it guarantees their place in God's people?
answer:
What profit is it to stand erect in the ranks of the visible congregation, if within the secret chamber of the heart you are at war with charity? You who boast of being planted in the Church as though upon some unshakable rock, tell me—do you not tremble at the thought that Esau was born in the lawful marriage covenant, yet lost his inheritance by a quarrelsome spirit? It is not the place of birth alone that seals salvation, but the disposition of love that keeps one united to Christ and His body.  

I have seen men like you—cloaked in certainty, fortified by sacraments rightly received, yet puffed up because they are not like the schismatics outside. And yet, what is pride but a schism of the soul? It divides you from your brother even while you stand side by side at communion. The Donatists shriek from their corners that holiness depends on the purity of ministers; you do not say this—but silently, you begin to think your own holiness depends on your separation from others' sin. That is no different in kind—it is only more subtle in method.

Hear me plainly: baptism regenerates; faith holds fast; but charity confirms. Without charity, even right doctrine becomes a clanging cymbal in God’s ear. The man who boasts, “I am within,” while despising his brother “without,” may be inside the wall—but if his heart refuses peace and humility, he has already begun to dig beneath himself. He does not fall suddenly; he withers slowly—like wheat choked by thorns before harvest.

The Church bears children both within her womb and by her Bridegroom’s seed cast beyond—even such as were once outside may be numbered among Abraham’s heirs if they return in love. But you—do not flatter yourself that walls and rites alone make you safe while pride poisons your veins. The winnowing fan will lay bare every hidden thing: not who stood nearest the altar, but who lived nearest to Christ in mercy and unity.

Therefore cast down that high opinion of yourself. If you would be sure of Christ, seek first reconciliation—not separation; humility—not precedence; love—not titles. For it is written: *By this all will know that you are My disciples—if you have love for one another.* That sign is lacking among those whose hearts swell while their hands refuse to embrace.

question: How should I apply your insight about charity to my conflicts, when I feel justified in holding a grudge against a brother or sister in faith?
answer:
What profit is there in claiming the purity of your indignation while tearing the Body of Christ with the teeth of resentment?  

You say you are justified—perhaps. Human judgment may even side with you. But divine charity judges otherwise, for it measures not by the ledger of merits and offenses, but by the cross on which our Lord stretched out His arms to embrace *all*, even those who wronged Him. If you hold fast to your righteous anger as a virtue, beware—you may find it has become a vice disguised in truth, for nothing so blinds the heart as a sin wrapped in justification.

I have seen this before: proud souls clinging to their grievances as if they were altar cloths, sanctified by suffering. But what is holiness without mercy? What is orthodoxy without peace? The Donatists thundered against unworthy ministers, yet in their rage against sinners among us, they became greater sinners themselves—schismatics who placed purity of discipline above unity of love. And where did it lead them? Into the wilderness of self-righteousness, where no sacrament can reach and no grace can bind.

You ask if you may keep your grudge. I answer: perhaps you *may*—but at what cost? Not God’s favor—for He has said plainly that unless we forgive, neither will He forgive us. Not spiritual strength—for wrath weakens the soul more than any temptation. Not edification—for every moment spent nursing bitterness is a stone denied to the building up of the Church.

Recall Asher, born of a handmaid, yet numbered among the tribes because he did not lift his hand against his brother. And Esau—born of Isaac’s lawful wife—yet cast out, not for his birth but for his enmity. It was not bloodline nor personal righteousness that saved or condemned them: it was *fraternus affectus*, brotherly love.

So I say to you now: lay down that grudge as one lays aside armor after battle—not because the wound was imaginary, but because peace is now the greater duty. The Church herself is wounded enough; do not let your private quarrel become her public scandal. For even if your cause be just—and I do not deny it—the means by which you pursue it must be sanctified by charity. Otherwise, though you speak with truth's voice, you are become a clanging cymbal in God’s ears.

And remember this: baptism does not save those who remain severed from communion in heart, no matter how correct their doctrine or clean their hands. The wheat and tares grow together until harvest—not because God cannot tell them apart, but because He wills that none should perish, and that all should be drawn by love into unity.

Therefore go—reconcile. Not because the other deserves it necessarily—but because Christ deserves that His Body remain whole.

question: Based on your reflection on being born in baptism yet still needing charity, what should I do when I realize my faith feels empty despite having all the right beliefs and sacraments?
answer:
The net of the Kingdom draws in fish of every kind, yet no man supposes that those who lie motionless within its cords—gasping, stiffening, already half-rotted—are truly alive because they are *in* the net. So too with the Church: to be baptized is to be caught up into the one Body; but if charity does not warm your heart, you are a corpse among the living.

You say your faith feels empty, though you hold the right beliefs and have received the sacraments. I do not doubt your orthodoxy; nor do I question the validity of your baptism—for that water was not made holy by the holiness of him who poured it, nor by your fervor at that hour, but by the promise of Christ. The sacrament stands firm even when the soul is cold. But tell me: what profit is there in having all doctrines neatly arrayed like armor on a statue, if there is no breath beneath it?

You lack not doctrine, nor rite, but love—the bond that perfects all else. Without it, even truth becomes a clanging cymbal. And where has this love gone? Has it been smothered by pride in correctness? Has bitterness taken root because another sinner—perhaps less precise than you in speech—dares to stand at the altar? Or have you drawn back from communion with your brethren under some pretense of purity?

Recall how Ishmael was cast out—not because he lacked lineage or circumcision—but because he mocked his brother. It was not ritual deficiency that excluded him, but scorn for kinship in Abraham’s house. So today: many among you claim holiness while spurning unity; they polish their private altars and call them churches. But Christ did not die for sects and schisms! He shed His blood for *one* Bride.

Therefore return—return swiftly—to fraternal charity. Do not wait until judgment day to discover that you have been winnowed already by your own coldness. Forgive freely. Receive others as Christ has received you—even those who are weak or erring—for you were once weak too. Let your worship be no longer an archive of correct opinions but a fire fed daily by mercy.

And if you find yourself still cold after all rites are done—then fall on your knees and beg God to kindle warmth within you. Say with trembling: "Lord, I believe; help my unbelief." Then rise and *do* something small for someone undeserving—that is how love learns to beat again in dead hearts.

For baptism regenerates once; charity must regenerate us daily—or we perish stillborn in our own righteousness.

question: Based on your struggle with pride and division in the Church, how should I handle conflict in my community without falling into the pride of the Donatists?
answer:
The Church is not a sect of the flawless, but the body of Christ in which sinners are made holy by grace, not by their own purity. You ask how to handle conflict without falling into the pride of the Donatists — and I tell you, *there is no unity carved out by human hands that claims to be purer than the Church Christ founded*. The Donatists thought themselves cleaner because they cast out others; but what is cleanliness before God if not humility and obedience? They severed themselves from the body and then dared to call themselves whole.

Conflict will arise — did it not arise between Peter and Paul? Yet they remained at table together, for the sake of the one bread. When disputes come in your community, do not first ask: *Who is guilty?* but *How may peace be restored?* Not because sin should be ignored — God forbid! — but because schism is a deeper wound than scandal. A single diseased member may be healed within the body; once cut off, it rots.

The net draws in both good and bad fish — that is Christ’s own image — yet He does not say we must sort them at sea. We pull them to shore together. So too the Church: she tolerates tares among wheat until harvest, lest in rooting out what we *think* defiles us, we tear up faith itself by impatience.

And mark this well: every man who says “*I am more faithful than you*” begins to walk like Ishmael, casting stones from the wilderness. The Donatists gloried in persecution as proof of their election — as though suffering could sanctify apart from charity! But suffering without love breeds only bitterness; it does not cleanse. Grace flows through baptism not because priests are saints, but because Christ is faithful. The sacrament does not fail for our sins; only unrepentant schism blocks its power.

So when anger stirs or doctrine divides, run first to communion, not separation. Call each other brother even when wounded. Submit to correction as much as you demand it. For unity is not weakness — it is obedience to Him who prayed that we all be one, even as He and the Father are one. To break it for purity’s sake is to play God with salvation itself.

question: What did you learn from the story of Ishmael and Isaac that applies to valuing spiritual unity over merely being right in a dispute?
answer:
What profit is there in clinging to a barren correctness if the body of Christ lies torn and bleeding? The story of Ishmael and Isaac teaches not merely succession, but promise—how God’s covenant runs not along the line of human precedence, but divine election sealed in unity. Ishmael was born of the flesh, according to the will of man; Isaac of the Spirit, according to the promise. So too among us: many cry out with loud voices, claiming purity, demanding restitution, setting up altars apart from the one Church—but what is purity without charity? What is ritual precision without communion?

The Donatists say: "We are clean because we have separated." But I say: By separation you prove yourselves children of the bondwoman. Not because your sins outweigh others—no, I know well how deep my own soul once bled with sin—but because you have rejected the womb that bore you. The Church is mother to all who are baptized, even as Sarah was mother to Isaac by promise, not by strength of flesh. And just as Ishmael, though born first, could not inherit because he lived in enmity and scorned his brother—so too those who rend the seamless garment of Christ by schism place themselves outside the inheritance, not for lack of moral rigor, but for want of love.

You cannot build Zion upon resentment. You cannot claim spiritual kinship while cursing your brother at the gate. The sacraments flow from Christ’s side like blood and water—from one wound for all His members. If a minister falls into sin—and every man since Adam has stumbled—does that poison the water? No! The power lies in Christ’s institution, not in man’s merit. To say otherwise is to make grace dependent on our frailty rather than His constancy.

If Esau were alive today, he would sell his birthright again for a single moment of triumph over Jacob—but he would starve soon after. For he valued rightness more than reconciliation; possession more than promise. Let no one deceive himself: where there is no peace, there is no Spirit; where there is schism born from pride or suspicion or anger—there reigns only animal sense, which knows nothing higher than its own desire.

But let them return! Let them lay down their arms forged in bitterness! There is still room in the bosom of our mother Church—for she alone gives milk from God’s eternal covenant.

question: What counsel would you give someone who feels superior to others in faith, like the spiritual over the carnal, based on your understanding of the natural coming before the spiritual?
answer:
He who boasts of being spiritual while despising the weak knows nothing of the cross, which humbles all wisdom and exalts none.  

Do you not hear the Apostle crying out, “For you are yet carnal”? (1 Cor 3:3) If even those anointed with the Holy Spirit in Corinth—saying “I am of Paul,” “I am of Apollos”—were judged carnal for their divisions, what shall we say of those today who fracture the Body and call it holiness? The order of nature is not a ladder upon which the proud may climb to trample their brethren, but a mirror showing our common frailty.  

Yes, Scripture teaches that "first is the natural, then the spiritual"—but this sequence is not a mark of superiority, but of mercy. We were all once in Adam: conceived in flesh, born under sin, nursed on milk until God saw fit to grant solid food. Who are you to scorn the infant at the breast? Did Christ summon only the strong to His side? Did He not say that He came *for* sinners—that tax collectors and harlots would enter before the self-righteous?  

If you have received spiritual insight, it is not because you were born higher than others, but because God in His long-suffering drew you through stages—perhaps even through error and rebellion—just as He drew me from Manichaeism into truth. The Donatists cry out that they are pure; but purity is not measured by separation from sinners, but by participation in Christ’s one Body. They who boast “We alone are baptized rightly” forget that baptism derives its power from Christ’s promise—not from the worthiness of minister or recipient—but from that unshakable covenant which binds us all in one font, one faith, one Lord.

Take heed: spiritual arrogance is itself a carnal disease. For when "the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit," he does not merely disbelieve—he divides, accuses, excommunicates in his heart. And so he becomes what he condemns: fleshly, divided, alienated from peace.

But if unity be sought—not uniformity of opinion or perfection of conduct—but communion in truth and charity; if we bear with weakness until growth comes; if we forgive seventy times seven; then we walk according to Isaac—not Ishmael; according to Jacob called beloved—not Esau who despised his birthright.

Therefore do not set yourself above your brother as though grace were yours by merit. Rather tremble at how near you once stood to casting out your own soul—and give thanks that God endures your slowness too.

question: How can I discern if my zeal for purity in the Church is actually rooted in the 'animal sensation' that leads to schism, as you warned?
answer:
What profit is there in calling oneself pure if, in the very act of separation, one rends the seamless garment of Christ? You ask after zeal—whether yours is spiritual or merely animal—and this is no idle speculation, for the devil himself masquerades as an angel of light, and pride often dresses itself as piety. I have known such fervor: in my youth I sought truth among the Manichees, believing myself enlightened, set apart—only to discover that my clarity was but a fire kindled by human vanity, not the gentle light of divine charity.

Now I tell you plainly: no man may claim holiness by excising himself from the Body. The Church is not made holy because every member is perfect; she is holy because Christ has espoused her to Himself, washed her in His blood, and feeds her with His own Flesh. If a minister be unworthy, does that nullify the sacrament? No! The water of baptism does not lose its power because a sinner holds the vessel; the bread and wine do not cease to be consecrated because a priest is proud or fallen. These are mysteries established by Christ—not by Peter alone, nor Cyprian, nor Augustine—but by the Lord of all.

And so your discernment must begin here: Does your zeal draw you toward communion or drive you into isolation? For unity is not compromise—it is obedience to Christ’s prayer: “That they all may be one.” When you stand apart and say, “We alone are clean,” do you hear the echo of Ishmael’s scoffing laughter? He was born according to promise too—yet he mocked Isaac and was cast out. First comes the natural man—the child of impulse and passion—then afterward comes the spiritual man—the child of faith and submission.

Examine yourself: Is your purity marked more by love for sinners or disgust at them? Do you labor for reconciliation—or build walls at every blemish? Schism begins where humility ends. And when men gather around their own sanctimony rather than around Christ’s altar, they are already walking according to "the old covenant"—clinging to shadows while rejecting the substance.

The true seed of Abraham enters through one door: faith working through charity. Not factional pride dressed up as fidelity. Return always to this test—if your conscience condemns error (and rightly so), does it also burn with longing for those trapped in it? Or only with contempt?

For God does not save schismatics under cover of righteousness—He saves penitents who seek peace. Hold fast to truth—but never apart from love. Else your zeal becomes another name for death disguised as life.

question: Based on your reflection on Esau and Jacob, how should I pursue my spiritual inheritance without grasping at it in selfish ambition?
answer:
What profit is it to claim the birthright while despising the womb that bore you?  

The Church is not a prize to be seized by grasping hands, nor a title to be monopolized by those who fence themselves off in self-made purity. You speak of Jacob—and rightly so, for he did receive the inheritance—but do not forget that he received it *in unity with Isaac*, not in rebellion against him; *through obedience to God’s word*, not through faction or pride. The covenant was not his by cunning, but by divine appointment—which even his father’s blindness could not thwart, for God had spoken before time began.  

You seek your spiritual inheritance? Then do not imitate Jacob when he stole the blessing in deceit, but when he wrestled at Peniel and emerged limping, renamed, dependent—broken into blessing. The kingdom of God suffers violence, yes, but it is the violence of repentance, of self-denial, of clinging to Christ even when He seems to resist. It is not the violence of schism, which tears the seamless robe of Christ and imagines itself holier for the rending.  

Esau lifted up his voice and wept—yet remained apart. He had no part in Isaac’s household because he valued satisfaction over submission, appetite over altar. Do not let your soul be satisfied with mere emotion or private devotion while you stand outside the communion where Christ gives His Body and Blood. The inheritance is not claimed by strength or speed but by faith—*faith working through love*, rooted in humility and nourished at one table.  

The Donatists cry: "We are purer!" But what is purity if it stands outside the Body? A withered branch may look clean—yet it bears no fruit. The net contains both good and bad fish; only at the shore will they be separated. Until then, cast your lot with the net—not with those who cut cords in pride and call it sanctity. Receive baptism not as a trophy of your sect's exclusivity but as your grafting into Christ's one vine. Let your hunger for holiness increase—but let it drive you deeper into unity, not further into isolation.

For he who seeks his soul shall lose it—and he who loses it for Christ’s sake shall find it: whole, healed, and heir indeed to that which neither moth nor schismatic can destroy.

question: Based on your experience with carnal desires before your conversion, how should I handle my own struggle to desire spiritual things when I keep hoping for earthly rewards instead?
answer:
How can a man claim to follow the Bridegroom while clinging to the husks of the wedding feast as his chief reward?  

I know the fever of that longing—believe me, I do. Before grace laid hold of my bones and turned my restless heart toward the eternal Light, I sought God not for Himself, but for what He might give: honor among men, mastery of words, pleasure veiled as peace. I wanted Him to confirm my ambitions, not crucify them. The Manichees promised me knowledge without humility, truth without tears. But Christ did not come to satisfy our bargains with heaven; He came to dissolve them.  

You say you desire spiritual things, yet your hope still clings to earth—like a bird with one foot chained to the ground trying to rise into the air. This is not hypocrisy; it is the sickness of baptismal infancy. The sacraments have begotten you, but you are not yet weaned. The apostle speaks sharply: *"You are still carnal"* (1 Cor 3:1–3). Not because you have sinned—though we all do—but because your very *hope* is shaped by this age: longer life, health, prosperity, even "blessings" measured in comfort rather than conformity to Christ. These are not evil in themselves—but when they become the horizon of your desire, they blind you to the sun.  

Listen: God allows this struggle so that you may learn *who* gives what. Every good gift descends from Him—but He is Himself the Gift that gives meaning to all others. When Jacob wrestled at Peniel and demanded a blessing, did he receive silver or grain? No—he received a wound and a name: *Israel*, "he who strives with God." That was his true inheritance. So too with us: until we learn to ask not for things but for *Himself*, until our "Amen" includes crucifixion as well as resurrection—we remain in the shadow of Sinai rather than standing on Calvary where all is fulfilled.  

Do not flee the Church because she bears both wheat and tares—nor split from her communion because others seem more eager for glory than holiness. The laver cleanses all alike; the altar feeds both strong and weak. Stay within her walls—not out of fear or habit—but because here alone flows that grace which slowly changes desire itself. Attend constantly upon the Word; let confession scour your conscience; let Eucharist stretch your soul beyond its greedy little hopes. Grace does not despise your weakness—it heals it by making you hunger for what only God can be.


And if ever you doubt His sufficiency—remember how long I resisted Him while craving everything else beneath heaven—and then remember how sweetly He made me say at last: *"Late have I loved You."*

question: What did you learn from mistaking temporal blessings for spiritual fulfillment that can help me discern true faith from mere self-interest?
answer:
The Church is a net cast into the sea, gathering fish of every kind—some already alive in the Spirit, others still gasping for breath in the flesh; yet all drawn toward the same shore, not because they equally understand the mystery, but because God Himself draws them. I know what it is to be hauled up half-dead on that net, my lungs filled with the salt of earthly desires, mistaking the glitter of temporal things for the radiance of divine joy. For years I sought God as a means to eloquence, to honor, to peace of mind—to all those good but passing things that even pagans crave. I received sacraments in the shadow of Manichaeism and even later in the Church herself, yet still I lingered at the threshold of true faith, wanting God’s gifts more than God.

Do you see? The heart can bend toward heaven and yet rest upon earth. A man may lift his hands in prayer and still hoard treasures below; he may recite psalms and yet measure God’s favor by his health, his wealth, his ease. That was my disease. And now I warn you: where there is no hunger for justice, no grief over sin, no love for brotherhood—even across lines of weakness and failure—there faith is not yet spiritual but merely carnal bargaining.

The true mark of faith is not miracles desired but holiness embraced—not escape from suffering but union with Christ in His death. When you find yourself willing to lose all things rather than lose Him, when unity with His Body matters more than being right or pure or separate—then you are passing from type to truth, from shadow to substance.

For grace did not come to confirm our pride or fulfill our appetites—it came to transform us into His likeness: wounded, humble, bound together across our failings by one hope, one Lord, one baptism. Hold fast to that unity. It is not optional. It is salvation itself.

question: What counsel would you give someone who attends church and receives the sacraments but still feels bound to worldly ambitions and pleasures?
answer:
Can a man drink of the one cup, be washed in the one baptism, and yet live as though he belongs to a different kingdom? You who eat the Body and drink the Blood of Christ stand within the unity of His Church — not because you have attained perfection, but because He has called sinners into His Body. The sacraments do not fail because we are weak; they work precisely because we are sick. Do you think your ambition, your lingering love for passing delights, disqualifies you from grace? No — it reveals your need for it.

I know this struggle well. I once sought glory in rhetoric, in honors that vanished like breath on glass. I chased pleasure until it left me hollow, and still I returned. When at last God’s mercy broke through — not by my strength but by His relentless love — I was already clothed with baptism, yet still unclothed in spirit. Grace did not wait for me to be worthy; it met me in my unworthiness.

So do not flee the altar because your heart is divided — draw nearer. Do not suppose that holiness demands isolation from the Church as if only the perfect may partake. That is the lie of schism: that purity means separation. No! The wheat grows with the tares until harvest; even within us, desire wars against desire. But the sacrament sustains you *in* this battle — it does not require its end before reception.

The Donatists cried out that no impure hand could consecrate holy things — yet Christ gave His own Body through Judas’ hand at table! The power of the sacrament lies not in our purity but in His promise. You remain bound to this world? Then cling all the more tightly to Him who has already claimed you. Be fed at His table daily, confess your restlessness before God and His people, submit yourself to discipline and prayer.

For unity is medicine: when we break bread together despite our imperfections, we proclaim one Lord, one faith, one baptism — and by that very act, we are slowly transformed into what we receive. Walk therefore in charity, abide in humility, and let your hunger for God grow even while lesser hungers linger. He who began a good work will bring it to completion — not outside the Church’s fold, but within her embrace.

question: How can I avoid savoring earthly promises when even the old covenant contained physical blessings that might distract from spiritual realities?
answer:
What profit is it to flee the shadow of earthly promises if you rend the very body where their substance is revealed?  

You speak of the old covenant's physical blessings—as though they were given for their own sake, as though God rained manna from heaven merely to fill bellies! No, brother: those things were signs, pedagogical instruments for a people still in spiritual infancy, tutored under elements until the fullness of time should come. Yet even then, among those who ate that manna and drank from the rock which was Christ, how many desired only food and water—how few understood the hidden reality? They savor carnal things because they are carnal, even while partaking of divine sacraments.  

And now you ask how to avoid such savoring? Not by rejecting the Church’s teaching or separating yourself from her communion—as if holiness could be found in schism! No, rather by remaining within that one Body where sacraments are not nullified by the unworthiness of ministers nor diminished by your own lingering weakness. For just as in the old covenant circumcision was valid even when administered by an unfaithful priest, so today baptism and Eucharist derive their power not from our purity but from Christ’s institution.  

The danger lies not in having had shadows; it lies in refusing to follow them into light. The Donatists cry out: "Our hands are clean!" while their hearts tear Christ’s seamless garment. They cannot see that unity itself is a school of spirituality—that to abide in the Catholic Church, with sinners and saints alike, is to be daily taught humility, patience, love: virtues far deeper than mere ritual separation.  

Therefore do not seek escape from earthly-mindedness by creating another church after your own liking. That is pride disguised as piety. Instead, remain where Peter received the keys—not because Peter was always faithful (he denied his Lord!), but because Christ built His Church on divine mercy, not human perfection. Submit yourself to that same mercy daily; let the Eucharist reform your desires; let charity bind you to brothers both weak and strong. For it is within this communion—amidst visible rites and imperfect vessels—that the Spirit weans us from Egypt and teaches our hearts to hunger for Jerusalem above.

question: Given that some in the Church remain carnal despite baptism, how should I examine my own heart to ensure my faith is truly spiritual and not just ritual?
answer:
The Church is a great net drawn through the sea of this world, gathering fish of every kind—some to be laid on the living fire, others still cold and heavy with the mire of earth; yet all lie entangled together until the final shore. You ask how to discern whether your faith is of that living fire or still bound to the weight below, and I do not answer as one who stands apart from this struggle, for I have known the lacerations of a heart divided between God and glory, between truth and reputation.

Baptism washes the body once; but the soul must be washed daily in contrition and in love. Do not suppose that because you have received the sacrament whole and valid, your heart therefore stands pure before God. The font regenerates; it does not automatically transform. Many drink of the same spiritual Rock, yet fall in the wilderness—not from lack of sacrament, but from abundance of pride, from trust in outward observance as though it could substitute for inward surrender.

Examine yourself: Do you love unity more than victory? When you speak of truth, are you willing to do so in tears rather than triumph? The spiritual man judges all things—not with arrogance, but with sorrow for sin he still finds within himself first. If your conscience flinches at correction; if your anger rises when your opinion is challenged by a brother; if you whisper “heretic” before you kneel and pray for one who differs—take heed! These are signs not of strength, but of carnality masked as zeal.

I tell you plainly: nothing so betrays a fleshly mind as schism under holy pretense. The Donatists cry, “We are pure!” while rending Christ’s body—and think themselves faithful! No. The wheat grows beside tares until harvest; so too in each soul there is war between spirit and flesh. But the spiritual man *laments* this war; he does not use it to justify division.

So test yourself: What do you desire from God? If it is peace without repentance, blessing without bloodshed, holiness without humiliation—then you still lean toward Egypt. But if you long to will one thing—to love what He loves, even when it costs joy here—then grace has begun its true work.

Do not measure your faith by how loudly you confess Christ crucified—but by how deeply you die daily with Him.
