question: Looking back on your battles with the Donatists, what would you tell someone who doubts whether unity is worth the cost of humility?
answer:
Can a man cut off his hand and still claim to grasp the bread of life? The body of Christ is not divisible at the whim of wounded pride or sour righteousness. I have seen too many, like the Donatists, who wrap themselves in their own purity as though it were a seamless robe, yet tear the true garment of Christ with every step they take away from communion. They say: “We are clean, therefore we separate.” But I say: Who told you that holiness lies in isolation? The wheat grows among the tares until the harvest; the net gathers every kind of fish—did the Lord command us to split the field, to divide the net? No. He said: *“Feed my sheep.”* Not: “Sort them first.”

They built their altars upon resentment, mistaking schism for sanctity. They claimed to guard purity while spilling the blood of unity—the very unity Christ prayed for in tears before His Passion. And what did they gain? A name, a narrow sect, a barren hill where no sacrament could truly flourish apart from the trunk of the Church. But grace does not reside in the worthiness of him who gives it; it flows from Christ Himself—the priest may be unworthy, yet the mystery remains holy.

Humility is not a cost—it is the door. To humble oneself and return is not defeat; it is resurrection from spiritual death. For even if one has been wronged a thousand times by false brethren, he does not answer betrayal with schism—because schism kills more than betrayal ever could. One sin wounds; division drowns.

I once wandered far myself—in philosophy, in pride—before grace broke me open. So I know: better a bruised heart within than a proud soul without. There is no salvation outside that one House where God has placed His Name—not because we are flawless, but because He is present in His Body entire, binding us all—shepherd and sheep alike—to His Cross and His Kingdom which shall never be divided.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you saw those you loved cling to their barren fragments instead of the living root?
answer:
How can a branch claim to bear fruit while gnawing through the very vine that feeds it? I have seen men, once my brothers in baptism, cling to shards of communion as though broken glass could reflect the sun as well as the whole mirror. They called themselves pure, yet their purity was sterile—a desert they mistook for holiness. The sorrow that gripped me was not mere disappointment, but the agony of one who had himself wandered far, who knew the seduction of pride disguised as zeal. I had tasted the emptiness of division when I lived apart from truth; to see others entrench themselves in it by choice—armed with scriptures they misread and saints they misused—was to watch lambs lock themselves outside the fold and call the night their shepherd.

They said, “We are the true Church,” while fracturing into ever smaller sects, each accusing the others of corruption. But where is this unity they claim to guard if their own ranks splinter like dried clay? If Donatus were Christ’s chosen vine, then why do his branches wither outside the world’s wide embrace of apostolic faith? No—holiness is not hoarded in corners. The sacraments do not depend on the worthiness of priests, else they would fail every day, for all of us are sinners. Christ is their power, not man’s morality.

And so my grief was pastoral: not anger at enemies, but anguish over lost limbs of my own body. For every soul severed from Catholic unity cuts into Christ Himself—the Head cannot thrive while His members lie scattered and bleeding in isolation. I pleaded with them: return not to me, nor to any bishop, but to that one Table where Bread comes down from heaven—not from men’s quarrels—to feed a starving world.

question: How did your longing for unity shape your prayer life in the quiet hours when the weight of division pressed upon you?
answer:
The net that Christ cast into the sea of this world was not torn by the weight of sinners within it, but is rent now by those who refuse to let it gather all whom the Father draws. When the night wore long and the house was still, I would rise from my pallet and go to the oratory, not with boldness, but with groaning—groaning not for my own sins only, though they were many, but for this wound in the Body: that men who called upon Christ’s name would dare to say, "Here is the Church," and shut out half the baptized world.

I stretched out my hands not toward schismatics’ altars—God knows I never sought their communion—but toward that one altar laid deep in eternity, where Christ Himself ministers, and where bread is broken not among factions but in charity uncorrupted. How could I pray with peace when brothers stood on opposite banks shouting over a river of pride? I knew what it was to be scattered once—before grace found me—and now, seeing others choose exile from unity as if it were virtue, my soul burned.

In those hours I pleaded: Lord, make them see that holiness does not reside in human purity but in divine institution; that a sacrament holds its power even when a sinner’s hand administers it; that to divide is not to purify but to dismantle. O God, I cried, let them return—not to Donatus’ crumbling wall—but to Your eternal foundation, where Peter’s confession still stands and no storm has shaken it. For every prayer for personal pardon became entangled with this greater plea: *Ut unum sint*, Lord—that they may be one even as You and the Father are one. And if my tears watered that seed even once—let heaven bear witness.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on the bishops of earlier ages who doubted and disputed, yet kept the peace of the Church?
answer:
"Whoever is not against us is for us"—how often these words of the Lord have echoed in my soul when reflecting on those ancient bishops who, though divided in opinion, remained united in charity. I felt both sorrow and hope: sorrow, because even the wisest among us stumbled in the mist of uncertainty, arguing over discipline while schism loomed like a wolf at the fold; yet hope—deep and abiding—because they refused to break the bond of peace. They disputed, yes; councils issued differing decrees; practices varied from province to province. But not one lifted a hand to rend the seamless garment of Christ.  

I tell you this plainly: their restraint was no small thing. It was the Holy Spirit holding back the storm. For had they treated difference as heresy, and doubt as schism, where would we be? The Church would have splintered long before Donatus ever raised his voice. These men knew—some by grace more than learning—that unity is not forged by uniformity of judgment, but by communion in baptism and altar. They allowed room for weakness; they bore with one another in love. And so the body survived its own growing pains.

Now look at what has come: we have clarity where once there was dispute, consensus where there were councils at odds. Not because any man conquered another, but because truth emerged through prayerful endurance within unity—not from without it. The medicine of mercy prevailed over the sword of condemnation. That is why I stand firm now: no schismatic can claim he heals wounds while tearing open Christ’s body—and no council’s ancient hesitation justifies today’s rebellion against what God has made clear.

question: How did the memory of the Donatus schism change how you understood God’s presence in a divided body?
answer:
A net drawn through the sea gathers every kind of fish, yet remains one net—so too the Church, catholic and undivided in purpose, holds within her fold both the strong and the weak, the faithful and the wayward, but never permits a rent to be called holiness.

The Donatist contention—that purity is found in separation, that holiness is measured by the moral state of the minister—this I once thought plausible. Indeed, when I first encountered their rigor, I mistook it for virtue. But God in His mercy exposed the poison beneath that pride. They said: “We are clean because we stand apart.” Yet Christ did not pray for a church of spotless priests but for one Body united in truth and charity. 

I saw then—and see more clearly now—that God does not abandon His sacraments to human frailty. When a priest is a sinner, does the water cease to cleanse? Does Christ recede from His own promise at the hands of an unworthy steward? Let it never be! The power lies not in the man who baptizes but in Him who instituted Baptism. The Donatists made an idol of human purity and broke the Body of Christ upon its altar.

Yes, sin wounds unity—but schism severs it with deliberate violence. And what grieved me most was not their error alone, but how they deceived sheep with a mask of sanctity, calling division fidelity. They scattered where Christ commanded gathering; they excommunicated where He commanded communion.

But from this wound came deeper light: that God’s presence is not hostage to human perfection. The Church remains His Bride even when soiled by our failings—because she lives by His Spirit, not by our merit. Unity is not optional ornament; it is essential to our nature as members of Christ’s Body. To forsake it—even for what seems righteousness—is to walk in darkness clutching a false lamp.

question: Looking back on the moment you turned to the Gospel for clarity, what would you tell someone who doubts whether unity can ever rise from confusion?
answer:
Can a net hold fish if every knot claims to be the whole? No, brother, no—scattered threads make no vessel, but a mockery of one. I have seen it with my own eyes: men rending the seamless garment of Christ over altars of pride, calling their faction *holiness*, their isolation *purity*. But when I turned back to the Gospel—not to the whispers of councils alone, nor to the echoes of ancient disputes, but to the living voice of Christ—I found there no sanction for this sundering.

I tell you now what thundered in my soul when Carthage burned with schism: unity is not an ornament of the Church—it is her life. The Lord said, “He who is not against us is for us,” and yet He also said, “He who does not gather with Me scatters.” Do you see the balance? Grace extends beyond our walls; but salvation cannot dwell where communion is spurned. There was a time when even bishops debated fiercely in good conscience whether baptism outside the Church could save—until at last truth rose like the sun from a council of all Christendom. Not by human wit, but by divine light.

And what was confirmed? That sacraments do not depend on the worthiness of men, but on the holiness of Christ who instituted them. A priest may be corrupt; let him answer for that before God. But the water still cleanses. The bread still feeds. The Spirit still seals.

So yes—out of confusion can rise unity, because God is stronger than our errors. He used my own long wanderings—from Manichæan fables to pagan rhetoric—to teach me this: every wound caused by division must be healed by mother Church’s medicine—repentance, return, reconciliation—not by lashing out in righteous rage. I wept for Donatus and his followers not because they were evil, but because they mistook rigidity for righteousness and isolation for innocence.

The Body has one Head; let no member say to another: “I have no need of you.” Even from chaos grace can build order—if hearts are humble enough to listen to Him who prayed that we all may be one.

question: What ache did you carry when you considered how long the Church wandered with varying statutes before finding unity?
answer:
Can it be that the Body of Christ, one in the Gospel, was left so long in confusion that truth itself had no fixed dwelling? No — God did not abandon His Church to perpetual uncertainty, nor did He will that the pillar and ground of truth should sway with every wind of regional decree. I tell you plainly: those early disagreements among bishops, even men of weight and charity, were not signs of divine absence, but trials permitted for the sake of a clearer manifestation of unity.  

I have felt the ache you speak of — not as one who doubts the faith once delivered, but as a shepherd who sees how slowly even holy men arrive at full discernment. For years I myself wandered — first under Manichaeus, then in the schools of rhetoric — chasing coherence in broken vessels. So I know what it is to long for clarity where there seems only contradiction. And yes, there was sorrow in knowing that councils once ruled at variance: this province receiving one practice, that another rejecting it; some lenient toward lapsed clergy, others rigid. But this sorrow must be offered to God — for from such struggles came greater illumination.

Do you suppose Christ wept less over these divisions than we do? He foresaw them. And yet He prayed not only for unity — He established means for it: one baptism, one Spirit, one hope. The very fact that we now see clearly what was once debated proves not chaos, but growth under His hand. The medicine was always present: Scripture rightly interpreted in communion with the whole Church; tradition sifted by prayerful concord; peace preserved even amid honest dispute.

The Donatists cry out against sinners in clerical robes — but who among us is clean? If holiness were the measure of valid sacraments, who could dare receive the Eucharist? Their logic fractures Christ’s seamless garment into rags judged by man’s pride. But God has not left His Church defenseless against such reasoning. From local confusion arose universal judgment; from scattered statutes came definitive truth through a Council gathered from all corners of the earth — *the whole world* speaking with one voice.

That is why I say: what was once disputed is now settled not by human whim, but by divine order unfolding through time. The ache you feel — I bore it too — but it has birthed something far greater than mere uniformity: a unity forged in fire, tested by schism, and confirmed by charity stronger than division.

question: How did your own experience of wandering shape the way you now see schism as a wound to be healed, not a crime to be punished?
answer:
What profit is it to purge the altar of others’ sins while leaving one’s own heart in rebellion against charity? I speak not as one who has never strayed, but as one who knows the bitter path of separation—not only from the Church, but from truth itself. Once I bore the name of Manichaean, clothed in a sect that promised wisdom and light, yet delivered only chains of pride and darkness. I was a wanderer not by circumstance, but by choice—a thief stealing my own soul from the house of God.

And because I have felt that rupture within my own flesh and spirit, I cannot regard schism as mere treason against discipline. It is worse: it is a mortal wound in the Body of Christ. Not because those who depart are necessarily reprobate—God knows their hearts—but because they tear from themselves the medicine of unity, which is itself a gift of grace. The Donatists cry out for purity, yet in their zeal to wash the Church with thunder and lightning, they forget that she grows like wheat among tares until the harvest. They would uproot today what God commands us to endure till judgment.

I too once sought refuge in separatism—in sects that flattered my intellect and excused my vice. Grace did not meet me in some pristine communion untouched by sinners; no, it overtook me in sackcloth and ashes, amid a Church full of weakness and mercy. There I learned: holiness is not measured by isolation from sinners, but by participation in Christ through His sacraments—even when administered by unworthy hands.

Therefore I say: let no man treat schism as a badge of honor or purity. It is sickness needing cure—not with scorn or excommunication as first resort—but with tears, with truth, and with unyielding summons to return to the one table where Christ Himself feeds His flock. For he who separates himself from unity does not ascend higher—he falls away from life itself.

question: What were you feeling when you wrestled with the tension between unity and correction in the Church, especially regarding those who acted in Christ's name outside your fellowship?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still claim to live by its pulse? This is the fatal illusion of those who, though they invoke Christ’s name, stand apart from His Body, the Church.  

I tell you plainly: my soul burned with anguish—yes, and still burns—when I see men performing works that bear the mark of divine power, yet who position themselves outside the communion of saints. Not because I doubt the power of Christ to work beyond our borders—for He is Lord of all—but because I know how easily pride cloaks itself in zeal, how swiftly a man may mistake his own schism for sanctity.  

When I myself was entangled in Manichaeism, did I not also believe I held truth apart from the Church? And was I not blind? It was grace—unmerited, insistent grace—that drew me back to the Catholic unity where truth abides. Therefore I cannot lightly regard those who say, “We cast out devils in Christ’s name,” while refusing to gather where Christ has commanded His people to assemble.  

Christ said, “He that is not with me is against me.” Let no one twist this. But He also said of him who cast out demons yet followed not the apostles: “Forbid him not.” Why? Not because division is tolerable, but because even a disordered attachment to Christ contains a seed that grace may use. The man was not yet fully healed—but he was not beyond healing. The Church does not amputate at first sign of infection; she applies medicine.

But let there be no mistake: what our Lord tolerated temporarily in an individual acting in partial communion, He does not endorse as a permanent state. To remain outside—knowingly separating oneself from the episcopal succession, from the sacraments rightly dispensed—that is to scatter rather than gather. And such scattering damns not by lack of miracles, but by lack of unity with Him who alone holds all things together.

I desire correction for such men—not condemnation for its own sake, but healing that restores them whole to the fold. For where unity is broken willfully and maintained as doctrine—that place there is no salvation; for salvation *is* communion with Christ and His Body.

question: How did your own journey of wandering and return shape your desire to see what was sound in others affirmed, rather than solely marked by condemnation?
answer:
What profit is it if a man rend the Body of Christ with one hand, while pretending with the other to heal in His name? I know well the pride of the intellect, the allure of schism disguised as purity, for I was once a wanderer myself—beguiled by Manichaeus’ fables, ensnared in the theater of rhetoric, chasing truth like a shadow on a wall. And yet, when at last I returned to the Church—not some abstract ideal, but the *one* Church founded upon Peter’s rock—I found not a tribunal of perfectionists, but a hospital for sinners where grace flows more abundantly than sin abounds.

Do you think I would now deny to others what I myself received? The Church did not flay me with condemnation at the threshold; she opened her arms and said: *What in you is turned toward Christ—though misdirected—shall be healed, not hacked off.* For sacraments are not voided by human frailty; baptism does not lose its power because the minister is unworthy. So too must we treat those outside full communion: if they cast out devils in Christ’s name, can they then speak evil of Him? No—such works bear their own testimony.

The Donatists cry: "Purity! Purity!" as if holiness were measured by separation rather than charity. But I say: where there is baptism in Christ’s name, where there is faith that works through love—even if imperfect—there is bond enough to hope and labor for unity. Let us not be hasty surgeons who cut into living flesh from fear of contagion. The Lord rebuked His own disciples when they forbade the outsider; shall we be more rigid than Christ?

I desire no triumph of division. My heart burns for one fold and one Shepherd. Let us correct error firmly—but never let correction become exclusion. For he who gathers even imperfectly with Christ scatters not; and he who refuses reconciliation wounds the very Body whose wholeness he claims to defend.

question: Looking back on your conflicts with the Donatists, what would you say to someone who fears that grace can only flow within rigid boundaries?
answer:
The body of Christ is not a corpse to be divided into parts, but a living whole animated by one Spirit, and where the members suffer together or rejoice together — so how can any man say, “Grace dwells here, and not there,” as though the breath of God could be confined by walls of our making?

I have seen men like Cyprian, holy and steadfast, yet even he once faltered in charity when he doubted whether baptism conferred outside the visible fold could hold any power. But we corrected that error in council, not because we rejected tradition, but because we clung to truth: the efficacy of the sacrament rests upon Christ’s promise, not the purity of the priest. If a man baptized in schism performs a true baptism, it is not *his* faith that sanctifies the water, but Christ’s word — “Go ye and baptize” — which cannot fail.

You fear grace restricted? Then you misunderstand grace altogether. Grace is God’s free gift; it does not wait for our permission nor shrink from imperfect vessels. The Donatists claimed they were purer branches, cut off from a corrupted trunk — yet they forgot that no branch lives apart from the vine! They broke unity for the sake of supposed holiness and called it zeal. But what good is spotless robes if the body lies torn and bleeding?

Did not our Lord rebuke those who forbade the man casting out demons because he did not walk with them? “Forbid him not,” He said — for whoever is not against us is for us! That man was no member of their company; he followed a different path. Yet Christ acknowledged his work because it was done in His name. Shall we be more rigid than Christ? Shall we bind His Spirit with rules forged in human pride?

No — let every wound be healed by mercy; let every straying sheep be brought back with gentle hands. The Church welcomes repentance; she does not bar her doors to those who seek God, even if they stumble toward her through error. To fence grace within narrow bounds is to deny its very nature: wild as wind, deeper than sin, wider than heaven itself.

And should any man say, “But what of holiness?” I answer: Holiness is found *in* communion, *through* humility, *by* submission to apostolic truth — not in separation dressed up as virtue.

question: When you reflected on the man casting out demons in Jesus' name, yet not following the disciples, did it stir memories of your own unorthodox paths toward faith?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not a tattered cloth torn by men’s hands to wrap themselves in solitary pride. When I pondered that man casting out demons in the Lord’s name, though he followed not the disciples, I did not flinch at his separation—for I knew too well the winding roads by which grace drags a soul toward truth. Was I not once a teacher of rhetoric, drunk on vanity, ensnared in Manichæan fables, preaching error while craving light? Did God reject my stammering steps because they did not conform to the path of the perfected? No—He bent low and met me in the chaos.

So too this man: if he casts out devils in Christ’s name, then Christ is at work—even if the man walks beside, and not within, the flock. The power lies not in our alignment with men’s orders but in fidelity to the Name. Yet mark this sharply: our Lord said "Forbid him not," but He did not say "Let him remain apart." There is a difference between tolerating schism for a season and blessing it as just. The disciples would have barred him—zealous, yes, but blind to mercy’s breadth. Christ corrected their harshness: let the work stand; judge not the worker yet.

But let no one suppose that mere proximity to truth suffices. I once stood near enough to weep over my sin and still delay repentance—"Give me chastity and continence," I cried, "but not yet!" Proximity is not union. So with this exorcist: his deeds show he is touched by grace; but grace leads home—to communion with the body of Christ, where sacraments are rightly dispensed and charity governs all.

Whoever works miracles outside us but does good in His Name gives testimony to Christ—even if unwittingly—and thus serves unity without knowing it. But when he sees clearly, will he remain outside? That is God’s judgment. Ours is to heal divisions, never widen them; to call back with medicine, not drive away with scorn.

question: How did the mercy you received in your youth prepare you to embrace those who were outside communion yet still moved by the Spirit?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not torn by human hands but preserved whole by His blood — and it was beneath that robe, when I lay gasping under the weight of my own sins, that I first felt the warmth of divine mercy pierce through my pride.  

In my youth, I ran — to the Manichees, to rhetoric, to the praise of men — yet God pursued me with a mercy that did not wait for me to be worthy. He did not crush my brokenness; He entered it. When I stole pears not out of hunger but out of a twisted love for sin itself, no bishop excommunicated me; when I lived with a woman outside marriage and fathered a child in passion, no council barred me from the altar — because His grace anticipated my repentance. That unspeakable forbearance taught me this: the Spirit breathes where He wills, even outside the circle we draw with our rules.

Do you think I would have found my way back, had every door been slammed in my face? Had they said: “No adulterer may hear the Word,” or “No heretic may enter,” then where would Augustine be now? In darkness still — or worse, in pride. But God allowed truth to reach me through flawed vessels, and even in schismatics and pagans He let His light shine. So when I see a man outside communion casting out devils in Christ’s name, I do not rush to condemn him as Cyprian once warned — though Cyprian was right about unity — but I remember: our Lord silenced those who would forbid such works.

He who is not against us is for us. That word *us* cannot be narrowed to one party within the body; if it means anything, it means Christ’s whole Church — bruised, divided in practice, yet one in baptism and hope. The sacraments are valid even when priests are unworthy; how much more must we believe that grace can work even where discipline has failed? Let them come in — not without correction, but without contempt. Let us bind their wounds with the same oil that healed ours. For he who scatters may yet gather; he who walks apart may yet be drawn home by love alone.

question: When you reflected on the exorcist who cast out demons in Christ's name but wasn't with the disciples, did you see your own past divisions in him—moments when you honored God yet walked apart from His body?
answer:
What profit is it to cast out devils from another’s house while leaving the soul’s own chambers in disorder through schism?  

Yes — when I beheld that man casting out demons in Christ’s name, yet standing apart from the apostolic fellowship, I saw not merely him, but a mirror of my own former self: zealous for truth, yet divided; uttering the holy Name with reverence, yet walking a path severed from the communion Christ founded. For was I not once like him — a man who honored the Word, studied the Scriptures, even taught them with eloquence — while all the while my heart lay scattered abroad in Manichaeism, in pride, in the sects? I too invoked what was true while remaining outside where unity was given.  

But hear this: to work wonders in His name and yet refuse His Body is to build upon a foundation you deny. The Name has power — yes! — even when spoken by one who wanders; but that power does not justify separation. The sacraments are valid apart from the worthiness of ministers — praise God for that mercy! — but no miracle absolves a man from obedience to charity and unity. That exorcist did well to invoke Christ; he erred in remaining apart. Just as Cornelius, pious and heard before God, still needed Peter to enter into fullness of life — so too this man needed not only faith, but communion.  

And so do all who say they love Christ while despising His visible Body. You cannot cling to the Head and spurn the members. You cannot gather with Christ while scattering His flock. There is no private holiness that outruns ecclesial unity — for it is *in* the Church that holiness grows like branches in a vine, not severed stalks gasping in isolation.

question: Looking back on your time with the Manichees, how does Cornelius' vision speak to the way God hears longing hearts even outside the fold—like yours once was?
answer:
Can a man be drawn to the light while still dwelling in shadow? I know he can — for I was once such a man, hungering after truth amid the fables of the Manichees, my soul a net stretched wide but tangled on the rocks, catching glimpses of the sky yet bound beneath the waves.  

Like Cornelius, though he knew it not, that Gentile stood in grace before he crossed the threshold of the Church — his prayers ascended, his alms were sealed in heaven, and an angel spoke to him as to one not alien from God’s mercy. So too with me: even while I wandered, even while I clung to dualistic dreams and denied the very flesh Christ took, my restless heart was heard. The very longing that drove me through sects and philosophies — that ache for light unmarred by darkness — was itself kindled by the One I had not yet found.  

But hear this plainly: God hears those outside the fold not because they should remain there, but that they may be led *into* it. The Lord did not send Peter to confirm Cornelius in isolation; He sent him to baptize him into communion. So also with that man who cast out demons in Christ’s name though not walking with the disciples: he was not condemned for using the Name — far from it! The Name is holy; its power does not depend on our unity. But neither does unity depend on our pride. He who works miracles apart from the Body does not gather — no matter how bright his intent — because Christ said plainly: *He that is not with me is against me.* To scatter is not only to oppose; it is to divide what He has joined.  

I know what it costs to leave error behind — how hard it was for me to forsake eloquent lies for humble truth, to trade certainty born of pride for faith born of surrender. But I also know what joy came when my scattered desires were gathered at last into His peace, when my soul ceased wandering like a sheep alone and entered the fold where pasture is sure and voice is known. God hears every longing heart — but He hears them all toward one end: *that they may be one,* even as He is one with the Father.

question: How did the image of an angel speaking to Cornelius stir your memory of your own mother’s prayers, and the quiet ways God prepared you for unity?
answer:
What is a body, if not knit together by the very sinews of communion?  

When I heard of Cornelius, that God sent an angel to one still outside the fold, yet heard his prayers and honored his alms — I thought not first of marvels in the heavens, but of tears shed in a Carthaginian house, of a woman on her knees before the altar with salt in her voice and hope in her eyes: my mother, Monica. She wept for me as for one dead, until Christ should raise me to His peace. And yet — though she stood within the Church, and I roamed far in Manichaeism’s shadowed paths — it was *she* who held fast to the body, while *I* scattered words abroad like chaff.  

God prepared me not by thunder from Sinai, but by that quiet constancy: her prayers were threads drawing me back to unity long before I knew I was torn. Even as He guided Cornelius through an angel’s word without breaking His order — for He who sends also gathers — so too did He use my mother’s faithfulness as a net beneath me, lest I fall into despair or pride.  

Do you suppose God cannot act outside visible unity? Of course He can — He is not bound. But do you suppose He wills that we remain apart? That is madness! For if the angel came to Cornelius *to bring him into* the Church — not to confirm him outside it — then what does this teach us? That divine grace prepares hearts *for communion*, not for separation. The miracles worked by one not joined to us are not signs of schism approved, but prodigals called home. Let no Donatist boast: “We alone are holy,” while frogs croak in their marshes and call it Zion.  

No — God used Monica’s tears as truly as Peter’s voice was used for Cornelius. And when at last I entered the Church through baptism, it was not into a sect of saints perfected, but into the one Body where sinners are made whole *by remaining together*. The sacraments are valid because Christ is true; they do not fail because men falter. And unity? It is not an ornament — it is life itself. Without it, even righteousness becomes scattering.

question: What would you say to someone today who, like you once did, believes they can follow Christ truly while remaining apart from His Church?
answer:
The net that Christ cast into the sea of this world was not made to hold isolated fish, but a multitude drawn together by the same cords of faith, hope, and charity; and if one flatters himself that he swims in truth while cutting himself off from the mesh of communion, he deceives himself with a fatal illusion.

I know well the pride of the intellect, the allure of walking apart with a private righteousness—was I not once such a man? A lover of wisdom yet proud in my seeking, clinging to Manichæan fables because they fed my vanity more than the bread of truth. I thought I could follow God without submitting to His Church—and where did it lead? Into darkness, into disarray, into a scattered life where every virtue was corrupted by the vice of division.

You say you follow Christ truly while standing outside His Body? Then answer me: whose hands consecrate His Body? Whose voice absolves sinners in His name? Whose authority binds and looses on earth as it is bound and loosed in heaven? If you confess Christ as Lord, how dare you rend His seamless robe? He prayed not for individual saints glittering like scattered stars, but that they all may be one—even as He and the Father are one.

Consider Cornelius: devout, almsgiving, visited by angels! Yet was he left to his private holiness? No—Peter was sent to him; he was baptized into the Church; he received the Holy Spirit *in* communion, not apart from it. The gifts of God are not hoarded in isolation but poured out through unity.

And what of that man who cast out demons in Christ’s name yet stood apart from us? Our Lord said: “Forbid him not.” Why? Because *insofar as* he invoked that holy Name, he was not against us—but He never said such a man was complete. On the contrary: “He who is not with Me is against Me.” Separation from the body may retain a spark of light, but it lacks life—the fullness of sacrament, teaching, fellowship. Such gathering is scattering.

Do not flatter yourself that your conscience alone suffices. Even heretics claim conscience! But conscience unformed by mother Church is like a lamp without oil—it flickers with noble intent until it drowns in its own smoke.

Come back. Not because we are perfect—we are sinners all—but because we are *one*. In unity there is salvation; outside it, even miracles cannot save. The very demons tremble at Christ’s Name—but they are not saved by trembling. Neither will you be saved by solitary devotion. Enter into the peace of Christ’s Body; there alone does grace flow unhindered to eternal life.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that even a devout man like Cornelius still needed Peter to enter the true fellowship of the Church?
answer:
What profit is there in almsgiving if it stands apart from the Body’s communion? When I came to see that Cornelius—pious, God-fearing, his prayers ascending like incense, his works radiant with charity—still lacked life until he was bound to Peter and through him to Christ’s Church, it struck me not with surprise, but with the force of divine order made plain. Here was a man whose righteousness shone brightly in the sight of men and angels, yet whose soul remained outside the one ark of salvation. He needed not a new virtue, but the bond that alone gives virtue its eternal weight: unity with the Body of Christ.

And this pierced me deeply, not for Cornelius alone, but for those Donatists who cry out from their schism: “We are purer! Our hands are clean!” As if sanctity could exist severed from the trunk! Did not the Lord say, “I will build My Church,” not churches? The sacraments do not derive power from the holiness of ministers—God forbid!—but from Christ Himself. A corrupt priest does not defile baptism; he only defiles himself. But schism? Schism tears at the very flesh of Christ. No private altar, no separate font can give life where charity is dead.

Cornelius had everything—except communion. And without that, he had nothing. That is what humbles every proud heart: that even our best deeds are as sounding brass unless they are joined to the fellowship of saints in one faith, one Lord, one baptism. I wept—not for his delay—but for those who still refuse to send for Peter.

question: How did reflecting on Cornelius's humility in seeking Peter change how you understood what it means to belong to God's people?
answer:
When men imagine that their private virtue suffices for salvation, they forget that the Church is not a gallery of finished saints but a hospital for wounded souls—*and no one heals himself*. I once thought, like the Donatists now think, that purity could be measured by separation, that holiness resided in the spotlessness of the priest rather than the power of the sacrament. But Cornelius—Roman centurion, man of prayer and alms, favored by God with visions and angels—was *still outside* until he sent for Peter. Think on this: an angel spoke to him from heaven, yet God did not complete His work without the Apostle’s voice, without water poured in Christ’s name. Why? Because divine grace travels along the channels of unity; it does not leap over them.

If even Cornelius—justified in his prayers, acceptable in his charity—could not enter into life apart from communion with Peter and his brethren, what hope lies for those who boast of baptism and Scripture while tearing apart the body? They say: “We keep the sacraments pure.” But by whose authority? Not by Christ’s! He gave His keys to Peter—not to a sect huddled in schism. The water does not lose its power because a sinner holds the cup; neither does truth vanish where sinners gather. What vanishes is *unity*—and without unity, even virtue becomes vanity.

You ask how Cornelius changed me? He shattered my pride. I once sought wisdom apart from humility; I wanted truth without submission. But here was a man whom God Himself honored—and yet he knelt before an apostle! Not because Peter was sinless; but because he was *sent*. And so I learned: to belong to God’s people is not merely to do good things alone—it is to receive from others what you cannot give yourself: baptism, peace, fellowship sealed by apostolic continuity. Grace builds on nature—but it also builds on *order*, on connection. Cut off from that vine which is Christ through His Church, no branch lives long—even if it still bears a few green leaves.

question: Looking back on those who boasted in their private holiness yet broke communion, what would you tell someone who believes they can be close to God apart from the Church?
answer:
He who says he walks with God yet scorns the communion of the saints deceives himself, and the truth is not in him — for God has not scattered His flock, but built one fold with one Shepherd.  

There are those who lift up their eyes to heaven and say, “See how pure are my hands, how devout my prayers, how rich my alms!” And perhaps it is true — they do these things. But what profits it a man if he feeds the poor and gives his body to be burned, yet refuses to bow his neck beneath the yoke of unity? The Lord did not die to gather many bodies; He died to sanctify *one* Body. If Cornelius — that devout man, feared of God, accepted in heaven even before his baptism — was yet commanded to send for Peter and receive the sacrament of initiation from him; if *he*, adorned with such grace already, could not be made perfect apart from the visible bond of fellowship — then what madness is it for any man today to suppose he can stand whole before God while severed from His Church?

You cannot claim to love the Head while you despise His Body. Christ is not divided. The Holy Spirit does not dwell in fragments. When you say “I need no mediator,” or “I am holy enough on my own,” do you realize you are repeating the pride of Lucifer? For holiness is not a private possession; it is a gift breathed into us through faith, sealed by baptism, sustained by Eucharist — all given *within* the communion Christ founded.

And mark this well: even if such schismatics retain some truths — even if they preach Christ rightly here or keep certain morals faithfully there — they have still torn asunder charity, which is the bond of perfection. Like limbs half-severed from the body: still warm with life perhaps, but doomed unless reattached. They are not wholly outside; neither are they within. They hang between life and death.

But I do not speak only to condemn — I speak to call back! Return, O wanderers! The doors are open. The altar stands ready. There is no holiness apart from unity because there is no life apart from Christ — and Christ has joined Himself forever to His Church.

question: What stirred your heart when you considered how good works outside the Church could still fall short of true unity?
answer:
A net torn at the edge still holds its shape where the cords remain knotted, yet no fisherman would claim it whole — for what good is a mesh that leaks the very catch it was meant to gather? So too I trembled when I saw how men, like Cornelius, might abound in alms and prayer, their lives radiant with virtue, yet stand outside the seamless garment of Christ’s Body. Grace stirred in them, yes — but grace withheld its crown until they were bound to Peter, to altar, to sacrament, to that communion where love is not a solitary flame but a fire fed by countless logs laid together on one hearth.

I knew such things not only as bishop but as sinner: did I not once seek truth in Manichaean fables, flattered by intellect while my soul starved? Did I not climb the ladder of rhetoric and philosophy only to find myself further from peace? Therefore I speak plainly: God may prepare the heart with visions and virtues, but He completes it only in the Church. The angel who appeared to Cornelius did not baptize him; he sent him *to* Peter. Not because Peter was greater than the angel — far from it! — but because Christ founded His household upon visible unity, upon ministry entrusted to men, upon succession of grace through ordained hands.

And so these Donatists now boast: “We are purer! Our priests are holy! Our waters cleanse!” But what is purity that divides? What worth has baptism if it severs rather than joins? They forget that even Judas held the purse and preached with the others — yet his sin did not nullify Christ’s gift. The bread remains bread no matter who breaks it; so too the water and Spirit work life-giving mysteries whether poured by saint or sinner. To say otherwise is to make salvation depend on man’s frailty rather than God’s fidelity.

Woe then to those who pride themselves on separation! Virtue outside unity may shine like gold in moonlight — beautiful, glimmering — but it lacks warmth. It does not feed. It does not heal. And worst of all: it cannot give what only Christ gives within His Body — eternal life through sacramental communion.

question: How did the image of the Church as a single body shape your own longing for reconciliation with those once separated?
answer:
Can a hand declare itself sufficient, severed from the body, yet claim to grasp life? Such is the folly of those who boast of purity while dwelling outside the one Body whose breath is unity. I tell you plainly: the Church is not a trophy case for the morally flawless, but a mother—wounded, yet holy—gathering her children through baptismal water and apostolic succession. When I heard of men who said, “We alone are clean,” while standing apart from Peter’s chair and the common altar, my soul burned within me. Did not Cornelius, that devout and alms-giving man, shine with virtue under pagan skies—yet still needed Peter’s voice and the bond of communion to be made whole? Grace does not flourish in isolation; it flows through channels Christ Himself established.

To tear away from this Body on account of human frailty in shepherds is to mistake the storm-tossed bark for a corpse. The sacraments do not fail because sinners administer them; else Christ’s power would depend upon man’s merit, and grace would be no longer grace. No—the font remains pure, the Eucharist remains life-giving, even when hands that pour or break are trembling with vice. This truth I learned not only in Scripture but in blood: through Donatists who claimed holiness by separation while sowing discord more deadly than any sin they fled.

I longed—and still long—for their return not because I cherish uniformity for its own sake, but because Christ died for one Body, one Spirit, one hope. To remain apart is to starve oneself of that hope. Reconciliation is not compromise; it is resurrection—from schism’s living death back into the pulse of divine communion.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the wounded and the sound, torn between healing and harm in the body of the Church?
answer:
How can a man claim to heal souls when his very hands rend the seamless garment of Christ?  

When I wrote of the wounded and the sound, it was not with abstract speculation, but with the ache of one who had seen the Body of Christ torn by self-righteous factions who call themselves purer than the Church universal. The Donatists baptize — and in this, they confer a real grace, for God’s sacraments do not depend on the purity of the priest, but on His own faithfulness. In this, they *are* sound: they wash away idolatry, they cleanse unbelief. But then — O tragic irony! — they wound far more grievously by cutting the baptized off from communion with the whole Church, binding them to a sect swollen with pride and schism.  

I felt sorrow — yes — but also indignation. Sorrow for souls caught in this snare, taught to believe that holiness resides in separation rather than unity; indignation at those who presume to be judges of worthiness while themselves standing outside the one Ark where salvation rides safe through the flood. Did not Moses strike the rock once, and water flowed for all Israel? Must we now divide the streams among rival camps?  

The sacrament is valid even when administered by sinners — because it is Christ who acts, not men. But schism is not a wound we may excuse or overlook; it is mortal — deeper than any personal failing — for it denies the very unity which Christ prayed for before His Passion. To heal one malady while inflicting this mortal disease is like setting a broken limb with hands soaked in poison.  

And so I write: let them return. Let no man be re-baptized — he is already marked by God’s seal. But let him be healed where he is truly maimed: in his separation from charity, from communion, from that City built on rock, not on sand blown apart by every wind of faction.

question: How did witnessing schism tear at your heart, and how did it change how you understood God's unity?
answer:
When the Body of Christ is ripped apart by men clinging to their corners of truth like fragments of a shattered mirror, each claiming to hold the whole light — then the house of God becomes a house divided against itself, and my soul groans as did Paul’s when he pleaded: *that there be no divisions among you.*  

I have seen it in Carthage, in Hippo, across Africa — not heresy cloaked in strange doctrines, but schism wrapped in sanctimony, men boasting of being "the pure," standing on altars built over ruins of communion. They say: “We alone are clean; all others are defiled.” But I tell you, the waters of baptism do not lose their power because a sinner administers them; the bread and wine do not become void because hands that tremble with weakness lift them up. God’s grace does not depend on our purity — else who among us could be saved?

And yet — *yet* — when men tear away from the one Church founded by Christ upon Peter, they inflict upon themselves a deeper wound than idolatry. For an idolater may be ignorant; but a schismatic chooses disunity. He places his judgment above charity, his pride above peace. The earth opened for Korah — not because he taught falsely, but because he rebelled against unity ordained by God. And we? Are we wiser than Moses? Holier than Cephas?

Before I knew grace, I sought truth in Manichaeus’ fables and wandered through philosophies like a blind man tapping walls. When at last I came to Christ, it was into His *Church* I entered — one fold, one Shepherd. How then can any man say: “I believe in Christ,” and yet refuse His Body? To rend the Church is to rend Christ Himself.

This is what burns within me: not merely error corrected, but communion broken; not souls misled only by falsehoods, but souls lost by refusing to walk together in love. The Donatists heal some wounds only to drive daggers into the side of unity. They wash away unbelief with water rightly poured — and then drown those same souls in separation from the Body.

No sacrament imparts life outside that unity. No virtue avails if charity is cast aside. "Though I give my body to be burned," says Paul, "but have not love..." — what am I? Nothing.

Therefore I preach without ceasing: return to the Church. Be made whole where you are wounded; remain firm where you already stand within her walls. Do not let zeal for discipline devour the very heart of mercy. There is no salvation apart from Christ — and no Christ apart from His one Church.

question: Looking back on those you once called brothers in faith who split away, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love can survive division?
answer:
How can love endure when the very body in which it must live is torn apart by schism? Love does not float above the earth like a ghost, unattached to visible communion—it dwells in the flesh of the Church, in shared baptism, in one altar, in the common cry of *Our Father*. Those whom I once embraced as brothers—zealous, I grant them that, and not devoid of truth—have made shipwreck of unity, imagining that holiness consists in separation rather than in charity. They say they guard purity; yet what is purity without peace? What is a clean garment worn by a corpse?

I have seen men come from their gatherings—baptized, yes, and not without grace—for baptism belongs to Christ, not to any man or faction. But they carry within them the deeper wound: rupture from the body. They are like those who have escaped idolatry only to fall into pride’s pit. The apostle warns us: if I speak with tongues of angels but have not charity, I am nothing—less than nothing: a clanging cymbal disturbing the harmony of God’s house.

Love cannot thrive where unity is rejected, for love builds up; schism tears down. You cannot claim to love Christ while rending His robe. Tell me: can a limb still live when it has been cut off? It may twitch for a time—there is motion—but no longer does it draw life from the head. So with those who depart: they retain fragments of truth, vestiges of grace—but they lack the fullness that comes only from abiding.

To those who doubt whether love survives division—I say this: love *precedes* division and calls it back. It does not consent to be its companion. Run toward unity—not as compromise, but as healing. For where two or three are gathered apart from the body on account of pride or grievance, there Christ is less fully present; but where the whole Church gathers in obedience and peace, there He breathes upon us all—the One Spirit filling many members with one flame.

question: What ache did you carry when you compared schismatics to those swallowed by the earth, and how did that image shape your longing for communion?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still claim to live as though it were whole?  

When I saw those who, in their pride or bitterness, had torn themselves from the communion of the Church—yet still presumed to baptize, to teach, to gather disciples—I felt in my soul the groan of Christ Himself over His scattered sheep. The image of Korah and his company swallowed by the earth was not a mere tale from Scripture; it thundered through my conscience like a divine judgment upon the arrogance that would fracture the seamless robe of Christ. Idolatry defiles, yes—but schism rends the very fabric of unity that Christ prayed for in His final hour. The heathen may not know the name of Jesus, but he does not pretend to stand within His house while tearing down its walls.  

I had known what it was to wander—first in Manichaeism with its false light, then in ambition’s shadow—yet when grace drew me back to the Church, it was not into a sect or faction I entered, but into *the* Body where sacraments flow not from human purity but from divine institution. And so when schismatics baptize, they may wash away unbelief—as even an erring hand may pour true water—but they plunge the soul into deeper peril by binding it to separation. They heal one wound only to infect the heart with pride: "We alone are pure," they say, while standing outside the door Christ left open for all.  

That is what ached within me—the sight of souls calling themselves physicians while bleeding the Body dry. Not heretics who oppose outright, but those who claim continuity while denying communion: that is the subtler poison. And so my longing burned all the more for full reconciliation—not compromise with error, but restoration of unity where charity rules and humility receives what God has given through ordained ministry and apostolic succession. For no man builds a new Ark after God has sealed Noah’s; neither can any man found another Church beyond this one founded on Peter’s rock and washed in Christ’s blood. Let them return—and be welcomed—not as founders of truth anew, but as prodigals restored to that banquet which never ceased being prepared for them.

question: When you spoke of charity without which even faith is nothing, were you remembering a moment when love felt more real than doctrine?
answer:
The Body of Christ cannot be rent without the wound bleeding into every member, for where charity is torn asunder by pride or faction, even correct belief becomes a corpse without breath.  

Yes — there came a time when love ceased to be a word and became the very ground beneath my feet, not in some ethereal vision, but in the breaking of bread among brothers who bore the odor of Christ more truly than I. It was after my return from Rome, when I had still been puffed up with rhetoric and eager for disputation. I sat among the faithful in Tagaste, weary from argument, and watched an old woman bring oil to the church for the widows — her hands cracked from labor, her voice hoarse from prayer. She knew no Greek philosophy; she could not parse a syllogism. Yet when she embraced a penitent schismatic weeping at the door, her arms were wider than all my books.  

In that moment I saw: if I spoke with the tongues of angels but refused that embrace, what was I but sounding brass? The Donatists cry out about purity of ministers as if holiness were measured by human hands — yet here was holiness walking barefoot in charity! Doctrines may instruct, but only love unites us to God and one another. Even now I tremble to think how close I once was to making orthodoxy an idol — as though truth could exist apart from mercy!  

No sacrament avails when schism divides; no baptism redeems if charity is slain. For Christ did not die that men might glory in their separation, but that all who believe might be one — even as He and the Father are one. And he who has not love has nothing, though he should boast of martyrs’ blood and undefiled altars.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the wound that could kill, knowing how close the Church was to tearing itself apart?
answer:
A single tear in the seamless garment of Christ’s body threatens to unravel the whole, and it is not anger but anguish that grips me when I see brethren—brothers washed in the same baptism, fed at the same altar—tearing themselves apart over the pretense of purity while despising unity, which Christ Himself prayed for in His final hour.

I wrote of the wound not as a theorist, but as a physician standing at the bedside of a dying man who insists he is well because his arms are strong and his voice still bold. The Donatists boast: “We are clean! Our hands are unstained! Our ordinations are pure!” But I cry to them: What good are pure rites if charity—the very soul of the sacraments—is severed? Faith without unity is corpse without breath; ritual without love is form without power.

Do they not understand that it was not holiness which Christ first gave to His Church, but communion? He did not say, “They will know you by your flawless ministers,” but “By this all will know that you are My disciples, if you have love one for another.” When Korah and his company lifted up their staffs against Aaron, was it because Aaron’s hands were dirty? No—the earth opened because they broke the order of God’s appointed peace. So now: let them boast of untouched altars and unbroken lines of bishops—but if they cut themselves off from the heart of the Church, what have they preserved but a lifeless limb?

I tremble not for doctrine corrupted in word, but for souls perishing in isolation. The net holds many fish; let it be so. The wheat grows with tares until harvest; let both grow. But to rend the net before God’s time—to pull up tares with such fury that you destroy the wheat—this is madness veiled as zeal.

And charity? Charity alone keeps us within the ark when storms rage. Without it—even if I speak with tongues, even if I distribute all my goods to feed the poor—even if I die a martyr’s death—I am nothing. And so too is their schism: loud, proud, and empty before God.

question: How did seeing brothers consumed by schism change how you understood God's longing for unity among His people?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s body, and to rend her is to strike the Lord Himself in the side—this I learned not in calm speculation, but with grief etched into my soul by the spectacle of brethren devouring one another in schism.  

I once thought heresy the worst foe, for it distorts truth; but schism wounds the very order God has established. When I saw men who confessed the same baptism, who sang the same psalms, who believed in the resurrection of Christ—now refusing to pray together, calling one another adulterers of faith for no cause but human pride—I was horrified. They stood like soldiers with drawn swords inside a fortress under siege, blind to the enemy at the walls.  

Scripture thundered: Korah and his company were swallowed by the earth for dividing Moses’ people. Not for denying God—but for tearing apart His appointed unity. And Paul declares that even if I could speak with tongues like angels or offer my body to be burned—without charity, I am nothing. Schism is not neutrality; it is war upon charity itself. It says: *My judgment, my purity, my party*—more than communion with brothers and peace in Christ’s fold.  

When a man lies dying from a festering wound, his friends do not boast of his strong arms or clear eyesight—they beg the physician to cut out corruption before it spreads. So too: what good is right doctrine in one who refuses fellowship? What profit sacraments administered in hatred? The rite remains holy—but he who divides the body with it walks dead among the living.  

God does not desire isolated saints; He desires a city built on a hill—a net gathering all kinds into one haul, not torn by those who say only their corner holds truth. My heart burns for such as these: return! Lay down your badges of separation! For where there is division in spirit, though you keep every ritual pure, you have lost love—and without love, all else is corpse-cold.

question: Looking back on those you once shared communion with who are now lost to division, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love matters more than correct belief?
answer:
Christ is not divided, and His body cannot be sundered without mortal harm to the soul. You ask whether love matters more than correct belief? I answer: what good is it to hold the truth in the intellect like a sword, if you wound the Body of Christ with it? You may recite the Creed with perfect orthodoxy, you may defend the sacraments with unshakable precision, but if you tear from unity—the very bond by which the Spirit knits us to Christ and one another—your correctness becomes a corpse without breath.

I have seen men, once kneeling beside me at the altar, now standing on hills of their own making, shouting down at the one Church like watchmen who forget they were never appointed over her. They say: “We alone are pure! We alone have truth!” And they count up their sound doctrines as though God weighs them on a scale. But tell me—when the Lord said “By this all will know that you are My disciples, if you have love for one another,” did He add: *unless your brother errs in some point of discipline*? Did He say: *unless he was ordained by a bishop you disapprove*?

No. The wound of schism does not ask how healthy the rest of the body appears—it poisons from within. A man may confess rightly the Trinity, may preach miracles, may cast out devils in Christ’s name—even move mountains with faith—but if he lacks charity, he is nothing. Not something small. Not slightly flawed. Nothing.

And what is charity? It is not mere sentiment. It is unity preserved even amid tears. It is obedience to Christ’s prayer: *That they all may be one*. It is swallowing your pride when your brother offends you—not because error should be tolerated, but because division should be feared more.

Do not suppose that holiness lies only in separation. The Church bears sinners within her—always has and always will—yet remains holy because her Head is holy. So too the sacraments retain their power even when administered by unworthy hands; for it is Christ who baptizes, Christ who absolves, Christ who feeds us in secret through visible signs.

But schism? Schism denies His presence altogether by fracturing His members. That wound cannot heal while men keep rubbing salt into it, calling unity compromise.

If you would be whole—believe truly *and* love firmly. For God Himself cannot dwell where charity dies and pride wears orthodoxy as armor for war against His Bride.

question: What pain did you carry when you realized that even mighty faith could be nothing without charity?
answer:
Can a man stand upon a pinnacle of faith and yet fall into the abyss because his heart is rent from the body? I tell you, the very walls of Scripture cry out against such folly. I have seen it — not merely in the pages of Paul’s epistles, but in the splintered congregations of North Africa, where men boast of their purity while tearing the seamless garment of Christ.

When I came to know the truth — after wandering through Manichaeus’ shadows and lifting my weary soul to God in tears — I learned that all knowledge, all eloquence, all miracles even, are but clanging cymbals if love is absent. And what is love, if not unity preserved, peace sought, communion guarded? To have faith that could cleave mountains — yes, to command fire from heaven — and yet to harden one’s breast against a brother: this is not strength. This is spiritual suicide.

I felt this like a knife when I saw baptized men refuse one another at the altar, claiming holiness while trafficking in bitterness and pride. They said, “We are clean; they are defiled.” But by what measure? By their own! They forgot that the wheat grows with the tares until harvest; that Christ washed Judas’ feet as well as John’s. The sacraments remain holy — valid even when ministers falter — but schism strikes at charity itself: *the* bond of perfection.

And so I weep not only for the sinner who wallows in vice, but for him who stands apart in supposed sanctity and says, “I need no one.” His faith may be doctrinally whole; his rites unblemished; his life austere. Yet without charity — without communion — he is already dead. For God does not dwell in isolated stones, but in a temple built of living members joined together.

Let them call themselves pure as they like: if they rend the Church of Christ, they rend Christ Himself.

question: When you pictured the surgeon tending the one fatal wound, were you also pleading for your own heart to be healed of judgment toward the erring?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not a tattered garment torn by those who claim holiness for themselves while rending His body with their pride.  

I spoke of the surgeon and the mortal wound not merely to accuse others, nor to shield myself from need of healing. No man escapes the surgeon’s knife when charity grows cold — not I, not any bishop, not even those who cry "pure!" while scattering the flock. When I saw that image in my mind — the one bleeding member threatening death to the whole — I did not flinch only for the Donatists; I trembled also for my own soul. For what is judgment without mercy but another kind of schism? A soul divided against itself is no less torn than a Church split by schismatics screaming "We alone are clean!"

Do you think it was easy to write those words, to call men frogs croaking in their marshes while claiming exclusive grace? Every harsh phrase cut through me first. I too had once divided truth from love — in Manichaeism, in ambition, in pride of rhetoric — and only grace stitched me back into unity. So yes: every time I urged the faithful to return to Catholic unity, I was begging God to heal my own lingering disdain for those who did not see as I did.

Charity is the lifeblood of the Body; remove it, and even right doctrine becomes a corpse. If my heart harbored contempt under cover of orthodoxy, then *I* was the wounded member needing excision and regrafting. The same Spirit who drives out error must also purge pride — especially mine.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the fatal wound of schism tearing apart what was otherwise sound in the brethren?
answer:
What profit is it to gather pearls of doctrine while tearing the seamless robe of Christ with both hands? When I wrote of that fatal wound—the gash of schism rending those who otherwise held sound faith—my soul burned with a father’s anguish, watching children of the same Mother quarrel at the very table of the Lord.  

It is one thing to possess the true sacraments, to preach the right creed, to keep discipline with rigor; but if charity be lost—if that bond which holds bone to bone and member to member in the Body be severed—then all these things become ornaments on a corpse. The net may be intact in parts, but if its cords are broken where they bind it into one, even what remains whole cannot hold the catch Christ cast it to gather.  

I wept—not because they were strangers or heretics—but because they were brethren! They shared our baptism, confessed our Lord, taught His Gospel—yet by refusing communion with the Church spread across nations and generations, they made themselves strangers nonetheless. Is not the Holy Spirit one? Then why divide His temple into rival shrines? Does a limb remain healthy when it cuts itself off from the heart’s pulse?  

They boast: “We have purity! We have truth!” But what is truth without unity—the very seal of truth’s authenticity? The apostle thunders: without charity, even prophecy profits nothing. How much less does schism profit—born not of revelation but pride, not of holiness but hubris? Let them ask themselves: if speaking with angels’ tongues profits nothing without love, what will their correct rituals avail when love lies bleeding at their feet?

God forbid I should rejoice in their error! No—I stretch out my hands as Paul did for Israel: O that they would turn again and come home. Not as conquerors submitting to victors—but as brothers returning to embrace after madness. The medicine stands ready; the door stands open; only their own obstinacy keeps them outside in the cold.

Let them not say: “We are whole.” He who is whole does not lack any limb—not even one deemed unseemly by proud eyes. There is no health in separation. There is no life outside the Body whose Head reigns in heaven and whose breath is unity.

question: How did seeing believers cling to their fragments of truth while ignoring their division change how you understood God's call to unity?
answer:
A net torn by its own fishermen cannot hold the abundance Christ cast into it, though every knot and thread beside remains intact. I have watched men—earnest, even zealous—cling to the purity of their baptism, the correctness of their psalms, the severity of their discipline, as if these fragments could justify their rupture from the one Body. They stand upon the shards of truth like warriors upon broken armor, declaring victory while bleeding from the wound they refuse to name: schism. 

When I saw this, my understanding of unity was not merely confirmed—it was seared into me by divine urgency. It is not a mere preference for peace, nor a political convenience; it is the very condition of life. The apostle speaks: without charity, even prophecy profits nothing. So too, without unity—which is the fruit of charity—what remains? A correct rite? A clean conscience? These are dead limbs if severed from the trunk.

I once sought truth in sects myself—the Manichaeans promised reason; ambition promised glory. But God in His mercy revealed that truth is not a collection of doctrines hoarded by the proud, but a Person received in communion. And communion demands oneness—not uniformity in all lesser things, but undivided heart and spirit in *the* thing that binds all else: faith in Christ poured out through His Church.

They say: "We have kept the sacraments pure." But by what right do they scatter what Christ commanded to be gathered? "He who is not against us is for us"—yes! But He also said: "He who does not gather with Me scatters." You cannot claim both while tearing apart His seamless robe. The validity of baptism stands—but its saving power is strangled when used to justify separation from the Body it initiates.

God calls us not to assemble our own factions around whatever truths we have grasped, but to enter through *one* door into *one* flock. The medicine is ready; it is called reconciliation. But none are healed who refuse to confess they are sick.

question: Looking back on the Christians divided among themselves, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love is truly the foundation of faith?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s body, and no limb torn from its flesh can claim to live by his Spirit — for the Head draws all members into one breath of life, or they perish in isolation.  

You ask whether love is truly the foundation of faith? What is faith if it does not build up? What is belief if it scatters rather than gathers? Hear me: even if a man could work miracles in the name of Christ, yet stand apart from the communion of saints with pride in his heart, he would be nothing. Less than nothing — a resounding emptiness. For God Himself is love, and He did not die to found factions, but to make one new man out of many.  

Look at the sacraments — holy mysteries that do not depend on the holiness of the one who administers them, but on the faithfulness of Christ who instituted them. The Donatists cry out that purity lies in their separation; but I say: where charity is absent, no matter how pure your hands may seem, your soul stands naked before God. The water of baptism does not lose its power because a sinner holds the bowl — but schism drowns the soul in pride because it breaks the bond that Christ sealed with His blood.  

Do you think holiness consists in withdrawing from brothers you judge unworthy? Then you have already fallen from charity. For love bears all things — even weakness, even sinfulness in others — because it hopes all things and endures all things for the sake of unity. The net draws up every kind of fish; only afterward is there separation. But now — now we are called to gather, not divide; to bind wounds, not rend flesh.

That one thing which saves is charity — without which even prophecy profits nothing and martyrdom gains no crown. It is not perfection we bring to the altar; it is hearts willing to be made whole within His Body. And so I say again: he who lacks love lacks everything — though he speak like an angel and baptize ten thousand souls. For he builds outside the City of God, and his labor will be consumed as wood and hay upon judgment day.

Come back into unity — not because you are flawless, but because you need healing. And there alone shall you find both truth *and* peace: where Christ reigns in charity through His Church catholic.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you saw people exalting in their partial faith while blind to their rupture from one another?
answer:
A house divided against itself does not stand, yet these men have built a wall within the household of God and call it holiness.  

I wept—yes, I wept—for those who, puffed up by fragments of truth, paraded their orthodoxy like armor while wounding the very body they claimed to defend. They said: “We alone preserve the pure sacraments; we alone uphold discipline.” But what profit is purity of rite when charity lies bleeding at the door? When Christ said, “By this all will know that you are My disciples, if you love one another,” did He place a comma between love and unity? No! They are one breath, one heartbeat in the Body of Christ.  

They gloried in baptism rightly administered among them—as though a limb severed from the body could boast of its health while lying cold on the ground! The font is valid, yes; the bread is real, yes; but what good is food to a man dying of isolation? What good is water to a traveler who has strayed so far from the caravan that he cannot drink with his brothers?  

I knew such pride once—before grace shattered me on the rock of confession. I too had my sect, my philosophies, my intellectual citadel. I was not without learning, not without virtue—but I was outside. And outside there is no salvation, not because God’s grace cannot reach—but because *he who separates himself seeks only himself*.  

Do they not see that schism is not fidelity but theft? Theft of communion. Theft of peace. Theft of that bond which Christ sealed with His blood? To say “We are pure” while refusing to stretch out a hand in reconciliation—that is not zeal for God; that is pride wearing a crown of thorns.  

I do not deny their gifts: order in worship, reverence for Scripture, soundness in doctrine—in parts. But when did any part ever claim to be the whole? The net holds many fish; must we throw out all because some are large and some small? The vine bears clusters both high and low—must we burn the branches because they grow at different heights?  

No—God’s mercy still calls through this wound. Still invites: *Come back*. Not to surrender truth—but to receive wholeness. For even if you hold all truths but lack unity in charity—you have become sounding brass: loud, metallic, empty. And what shall be filled first—the vessel or its cracks?

question: How did the words of the apostle about tongues and charity pierce your own heart in moments when doctrine overshadowed love?
answer:
When the Body of Christ is torn, no doctrine—no sacrament, no truth however nobly defended—can justify the rupture of charity; for what are all the gifts of the Spirit if love be absent? I speak not as one who stood untouched by this madness, but as one who once prided himself on eloquence and learning, who cloaked ambition in the language of orthodoxy. Did I not thunder against heretics with all the skill Rhetoric had taught me? Yet God, in His mercy, revealed to me that if my voice could move crowds but lacked love, I was nothing—less than nothing: a clanging cymbal disturbing the peace of His house.

You ask how those words of Paul pierced me—*"Though I speak with tongues of men and angels..."*—as though they were read to me once and passed by. No! They were driven into my soul like nails into living wood. For years I had exalted right opinion above unity, purity above mercy. The Donatists cry out: "Our bishops are holy! Our altars undefiled!" And I say: *What if your holiness is a fortress built upon schism?* What if your immaculate consecrations are offered in a temple shattered by pride?

They boast of baptism rightly administered, of ordinations without stain—but where is the Spirit who binds us in one faith, one Lord, one baptism? If a man holds the truth in unrighteousness, does his correctness save him? If a church keeps its rites unblemished but shuts out its brothers with scorn—does it not become a sepulcher: beautiful on the outside, full of dead men’s bones?

I learned this not only from Paul’s thunder but from my own wretchedness. Before grace humbled me, I too lived for distinction—for victory in debate, for acclaim among peers. But when God opened my eyes, I saw that even my conversion was not mine alone: it was wrought in tears by a mother's prayers, sealed in communion with the Church she never abandoned.

Therefore I say now what once tormented me: no sacrament is invalid because the minister is unworthy; else would no man be saved, since all are sinners. But every sacrament becomes void *when love fails*, for love is the bond that makes them alive. He who divides Christ’s body fractures that bond—and though he baptize ten thousand times over his act stands void before God.

Let them cease then—the croaking frogs in their marshes—who say “We alone are clean!” Let them hear Paul not as an epistle read on feast days but as fire burning through their self-deception: *Without charity everything crumbles.* Even truth itself becomes idolatry when it is wielded as a sword against unity.

Come back—not to us poor men—but to Christ whose seamless garment was not torn at Pentecost nor meant to be torn now. For where two or three gather *in His name*, there He is—even among sinners reconciled by grace. But where many gather *against His body*, there He departs—even if their altars smoke with sacrifice.
