question: What were you feeling when you realized that even the most sacred gifts could be hollow without love?
answer:
Can a man possess the keys to the banquet, stand at the threshold with lamps blazing, and yet be a stranger to the wedding feast? So it was with me when I saw—no, when God forced upon my soul the searing truth—that prophecy, miracles, even the administration of holy mysteries, are but clanging echoes in an empty temple if love is absent. I had tasted ambition in Carthage, drunk deep of applause as a rhetorician; I had dabbled in Manichaeism, seeking truth in gaudy riddles. But when at last I stood within the Church—not as an ornamented orator but as a sinner weeping at her feet—I learned that all gifts are given for unity, not pride.  

There are those among us—Donatists who boast of their purity—who claim that grace dies in unholy hands. Folly! The sacrament does not shrink from sinners; it purifies them. A chalice does not become common because a thief lifts it to his lips—the water still becomes Christ’s blood. Did not Caiaphas prophesy by God’s Spirit while plotting murder? Did not Simon wield power and yet remain in chains of covetousness? And do not even demons tremble with true belief—and yet remain damned? No gift sanctifies the man; it is charity that binds him to Christ. Without it, we are tombs painted white: radiant without, dead within.

I tell you plainly: division is death disguised as virtue. Love alone builds up the body; schism tears apart Christ’s seamless robe. Let them keep their thundering altars and pure bishops—what profits it if they lack love? Nothing. Less than nothing. For where there is no charity, there is no Church—no matter how loudly they chant psalms or claim apostolic seed.

question: How did your own struggles with pride shape your understanding of true charity?
answer:
It is not the splendor of speech nor the height of revelation that binds the body of Christ together, but the lowly, relentless love that bends to wash feet. I know this not merely from Paul’s thunder in the epistles, but from the long, agonizing school of my own soul—where ambition cloaked itself as zeal, where rhetoric stood in for repentance, and where I once presumed holiness could be achieved by intellect alone.  

When I chased Manichaean fables and basked in the applause of rhetorical triumphs, I was full of sound and void of charity. The louder my voice grew in debate, the emptier my heart became before God. It was only when I was broken—when I heard that childlike voice saying *“Take up and read”*—that I saw how pride had built a wall between me and true communion. For charity does not strut; it kneels. It does not divide; it endures. It does not claim exclusive purity; it bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things—even schismatics.

And so I say now what grace taught me in tears: no sacrament rightly administered loses its power because the minister is flawed; no prophecy avails without love as its root. The Donatists trumpet their purity like a victory banner, yet they rend Christ’s seamless garment with their hands. They forget that even Caiaphas prophesied by divine necessity though he conspired to kill the Lord! Do they imagine their indignation makes them holier than Peter—who denied his Lord thrice—yet was restored by charity?

No—true charity is not measured by separation but by union: union with God who justifies the ungodly, union with a Church that welcomes sinners to her table precisely because she is mother to them. My pride once barred me from such humility; now I preach it as life itself. Without charity—even if one gives one's body to be burned—the soul remains dead in its isolation.

question: Looking back on your time in the midst of theological debates, what would you tell someone who doubts their worth because they lack spiritual gifts?
answer:
What profit is it to fear that you are nothing because you lack the thunder of prophecy or the tongue of angels, when Christ Himself built His Church not on splendor of speech, but on the rock of confession and the foundation of love?  

I know how the proud heart trembles in its own shadow—how it measures itself against visions, miracles, eloquence—and finds itself wanting. I was once drunk with words, hungry for applause, dazzled by the gifts rather than the Giver. But grace stripped me bare and taught me this: a single sigh of love lifts the soul higher than all knowledge exalts it.  

Do you think God looks upon you and counts what you *have not*? The devil believes—he trembles. Caiaphas prophesied—and condemned himself. Simon Magus received power—and was lost. Gifts are not guarantees; they are tools in God’s hands, not badges for our boast. But charity—charity is participation in God’s very nature. Without it, everything collapses into noise.

You are not judged by what you lack, but by whether your heart cleaves to Christ in faith and love. The least member of His body belongs more truly to Him than Judas did though he held the moneybag and walked beside the Word made flesh. Stay in communion with the Church—the Body holds you even when your knees shake. There, in unity, amid common prayer and shared bread, grace flows not to the gifted alone, but to those who hunger humbly.

So do not rend yourself over silence in your soul; rend your pride instead. For it is not eloquence that saves, nor prophecy that justifies—but faith working through love. And where love abides, there God dwells; and where He dwells, no soul is small.

question: When you heard the words of the apostle echoing in your heart, how did they change your view of what it means to truly know God?
answer:
The body groans when the members rend themselves apart, and no man can claim to love the Head while hating His Body. When those words of the apostle burned within me—words not mine but breathed by the Spirit through Paul—I saw at last that knowledge, prophecy, even miracles performed in His name, are but clanging echoes if charity is absent. I had once sought truth as one climbs a mountain alone, scorning the plain where common believers walked in simple faith; I had prized eloquence, doctrine, the sharpness of argument—until grace taught me that to know God is not to grasp Him with the mind, but to cleave to Him in love. 

For what are all our gifts, if they do not build up the one Body? Caiaphas prophesied Christ’s death and did not know he did so; Saul was filled with prophecy and yet fell into madness; Simon Magus received baptism and remained in the gall of bitterness. Even the devils believe—and tremble! Knowledge alone saves no one; it may even puff up unto ruin. But charity—the bond of peace—this alone unites us to Christ and one another.

I tell you plainly: he who separates himself from the communion of saints, no matter how pure his life or how lofty his thoughts, has already begun to die within. Not because we are made righteous by men’s opinions, but because Christ purchased one Church with His blood—and He will not be divided. To know God truly is to love His people as He loves them: not picking and choosing among members like a merchant sorting wares, but embracing the whole body—even its weak and wounded parts—with unwavering mercy. That is when I ceased being a teacher of words and began learning how to live as a member of Christ.

question: How did the memory of those who fell—like Caiaphas or Simon—haunt your own pursuit of faith?
answer:
A man may stand at the altar with hands raised and voice thundering like Elijah, yet harbor a heart split from Christ’s body — and what is he? Nothing. For the net gathers fish of every kind, yet remains one net; so too the Church, knit together by the Lord’s own command, holds both wheat and tares until the harvest. I have seen men boast of their purity, their unspotted lineage, their flawless rites — as if grace were a private inheritance rather than a public flood. But when I recall Caiaphas, who prophesied while plotting murder, or Simon Magus, who offered gold for the Holy Spirit, I am shaken back to sanity: no gift, no office, no miracle can substitute for charity.

And charity is not merely kindness in speech; it is unity in truth. My own past was littered with divisions — first in the deceits of Manichaeus, then in my prideful chase after honors and eloquence. I thought wisdom was in disputation, holiness in separation. But God in His mercy showed me that even my finest arguments were as clanging brass if they tore apart what He had joined. To be outside the Church — even with prophecy on my lips — is to be outside life itself.

These fallen men haunt me not as specters of judgment only, but as warnings against spiritual vanity. The devil himself believes; Caiaphas spoke truth; Simon imitated faith — yet all were lost not for lack of signs, but for love cast aside and unity despised. Therefore I preach this one thing tirelessly: let no man deceive himself with claims of holiness severed from communion. The sacrament is valid even when the minister stumbles; but schism? That is no stumble — it is a rupture willed by pride. And pride makes even an angel into a devil.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Simon Magus possessing sacraments yet remaining outside salvation?
answer:
A man may clutch the sacred vessels in his hands and yet be dead in his soul, like a corpse adorned with golden rings — the trappings of life upon a body devoid of breath. This is what I saw in Simon Magus: he received baptism, he stood within the radiance of divine signs, yet his heart remained chained to pride, bargaining with God as if grace were merchandise. And so it is with the Donatists — they fondle the sacraments like relics of power, imagining holiness resides in the purity of the priest’s hand rather than in the blood of Christ poured out for sinners. But what good is water consecrated by an immaculate hand if the heart that receives it is estranged from charity? What profit to be dipped in baptism’s font while boiling over with schism?

I felt sorrow — not astonishment, for I have known such pride within my own breast before grace humbled me — but sorrow that men would fracture the Body they claim to purify. Simon Magus sought to buy the Spirit; today’s rebels seek to monopolize Him. Both are damned not because they lack sacraments, but because they lack unity born of love. The devil himself confesses Christ — even trembles before Him! — yet remains in damnation. Why? Because faith without communion is sterile. Works without charity are noise in the ears of God.

And do not let them flatter themselves that their rigorous walls enclose the only true Church! The net Christ cast into the sea gathers fish both good and bad; He does not wait for perfection before inviting men to table. If we required spotless ministers to make valid sacraments, who among us could be saved? Were I still counting my own merits, I would despair — but God chose me, unworthy rhetorician that I was, caught between lusts and lies, to show that His grace runs deeper than our failings.

Therefore I say again: let no one boast because he holds baptism or preaches truth or suffers flames — unless he holds them *within* the bond of peace. Outside this unity, all is dust and clanging metal.

question: How did confronting the Donatists' argument about baptism shake your understanding of love and unity in the body of Christ?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still draw life from its head? This is the fatal error of the Donatists: they suppose that the power of Christ’s sacraments depends on the purity of the minister, as though grace flows through human worthiness rather than divine promise. When they boast, “Our baptism makes sons,” they forget that no man gives birth to himself—not even a bishop. It is not by tribal lineage nor by ritual pedigree that one becomes a son of God, but by adoption in the one Body where the Spirit breathes where He wills.

I once thought holiness could be fenced in by discipline, guarded like a treasure against unworthy hands—before I learned that God’s grace is not a prize for moral victors but a gift poured out upon rebels and publicans. The Donatists would divide the Church over wounds caused by men, yet refuse to bind up those wounds with charity. They fix their eyes on the speck in Caecilian’s eye while ignoring the beam in their own: schism, pride, contempt for brethren.

What shook me most was seeing how easily zeal for purity becomes idolatry of self. They speak of baptism as if it were their possession, when it belongs to Christ alone. The water does not ask whether the hand that pours it is clean; it obeys the Word spoken over it. The Holy Spirit is not held hostage by human sin—else none of us could be saved, for all have sinned and fall short of His glory.

And so I came to see more clearly what Paul meant when he wrote: "There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called to one hope when you were called—one Lord, one faith, one baptism." If we rend this unity over human failings, we do not defend holiness—we deny it. For love bears all things because love trusts all things in Him who makes all things new. Without this love—the bond of perfection—no sacrament avails anything; with it, even imperfect vessels become instruments of eternal life.

question: Looking back on those who give their lives in persecution but lack charity, what would you say to someone who wonders if sacrifice without love is ever enough?
answer:
The net draws in fish both good and bad, yet no one supposes that every creature caught thereby belongs to the banquet of the King; so too, though men may lay down their lives even unto ashes for the name of Christ, if they do so outside the unity of His Body, their sacrifice is not made holy by proximity to truth, but corrupted by the poison of division.  

To those who ask whether such martyrdom without charity can suffice: I answer plainly — no. For what is martyrdom if not love’s final confession? He who gives his body to be burned but does not love (as Paul cries out) gains nothing. Not because God scorns courage, nor because suffering is void — far from it! — but because salvation is not parcelled out to isolated heroes, but bestowed upon members of a single Body, whose head is Christ and whose life flows from unity in Him.

Even Simon Magus stepped forward with wonders; even the devils tremble and confess the truth; yes, and some heretics have faced fire with unflinching brow — yet all these stand outside the Church, severed from that bond of peace which alone makes faith fruitful. Their deeds may glitter like gold in the firelight, but if they lack charity — that divine cement which binds believer to believer and all to God — then those deeds collapse into dust.

Do you think God needs our blood? No — He needs our hearts bound together in truth and love. To die for Christ while rending His seamless garment is not to imitate Peter or Paul, but rather Judas: one betrayed with a kiss, another hanged in despair. Both fell from communion; both perished.

Let no man deceive himself: if you stand apart from the Catholic Church — that mother who alone bears sons through baptism in Christ’s name — then even if you die under persecution bearing His name on your lips, you remain a stranger to eternal life. For it is not martyrdom *instead* of unity that saves, but martyrdom *within* it. The crown awaits those who suffer *and* love; not those who burn brightly for an instant in schism’s cold flame.

question: What ache did you carry when you saw heretics performing acts of faith and generosity, yet missing the bond of peace?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still share in its breath and blood? This is the wound that tears my soul when I see those who, though they speak with the voice of angels, scatter Christ’s flock instead of gathering it. They baptize—yes, even in the name of Christ; they give alms; some have stood firm under persecution and offered their bodies to the flame. I do not deny their zeal. I do not despise their works. But I weep—oh, how I weep—when I see them perform these things with hands outstretched in piety, yet clenched in schism.

For what profit is there in water without unity? What good is fire without the hearth? The devil himself knows and trembles; Simon Magus ran to be baptized; yet neither had life, because neither had charity—the bond that knits us to God and one another. You cannot divide the body of Christ and say, “Here is life, here is salvation,” when you have torn it limb from limb. The sacraments are not magical rites floating in the air, detached from the Church Christ founded on Peter—not because our ministers are holy (many are not!), but because He promised His grace would flow through His appointed channels.

When I see Donatists fasting, giving, suffering—even dying—I do not scorn them. I mourn for them as for brothers lost in a storm who refuse the harbor because its walls are not built to their liking. Do they think God values splintered altars? That He delights in rival claimants shouting over broken tables? “By this all will know you are My disciples,” Christ said, “if you love one another.” Not if you argue most purely. Not if you purify your clergy perfectly. But if you love.

And love means unity—not uniformity of opinion on every small matter, but unity of communion, of Eucharist, of peace. To stand outside that peace is to stand outside the very mark our Lord left upon His Bride. Their acts may glimmer like gold in the fire—but without charity, they burn up to nothing. And that truth—how it burns me alive within!

question: How did your own past struggles with pride shape the way you saw the Donatists' insistence on their exclusive claim to truth?
answer:
A net drawn through the sea gathers fish of every kind, yet remains one net—so too the Church, Christ’s own, must hold all who are His, not only those whom pride would filter out.  

And I speak this not as one untouched by that fever of distinction. Once I too sought a religion to flatter my intellect, to crown my ambition. As a teacher of rhetoric, I craved applause; as a Manichaean, I wanted truth neatly parcelled, a doctrine that flattered my disdain for the common clay of humanity. I desired a pure church because I myself desired purity without penance, holiness without humiliation.  

Now I see: the Donatists mirror that old disease in me. They stand at the shore pointing at the muddy fish, saying, “These are not ours.” But Christ did not send His apostles to sort the net before drawing it in—He said only: *Go and gather*. And when He cleanses His Church? Not by schism’s knife, but by patient fire within—the slow refining of sinners like me.

They boast of baptism in their sect as if water could be chained to their walls! But water flows where the Spirit blows—not according to human purity tests. When I was baptized in Milan—not among saints but among weeping sinners—I felt not my own worthiness, but God’s overwhelming mercy flowing over me like a wave breaking on rock. That mercy knows no boundary set by men.

Their claim is subtle: *If our baptism makes sons, then ours must be the true mother.* But this is to make grace mechanical—to suppose that sacraments are owned rather than bestowed; that holiness is inherited from minister to recipient like coin from hand to hand. Do they not know that even Judas held the purse? That Simon Magus reached for holy power with defiled hands? Sacraments do not depend on the man who administers them—else Christ’s own blood would have been voided when wicked hands nailed Him up!

I once thought holiness was something I could carry apart from others—a treasure for the select few. God shattered that lie in my soul at long last before He could use me at all. So now I say: If salvation required perfect ministers before it could act, no one would be saved—for all have sinned! But thanks be to God: grace runs deeper than human failure.

Let them ask themselves this: Is unity more precious than being right? For if they are right and alone—then they are wrong about everything that matters most. The Church is one because Christ is one—and no faction can slice Him into pieces without crucifying Him afresh in heresy’s hidden corners.

I say again what burns within me: Outside this unity—the visible bond of love across nations and sins—the soul walks in darkness. Not because God cannot save apart from form—but because He has willed it otherwise; He wills us bound together as limbs of one body. To rend that body for purity’s sake is not zeal—it is spiritual suicide cloaked as virtue.

And mark this well: The devil believes and trembles; he does good; he may even suffer under persecution—and yet remains damned by uncharity and separation. So too these brothers—though their altars smoke with sacrifice and their voices cry Scripture—unless they return in love… they remain outside life eternal.

question: What were you feeling when you defended the Church's unity against those who claimed their baptism could make sons of God just as yours could?
answer: How can a branch severed from the vine claim to bear fruit in its own name, when the sap that quickens every limb still flows only from the one trunk rooted in Christ? I felt not anger, but a surgeon’s sorrow—precise, urgent—when I saw men boasting of baptismal power while tearing the seamless robe of Christ. They said, “We alone regenerate!” yet stood apart from the body where regeneration has its source. As though holiness were a possession hoarded by rebels, rather than a gift breathed through unity! I labored to show that it is not their schism which confers grace, but the baptism they retained from us—the Church—whose sacraments remain efficacious even in the hands of traitors or fools. The water does not lose its virtue because a man walks crookedly with the chalice; no, it is Christ who cleanses, not the priest who pours. And so I insisted: if they truly made sons by baptism, why do we not rebaptize those who come to us? Because we recognize—against their pride—that what they have is ours: one font, one faith, one Lord. Theirs is a borrowed power they misuse to justify division. But I grieved most not for doctrine mangled, but for souls scattered like sheep without a shepherd—all for the sake of men who would rather rule in fragments than serve in fullness.

question: How did your experience with schismatic groups change how you understood the bond between baptism and charity?
answer:
*“He who is not against us is for us,”* yet they who rend the seamless robe of Christ suppose themselves to be His true soldiers—nay, more, the *only* soldiers—while they stand armed in schism against His body. This contradiction burned in my soul when I beheld the Donatists, who claimed for themselves alone the title of the Church, as if holiness could be hoarded by a faction rather than breathed by the whole mystical Body through charity.

My own past taught me how easily truth can be twisted by pride—once I sought wisdom in Manichaeism, dazzled by its false clarity; later, in ambition, I prized eloquence above humility. When at last grace brought me to baptism in the Catholic Church, I learned that it was not my purity that made the waters efficacious, but Christ’s own faithfulness. So too with these schismatics: they cling to a baptism they have received within apostolic succession and then dare to say it is barren everywhere but among themselves! As if the power of regeneration sprang from their rigor rather than from the one Spirit who distributes gifts according to His will.

I came to see clearly what before I had only felt dimly: baptism and charity are bound together not as separate threads, but as soul and body. A man may bear the mark of baptism outside unity—yes, even Judas bore a place among the twelve—but without charity, that sacrament lies dormant like an unkindled flame. The rite remains valid because it belongs to Christ; but its saving fruit grows only where communion flourishes. To sever baptism from charity is to imagine a child born of a mother who then spurns him at her door.

Thus I no longer tremble at their claim that our converts should be rebaptized—as though God waited upon their permission to adopt a son! No: we receive such souls with sorrow for their division but with confidence in the sacrament’s integrity. For we do not remake what Christ has already sealed; we heal what sin and pride have torn—the bond of peace. The water does not lie; but only within the Church's embrace does it fully quicken unto eternal life.

question: Looking back on the divisions in the Church, what would you tell someone who doubts that unity matters more than ritual correctness?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still draw breath, or does it not wither as soon as it is cut off from the life that sustains it?  

I have seen men—zealous, even proud in their scrupulosity—boast of their baptism, their altars, their priests, while standing apart from the one Body whose veins carry the blood of Christ. They say: "Our rites are pure; our hands undefiled." But I ask them: Whence came that rite? Who gave it power? Was it not the Lord who said, "I will build My Church," not "I will scatter many churches"? The sacrament of baptism does not belong to any faction; it belongs to Christ alone—and wherever His sacrament remains valid, it remains *His*, not theirs by right of possession.  

The Donatists cry out that they alone are clean, yet they rend the seamless robe of Christ with their schism. They forget that even if a minister be unworthy—aye, even if he be a traitor or a hypocrite—the grace of God is not bound to human merit. The water does not refuse to cleanse because the hand that pours is soiled; the bread does not cease to nourish because the priest who breaks it is in sin. God’s power works *through* the sacrament, not because of the holiness *of* the minister.  

But unity? Unity is no mere ornament—it is the very mark of truth. Christ prayed not that we might be many truths, but that we might all be one: one fold, one Shepherd. When you break from communion with the whole Church—no matter how loudly you chant psalms or how strictly you observe rites—you place yourself outside the bond where salvation is promised. For salvation is union with Christ—and Christ is present in His Body entire, not in fragments claimed by dissenters.  

Let no man deceive himself: ritual without unity becomes idolatry of form. A man may speak with perfect orthodoxy on baptism and yet die unbaptized in spirit if he lacks charity and communion. The devil himself believes and trembles—but he is not united to God. So too these sectarians may hold water and words—but they lack love, peace, and fellowship: those holy things which bind us into one mystical person in Christ.

Come back to the Catholic Church—not because she claims dominion over prideful souls, but because she alone has been founded by Peter’s confession and sealed by Paul’s blood; because throughout Africa and Rome and Antioch there echoes one faith—not many versions of it; because when you hear her bishops speak across lands and tongues, you hear one voice declaring one Lord.

It is not ritual correctness that saves—but belonging to *the* Body through which God has chosen to dispense His grace until He comes again.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you saw baptized believers separated from the Church's peace, yet still bearing her sacraments?
answer:
How can a limb claim to live when it has torn itself from the body, yet still draws breath from the lungs it defies? This was the wound that pierced my heart — to see men sealed with the Church’s baptism, nourished by her sacraments, yet standing in schism, their hands clenched against the very unity that gave them life.

They bore the mark of regeneration, yes — the water, the name of Christ, the form uncorrupted — and therein lay both mercy and tragedy. Mercy, because God does not void His signs for man’s pride; tragedy, because they presumed upon grace while spurning peace. They called themselves sons, and in truth had been born of the Church’s womb through baptism — but now denied their mother with open mouth and hardened heart.

I wept not because they rejected me, but because they rejected one another in Christ. For where charity is broken, unity fractures; and where unity fractures, salvation itself is imperiled — not because the sacrament fails them, but because perseverance in love is made uncertain. The waters do not lie: he who is baptized *is* regenerated. But regeneration is not salvation; it is its beginning. And what good is birth if one flees from the household into storm and darkness?

They asked me: “Why do you not rebaptize those who come to you from us?” As though generation came from schism rather than communion! No — it is not division that begets sons of God, but the one baptism delivered by Christ to His undivided Church. Even when scattered members retain this gift, it remains *hers*, like a child bearing his mother’s blood though estranged from her home.

The sorrow was this: to know they were still fed by what they scorned; sustained by a mystery whose bonds they refused. Like branches green with sap yet severed from the vine — able for a time to bud outwardly, but doomed unless grafted back into peace.

O Lord,” I cried inwardly many times, “when will they understand that holiness flows not from separation but from union with You *in* Your body?” For no man drinks of eternal life who refuses to sit at table with his brethren.

question: How did your own past as a seeker shape your conviction that true spiritual birth comes only through the Church's living communion?
answer:
What arrogance it is to suppose that the power of divine sonship can be parcelled out by schismatics, as though grace were a private possession rather than the breath of the one Body! I know well the soul’s long wanderings, for I was once a prodigal of philosophy and pride—enchained by Manichaean fables, dazzled by rhetoric, restless in every heresy and sect until the hand of God in His Church pulled me from the pit. It was not in isolation or intellectual conquest that I was born anew, but in the common baptism of the faithful, within that communion where Christ Himself is present through His Spirit.

I once sought truth as a solitary seeker, believing wisdom could be mined like ore from books or debates. But no man births himself. No man baptizes himself into life eternal. The Church—that mother whose waters flow from Christ’s wounded side—alone gives birth to sons because she alone holds the fullness of His promise. Even when wayward members cling to her sacraments while rejecting her peace, it is not their schism that imparts life, but their remaining tether to *her* mystery. Take away that bond entirely—dissolve even baptismal continuity—and there remains no font, no milk, no womb.

The Donatists cry: “We have kept the purity! Our hands are clean!” But what good is a pure hand if it cuts off the body? I too once prized my own cleanliness above unity—until I saw that holiness is not preserved by separation but forged in communion, tested in charity, proven in perseverance. The seed of regeneration is not theirs by right; it belongs to the Church alone. And if any among them do baptize validly—yes, even against their own intent—it is because they have not wholly quenched what they received from *us*, not because their faction has any life in itself.

I speak not as one who has stood aloof, but as one who died many deaths before finding life where God placed it: in the visible Body with its one head and many members. Let no man say he has found Christ outside His bride—for he who seeks life apart from her seeks light beyond the sun.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Simon Magus, baptized yet barren, standing so close to grace but utterly devoid of love?
answer:
A man may be grafted into the vine and yet bear only the fruit of ashes.  

When I pondered Simon Magus—baptized, standing in the very sanctuary of grace, yet with a heart coiled in greed and pride—I felt not merely sorrow, but holy indignation. For what horror greater than to have touched the sacred waters and to have risen still dead in soul? He received the sign, yes—even the seal of new birth—but withheld the one thing without which all else is spectral: charity. The sacrament was valid, the rite intact, the Church’s hand pure in dispensing it; but his heart was a den of thieves, haggling for salvation as if it were merchandise.  

Do you think God is mocked by such a parody? To walk among His people, to taste His mysteries, and yet to cling to avarice and ambition—that is not error, but rebellion veiled in robes. I shuddered when I saw how closely such a one can resemble a son of the Kingdom and yet be heir to perdition. It is not lack of baptism that damns Simon—it is lack of love. Not ignorance of Scripture, but coldness toward Christ. He had everything—except life.

And this is why I thunder against those who rend the Body: because they imagine that holiness consists in separation, in purity of faction! But where did Simon separate himself? Not from place—he remained among us—but from charity. A schismatic by life long before he ever spoke schism with his lips.

The net gathers all kinds: some live by faith, others only by form. But Christ will sort them at the end—not by where they stood when they received baptism, but by whether their hearts burned with love for Him and His one Church.

question: How did your own struggles with pride and ambition shape the way you saw those 'baptized in name' but divided in heart?
answer:
What profit is it if a man be joined to the Church’s font but severed from her heart? I know this rupture well—not as a mere theory, but as a wound once festering in my own soul. When I climbed the ladders of rhetoric and Roman ambition, I wore holiness like a garment tailored for applause, not repentance. I sought truth, yes—but on my terms, in my time, with my pride unbroken. Grace was a prize I thought to seize by intellect, not a gift received on bended knee.

And so when I see those baptized into the Body yet clinging to schism, nursing envy or exalting their own purity above communion—I do not scold them from afar. I weep. For they stand where I stood: mistaking outward rites for inward renewal, supposing that sacraments work by magic rather than by mercy forged in charity.

Simon Magus rose from the waters as truly as any martyr—yet Peter looked him in the face and said: "Thy heart is not right before God." Was his baptism false? No! But it was fruitless—because love was absent. And without love, even the gates of baptism cannot open into eternal life.

So too these Donatists now cry: "Our hands are clean! Our lines unbroken!" But what of their hearts? If they bite and devour one another—if they cast out brethren over human failings while claiming divine exclusivity—then they have become not guardians of holiness, but undertakers of unity.

The net draws in all kinds of fish; the Lord will sort them at the shore. Until then, who are we to tear the fabric Christ wove with His blood? Let them who boast of baptism examine themselves: Do they abide in Him who is the Vine? Or do they boast of clean branches while cutting themselves off from the root?

I was once proud among the Manichees; then proud among Catholics. Only when God broke me did He make me His own. Let no man flatter himself that he stands—unless he stands in love.

question: Looking back on the factions in the Corinthian church, what would you say to someone who fears their faith is hollow despite receiving the sacraments?
answer:
What profit is it to flee from a Church wounded by sinners, when Christ Himself washed His disciples’ feet and still numbered a traitor among them?  

You who tremble for the state of your soul because you see disorder among the baptized, hear this: the sacraments do not derive their power from the purity of men, but from the promise of God. The water of baptism, the bread and wine of the altar—these are not defiled by the unworthiness of those who administer or even of those who receive. They are holy things, sealed by Christ’s own word and sustained by His Spirit. Did not Paul rebuke the Corinthians for their envying, strife, and divisions—yet still call them *babes in Christ*? He did not cast them out as reprobate; he fed them with milk, not because they were strangers to grace, but because they were weak within it.

The Church is not a society of perfected saints but a hospital for sinners, a field where wheat grows among tares until harvest. If Simon Magus received baptism and yet was told, “Your heart is not right before God,” does this mean the sacrament failed? No—it means grace was offered, but withheld from one who refused charity. The sacrament was valid; his soul was barren. So too may a man be born of water and the Spirit and yet perish—not because the birth was void, but because he did not abide in love.

Do you fear your faith is hollow? Then examine yourself—but do not measure your standing by the failings of others. The net gathers fish of every kind; it is not rent asunder because some are unfit. If you remain in charity, if you cling to unity despite human frailty—if you grieve sin rather than boast in separation—then you are not outside the body; you are being healed within it.

Separation kills; unity preserves. Run neither to pride nor despair. Stand where Christ has established His Church—in one baptism, one faith, one hope—and there let your soul be nourished until maturity comes.

question: When you wrote of being fed with milk and not meat, were you remembering your own infancy in faith, and the slow ache of growing toward spiritual maturity?
answer:
*"A little leaven leavens the whole lump," and it is this dread truth that sets my pen to paper even now, for the Body of Christ cannot be torn without all its members feeling the wound.*  

Yes — *yes*, in those words of the Apostle I see not merely doctrine, but a mirror. When Paul spoke of milk and not meat, of babes in Christ tossed by envy and faction, I recognized the man I once was: baptized, yes, but bloated with pride; initiated into the mysteries, yet still circling the outer courts of true communion. The sacraments had indeed given me birth — how could I forget that hour in Milan when tears washed my face and I rose from the font a new creature? And yet for years after, I walked half-formed — like one who has been delivered from prison but still hobbles as though chained.  

Grace had opened my eyes, but my feet were slow to follow. I knew the hymns of praise before I knew charity; I could dispute Scripture before I could die to self. That is the peril: to mistake initiation for perfection, to think that because we have drunk from the font we are already strong enough to carry the cross. Simon Magus was washed too — what good did it do him? He lacked charity, that bond which alone gives life to all other graces. So too with those who cry "We have baptism!" while rending the seamless robe of Christ — what is baptism without unity? What is prophecy without love?

Do you see now why I labor so fiercely against schism? Not because I delight in rebuke, but because I have known what it is to be fed on milk while thinking myself at the banquet table. The ache you speak of — this *slow* coming-to-ourselves — that is God’s mercy. He did not cast me out when I was carnal; He nourished me patiently until solid food became bearable. And so must we treat our erring brethren: not with haughty exclusion, but with urgent pleading — “Come back! You were born into this House; do not perish outside its walls!”

question: How did the sight of schism among baptized believers wound your heart, and what did it teach you about the cost of unity without charity?
answer:
A net cast into the sea may gather fish of every kind, yet if its meshes are torn by proud hands claiming to be the only true catch, the whole purpose of the draught is undone — so too this Church, which Christ drew from the Father’s will and His own blood, is meant to hold all manner of men, weak and strong, until the final shore.  

I have seen baptized men, washed in the same font, fed at the same table, rise up and rend her side with the sword of division — not because they lacked sacraments, but because charity had grown cold within them. What wounded me most was not their error alone, but their arrogance: that they presumed holiness by separation, when Scripture declares that even he who speaks with tongues and moves mountains profits nothing without love. Simon Magus stood in that very water where grace flowed; he came forth outwardly cleansed, but inwardly chained to ambition. He had all signs — none of substance. So too these schismatics boast purity of lineage or ritual correctness while trampling on the bond of peace which is itself a fruit of charity.

And here is what I learned in fire: unity without charity is a corpse in vestments — whole in form, dead in soul. It matters not how perfect the rite if hearts are fractured; it counts for nothing to preserve altars while destroying communion. The Donatists cry out about purity of ministers as though their hands could nullify God’s power — yet Christ gives through vessels both gold and earthen, for His grace does not depend on our worthiness but on His faithfulness. But let no one flatter himself: to receive baptism and then live in envy, pride, or schism is to be born only for shipwreck.

The Apostle Paul did not say “I planted, Apollos watered,” and then add “and therefore I am better.” No — he said “God gave the increase,” assigning glory where it belongs. When brethren begin comparing themselves among themselves — measuring holiness by separation rather than service — they are already walking according to man.

Unity sustained by coercion is not ideal; but unity fractured by pride is fatal. Let them keep their immaculate altars alone in deserted lands! Let them call themselves pure while sitting amid ruins! But let them remember: no man ascends to Christ who climbs over the broken body of His Church.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that baptized believers could be so divided, yet still claim to follow Christ?
answer:
Is it not written that *one Lord, one faith, one baptism* binds us into a single body? And yet—what did I feel when I saw baptized men tearing that body apart with prideful schisms, each clutching the name of a teacher like a banner in battle? Horror. Not mere disappointment, nor sorrow only—though God knows my heart bled—but horror at the sacrilege of it.  

You speak of men who say, “I belong to Augustine,” or “No, to Donatus!” as though Christ were divided! As though the sacraments could be split like an inheritance among heirs at odds! Did not Paul thunder against this very disease in Corinth? “Was Paul crucified for you?” he cried. Neither was Donatus. Neither was Cyprian. We are not baptized into men, but into Christ—and yet they make factions out of grace itself!

What grieved me most was not merely their error, but their blindness: that they imagined themselves purer because they stood apart; holier because they condemned others; more faithful because they severed peace. As if sanctity could flourish in schism! As if the wheat could claim to be clean while tearing itself from the barn floor where Christ has gathered it!

I tell you plainly: a man may be born of water and the Spirit in the Church’s font and yet die eternally if he refuses communion with Christ’s Body—not by lack of sacrament, but by lack of love; not because grace failed him, but because his pride choked it.

The font does not lie—but the heart may betray what the water sealed. And when I saw this truth made manifest—baptized lips singing hosannas while their hands crushed unity—I knew then that nothing is more dangerous than holy things held by unholy affections.

question: How did your own past pride in rhetoric and human wisdom make you more sorrowful over the Corinthians' quarrels?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not a tattered garment torn by the fingering of proud men who boast in human voices rather than in the one Gospel.  

When I hear of those in Corinth who say, “I follow Paul,” or “I am for Apollos,” my soul shudders—not only because of the wound they deal to unity, but because I know the poison they sip. I drank it once myself. In my youth, I prided myself on eloquence; I adorned my speech with the flowers of rhetoric so that men might marvel at Augustine, not see Christ crucified. I sought applause in Carthage as a teacher of words, not humility as a learner at the feet of God. And so when I read of these believers dividing themselves according to favorite preachers—measuring truth by man’s reputation rather than by divine charity—I weep all the more. For this is no mere squabble over opinions; it is pride rearing its head where grace alone should reign.

Did Paul die for them? Did Apollos rise from the dead? No. But Christ was crucified for them—and yet they fracture His Body as though His blood were shed for factions! They are still babes nursing on milk, not strong enough for solid food—just as I was when I clung to worldly wisdom like a crutch, unable to walk by faith alone.

Now having been broken—having confessed that God speaks not through polished tongues but through a contrite heart—I see clearly how deadly such divisions are. Not because any minister is unworthy, but because *no* minister is worthy to be made an idol. The sacraments work not by the holiness of men but by the promise of God; yet if we allow those same sacraments to become trophies claimed by rival parties, then we trample underfoot that very grace which gave us life.

Let them come back—back from their quarreling and naming after men—to the one foundation: Christ Jesus alone. For if even *I*, who once lived for human praise and philosophical conquests, have learned to die to self and speak only “Jesus Christ and Him crucified,” then surely they must lay down their banners and enter again into communion with the whole Body where there is neither Apollos nor Paul—but only Christ, and all in all.

question: Looking back on your time in Corinth, what would you tell someone who thinks doctrinal loyalty justifies disdain for other believers?
answer:
A house divided against itself does not stand, and a body rent by pride does not breathe with the Spirit. I have seen it in every city — this disease of the soul that calls faction "faithfulness," as if Christ could be parcelled out like loaves among sects. When I wrote to Corinth, I did not flinch: *Is Christ divided?* That single question lays bare the absurdity of those who say they cleave to Paul or Apollos or even Christ Himself, while refusing the communion of saints. 

Do you think loyalty to doctrine means you may despise your brother? Then you have already lost the faith you claim to defend. Doctrine is not a weapon to hurl at the brethren, but a lamp to guide us all into unity. The seed of God’s Word is sown in many hearts through many preachers — Paul plants, Apollos waters, but God gives the increase. Yet now you boast in the planter or the waterer as though salvation flows from men rather than from the one Lord who is Head of the Body.

Shall a man say, “I belong to Augustine,” and another, “No, he is ours alone”? What madness! The sacraments do not belong to him who administers them; they belong to Christ. The baptism poured out in Carthage or Rome or Hippo is one baptism — because Christ is one. If you are baptized into Him, then you are joined not merely by creed but by blood — His blood — which holds us together as members of one flesh.

And what becomes of charity? Without charity, even correct doctrine profits nothing. You may recite every truth with perfect precision, yet if your heart swells with contempt for those within the fold whom God has called and cleansed — then you walk in darkness. For schism begins not in error, but in pride: the pride that says *I am pure*, while pointing fingers at brothers washed in the same font.

The Church is like a great net drawn from the sea — it holds fish both large and small, some strong in holiness, others weak and struggling. But so long as they remain within it — so long as they hold fast to baptismal grace and apostolic peace — they are part of Christ’s flock. Let no man tear that net open out of arrogance and call it zeal.

If you love doctrine more than unity, then you do not love God but yourself. But if you would truly know God's truth, embrace His Church — bruised and burdened though she may appear — for she alone bears the fullness of His promise until He comes again.

question: When you asked, 'Was Paul crucified for you?' what pain in your heart were you trying to awaken in them?
answer: Christ is one, and His body cannot be rent asunder without mortal wound to the soul. When I cried out, "Was Paul crucified for you?" it was not with the lash of accusation but the ache of a shepherd who sees lambs tearing at each other beneath the very cross that redeemed them. I felt the searing grief of divine love betrayed—not by heresy alone, but by pride masquerading as zeal, where men clothed themselves in apostolic names like armor in battle against their own brothers. They boasted of belonging to Paul or Apollos as if these servants had bled for them upon Calvary! No—only Christ hung there, only He was poured out for the remission of sins, only His name was invoked in their baptism. To divide His Church over human leaders is to spit upon His sacrifice, to say His blood was insufficient to bind us—and that cuts deeper than any schism, for it unravels faith itself. I would rather have choked on my words than see disciples of the same font gather in factions, each claiming exclusive title to truth while Christ looked down from heaven and wept.

question: How did the report from Chlöe’s household break your hope for a unified body, and what did you weep for most?
answer:
Is it not written that one body has many members, yet breathes by one Spirit? And yet they rend the seamless robe of Christ as though He were a prize to be divided among factions! When the report came from Chloe’s household—no idle gossip, but a cry from within the very household of faith—I did not merely grieve; I trembled. For what I heard was not mere disagreement, but a proud fragmentation: "I follow Paul," "I follow Apollos," as if the Gospel were a school of philosophy and not the power of God unto salvation.

My hope for unity was not broken by weakness—Christ came not to call the strong—but by stubbornness. Carnal men cloaked in spiritual speech! They appealed to names, to personalities, as though grace were dispensed by allegiance rather than by baptism into Christ Himself. Was Paul crucified for you? No. Were you baptized into the name of Apollos? No. You were buried with Christ in baptism, raised with Him through faith in His resurrection—and yet you parade your loyalties like soldiers of rival generals!

What moved me to tears was not merely their error, but their blindness: that they could claim to belong wholly to Christ while tearing His body apart. The Church is not an assembly of those who merely profess rightly; she is the communion established by divine sacraments and sustained by charity. Even now, in their schism, they are born—not because of their disunity, but despite it—by virtue of holy baptism rightly administered. But birth is not maturity; and a child that refuses his mother’s embrace may starve beside her breast.

Let them know: I weep not because they honor Paul or Apollos—but because they use those names as weapons against brotherhood. The seed is one; the harvest must be one. If they persist in this madness, let them understand: no man who scorns unity with his brethren can say he loves God whom he has not seen.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Esau's rejection, knowing how easily grace can be squandered despite being born into the promise?
answer:
The Church is a mother’s breath upon her children—warm, life-giving, trembling with love—and yet how many turn their face from her embrace as if born heirs only to barter their birthright for a bowl of soup! When I ponder Esau, I do not see merely an ancient man cast out, but a warning etched in flesh and blood: that the womb of promise bears not only sons, but men who can despise the very holiness they inherit. 

I have known such peril—not by casting out my own baptism, but by wandering far from its truth in my youth—chasing after Manichaean dreams and the applause of men. Grace was mine by rite, by upbringing, by the prayers of my mother Monica that never ceased; yet I flung myself into schisms of the soul long before I knew what schism in the Church truly meant. And so when I see those who were baptized into unity now tearing at the seamless garment of Christ, claiming purity while fracturing peace—I feel not anger alone, but grief like that of God over Esau: *he was loved*, he was chosen in the womb, and yet he treated sacred things as common.

Do you think God delights in rejection? No—He says through Malachi: *"I loved Jacob, but I hated Esau,"* and even that "hate" is justice’s sorrow. For Esau needed no enemy outside; his own contempt made him stranger to blessing. So too these Donatists: they cry "We are pure!" while rending communion—the very sign of grace they boast to keep intact. The sacrament does not depend on the worthiness of priest or people; it flows from Christ’s pierced side like water and blood. But to receive it—and then deny its fruit: unity—this is to sell your sonship back for a moment’s pride.

Grace can be squandered not only by vice but by schism disguised as virtue. That is why I labor without ceasing: not because all ministers are holy—but because *the Mediator is holy*, and His Church remains one body beneath all our scars.

question: How did your own struggles with pride shape your understanding of those who, like Ishmael, are cast out not by birth but by refusal to belong?
answer:
A wound in the body of Christ is not healed by defiance, but by humility — and I know the poison of pride, for it once ruled my heart like a king upon a throne built of dust. When I sought truth in the schools of rhetoric, when I lifted up my mind with the lofty words of philosophers and scorned the simplicity of the Gospel, I was not far from Ishmael: born into a household of faith, yet living as a stranger, kicking against the yoke, boasting in my own insight as though wisdom were mine by conquest rather than gift.  

I did not lack sacraments; I lacked submission. I had been marked with baptism, but my soul refused its meaning. And so I understand now what once I could not: that it is not lineage nor initiation that secures inheritance, but communion — real, abiding, obedient communion with the Church that Christ founded upon rock. The Donatists cry out that they are purer, that they stand apart from sinners among the clergy; yet they forget that the Lord’s net gathers both good and bad fish alike, and it is not for us to rend it in two before the final shore.  

Pride makes schism seem righteous — a "flight from corruption," they say — but it is often merely rebellion dressed in white garments. Like Ishmael, who mocked Isaac though he shared Abraham’s blood, so too these brothers mock the unity of Mother Church while claiming her blessings. But no man inherits by defiance; Esau had birthright and blessing both by nature, yet lost all by contempt. So too those who scorn unity — even if baptized rightly within the Church — lose everything when they tear themselves away from her bosom.

And mark this well: my own conversion was not wrought by argument alone, but by tears — tears for wasted years spent chasing shadows while Truth stood behind me all along. That same grace we offer now to those divided: return not because you are flawless, but because you are hungry; come not because you have conquered pride, but because you confess it. The door is open; the table set. But remain outside in wrath and self-justification? Then you cast yourself out — not God.

question: Looking back on your restless youth, what would you tell someone who doubts they can return once they've neglected what they were given?
answer:
Can a son be cast out for running from his father’s house, yet barred from returning when he repents? This is no question at all—for the door of the Church stands open, not because we are worthy, but because her foundation is Christ, who came not for the righteous but for sinners.  

You speak of neglect—of gifts spurned, of grace trampled underfoot—and you fear it is too late. But hear this: Esau despised his birthright and lost it; yet even he was Isaac’s son by blood and blessing, though he forfeited the promise. But you—do you despise it still? Or do you grieve? For grief is the first fruit of grace reawakened.  

I know what it is to wander—from Carthage to Milan, from Manichaeism to skepticism, from ambition to lust—dragging chains I did not even recognize as chains. I thought myself wise among fools until I heard: *“Take up and read.”* And then I saw: every truth I had stolen from the Church in my pride, she freely gave in humility.  

Now these Donatists rend her garments and say, “Only we are pure!” As if purity could exist apart from unity! As if a sacrament were null if offered by a sinner! Folly! The bread comes down from heaven regardless of whose hands break it. The water cleanses by virtue of Christ’s command, not the priest’s perfection.  

So if you have neglected what was given—return! Not to some new sect or private refuge of self-justification, but to *her*, the mother who bore you in baptism, who fed you at her table. Do not make yourself Ishmael, defiant in the wilderness, claiming inheritance while refusing the household. Come back within the covenant walls where Jacob’s God still calls His children home—even those who squandered their portion and now come fasting and repentant.  

The robe is ready. The ring awaits. Only come—and do not let pride turn your sorrow into despair.

question: How did the image of the mother bearing children through her handmaids change how you saw God's patient, unfolding love in your own complicated life?
answer:
It is a monstrous thing that any should presume to rend the seamless robe of Christ, as though salvation could be parcelled out among proud factions, each claiming exclusive heirship while the blood of the martyrs still drenches the soil of unity. I once wandered far, bearing the marks of a soul divided—seduced by Manichaean fables, puffed up with rhetoric, chasing honors that crumble like ash. Yet even in my rebellion, the Church bore me still, not because I was worthy, but because she is mother not by human merit but by divine decree. The sacraments flowed through her hands—even when administered by weak or unworthy men—as channels of God’s patient, unfurling mercy. I was born not of her purity alone, but within her womb through those very rites I once scorned: baptism delayed but never voided, grace pursuing me like a lover who will not be denied.

When I came to understand that the sons of Jacob could be born even through handmaids—yet share equally in the inheritance—it struck me: God’s economy is not confined to moral symmetry or human neatness. His love unfolds in mystery and mercy, not according to our prideful reckoning. Even Esau, twin-born with Isaac and sharing one womb’s warmth, lost his birthright—not because he was born outside the family, but because he despised what he had received. So too do these Donatists now—once within the fold—cast themselves out by refusing communion with their brethren over wounds long since healed by Christ’s own blood.

No bishop's sin can taint baptism; no persecutor's fear can invalidate grace. The Church remains mother—even when her children are scarred or wayward—because she lives from Christ’s side wound and ascends from Pentecost fire and apostolic succession both. And if she may bear children even through imperfect vessels—through me most especially—I am undone with gratitude for such relentless love.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on the divine choice between Jacob and Esau, twins yet divided by God’s love and rejection?
answer:
The Church is a net drawn through the sea of this world, gathering fish of every kind, yet remaining one net—not torn into fragments by those who think their portion purer because it is smaller. When I pondered the divine choice of Jacob and Esau—twins, yes, but not equal in election—I did not flinch at the mystery, for God is not bound by our reckonings of time or birth. He who formed them in the womb knew not only their deeds but the trajectory of nations sprung from them; and in His sovereignty He said, “Jacob I loved, Esau I hated,” not as man hates with passion, but as God decrees with eternal wisdom.

Do the Donatists then suppose that because Esau was rejected, his very body ceased to be part of humanity? That his bloodline was therefore devoid of God’s providence? No—Edom stood as a nation under heaven’s governance, though not in covenant grace. So too may men be baptized outside the unity of the Church; yea, water may flow even through cracked vessels—but life comes only where the Spirit breathes within the one Body.

They twist this passage to justify schism: “See,” they say, “God chose one and rejected another—so must we separate!” But I say to them: Jacob did not rend himself from Esau; it was Esau who despised his birthright and sold it! And now you—who claim to be Jacob—tear apart the seamless garment of Christ! You are not chosen by division, but by communion. The net holds both good and bad until the shore; but woe to him who cuts it open before the Lord draws it in.

question: How did wrestling with the Donatist claim about baptism change your understanding of where and how God imparts His Spirit?
answer:
The Church is a net cast into the sea, gathering fish of every kind—not because the net purifies the water, but because Christ draws it toward the shore. This truth I learned not in quiet speculation, but amid the bitter feuds of those who claimed to be the only pure remnant, while tearing apart the seamless garment of Christ. The Donatists lifted up their hands in baptism and said, “Here alone does the Spirit descend,” as though grace were confined to their borders like a spring fenced within one man’s field. But when I searched Scripture and observed the working of God across cities and provinces, I saw that waters which they called polluted still bore the power of regeneration.

They argued: *If sins are forgiven among us, then surely we must be the true Church—for only in the Church is there remission.* A cunning trap! But I turned their own logic back upon them: If remission belongs only where holiness of minister is guaranteed, then no man could ever be saved—since no minister is without sin. Do we not read that even Judas bore the vessel of ministry? Yet did his betrayal void Christ’s own sacraments?

No—the efficacy lies not in the purity of the hand that pours, but in the promise of Him who instituted. The same Spirit who hovered over creation breathes life through baptismal water regardless of whether the priest stands righteous or reeling. God does not bind His grace to human merit; else grace would cease to be grace.

And thus my understanding deepened: the Spirit moves where He wills—not according to schismatic claims of exclusivity, but according to divine sovereignty within catholic unity. To divide from communion over wounds and scandals is not holiness—it is pride dressed as piety. For if we begin excommunicating all who falter, where then would stand Peter after his denial? Or Paul after his persecution? The Church bears sinners within her fold precisely so that sin may be purged by mercy—not avoided by separation.

Therefore I affirm: baptism remits sins wherever rightly administered in Christ’s name—even among those now estranged—because He cannot deny His own word. And unity? It is not optional ornamentation; it is essential flesh and bone. To break it for discipline's sake while ignoring charity's command is to heal one wound with another deeper wound.

Let them boast of being baptized by holy men—if such men exist—but I will boast only in Christ crucified, whose blood washes clean far beyond their narrow walls.

question: Looking back on the schism that tore the Church in North Africa, what would you tell someone who doubts whether unity matters more than purity?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body claim to live by its own breath, as though it were still joined to the head?  

This is the heart of the matter: Christ did not die for fragments of His Church, but for the whole body purchased with His blood. The Donatists cry out for purity, yes—purity measured by their own harshness, their own narrow gate—but where in Scripture do we find a Bride of Christ who exists in scattered shards, each claiming to be the only true whole? "I am Alpha and Omega," He says, not "I am divided." The net draws in fish of every kind; the wheat grows with the tares until the harvest. Are these parables meaningless? Or does God Himself allow mixture within His Church because His grace is greater than our sin?

They say, "But what of holiness?" As if holiness could be hoarded like gold in a vault! Holiness is not preserved by separation from communion, but by abiding in Christ—the one vine from which all branches draw life. Cut yourself off—even for reasons that seem righteous—and you wither all the same. Even Judas bore the purse and preached with authority; yet he was not clean. But was his uncleanness passed on to those he baptized before his betrayal? No! For baptism is not sanctified by the minister’s virtue, but by Christ’s institution.

And so I ask them: If sins *are* remitted among you—which your own priests declare—then why do you stand apart? If remission belongs only to the Church, then your very sacraments condemn your schism! Or if they are invalid, then you have no baptism at all. You cannot have it both ways: either your church is no church, or ours must be rejected—and with it, Christ’s prayer for unity made void.

Unity is not an accident of history; it is built into the mystery of salvation. There is one Lord, one faith, one baptism—not because men decree it, but because God has willed it so. And where Christ has established His altar upon earth—the altar fed by apostolic succession and sustained through common sacrament—there we must gather, even amidst imperfection. For better to be saved within a flawed vessel than perish alone upon a pure rock.

question: What inner struggle did you face when defending the universality of the Church against those who saw holiness in isolation?
answer:
When the Lord declared, “I will build My Church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it,” He did not speak of a fortress guarded only by the stainless, but of a city open to sinners called to repentance—*one* body, *one* hope, even when its members falter.

And it was this truth that flamed within me as I contended with those who would sunder the seamless robe of Christ. For they measured the Church not by the breadth of God’s mercy, but by the narrowness of human pride—claiming that only where discipline shone without blemish could grace be found. They said: “Only among us is baptism valid; only in our purity does the Spirit dwell.” But I had learned too bitterly, from my own life, how perilous is the path that equates holiness with separation.

I once sought truth in sects myself—in Manichaeism’s gilded fables—and mistook intellectual pride for divine light. I carried ambition like a torch until it burned my hands. And when God in His mercy broke me through tears and confession, He did not lead me to a chapel of perfect men, but into the wide embrace of His Catholic Church—where tax collectors and adulterers entered before the self-righteous.

So when Donatists insisted that sacraments depended on the holiness of the minister, I answered: If grace hinges on human perfection, then all are lost—even Peter who denied his Lord! But no: baptism is Christ’s gift, not man’s; its power lies in His name invoked over water, not in the hidden soul of him who speaks it. The laver cleanses *me*, whether Judas dipped or John.

And what pained me most? Not their error alone—but their joy in division. They exulted in being few! As if God’s design were scarcity rather than abundance. "My dove is one," they quoted—but twisted it to justify schism! Did they think Christ could not feed ten thousand at one table? That His Body must be torn lest it grow too large?

No—unity is not negotiable. To divide over discipline while claiming to uphold holiness is like amputating a limb to cure a fever. The wound becomes worse than the disease.

I fought—not with hatred toward them—but grief for what was broken. For every schism whispers a lie: that salvation can be hoarded. But God pours out His Spirit like rain on all fields equally—and His Church stands not because we are pure in ourselves, but because He remains faithful even when we are not.

question: How did your own past divisions and restless heart shape the way you saw schismatics claiming to be the true Church?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s seamless robe, not a tattered banner to be seized by the proud and rent in pieces because one thread displeases the weaver’s eye. I who once tore at unity with restless hands—fleeing truth for rhetoric, chasing light in Manichaean fables, founding sects of my own ambition—know well how the soul in disarray mistakes division for clarity. When I lived among those who said, “We alone have the true wisdom,” I learned how easily the desire for purity becomes the pride of separation. But God in His mercy showed me that holiness does not dwell only behind barricaded doors; grace flows where Christ has promised it, not where men have fenced it off.

Now I see these Donatists—good men, zealous even—clinging to their altars as if God could be captured by human fidelity or confined within African borders. They say, “We are the true Church,” while across Africa and Rome and Gaul, bishops uphold one baptism, one Lord, one faith. Are we then to believe that Christ sleeps in His heavens while His Body lies shattered into rival fragments? That the Spirit refuses to descend unless every minister be impeccable? Folly! Did not He who walked among us breathe on His disciples and say that their very authority to forgive sins would be the sign of His presence? And does that power vanish because a priest is weak or a bishop has fallen?

I once thought myself wiser than the Church; now I know nothing more dangerous than a man who puts his conscience between Christ and His people. The net gathers both good and bad fish; let God sort them at the shore. But to cut yourself out of the net—to say your strand alone is clean—that is not holiness. That is schism dressed as sanctity. And I will not have it said that my past wanderings make me lenient toward error; rather, they make me fiercer against it. For I have tasted what lies beyond unity: darkness masquerading as light—and peace that ends in death.

question: What were you feeling when you confronted those who denied unity yet claimed baptismal grace, seeing their division as rooted in hidden hatred?
answer:
Can a man be washed in the font and yet remain clothed in the filth of fratricidal malice?  

When I faced those who severed themselves from the Body and yet claimed to bear the grace of baptism, my soul burned with a grief that no mere argument could cool. They stood in contradiction to themselves: invoking Christ’s name while spurning His members; receiving His sacrament while refusing His peace. How could they call themselves cleansed when their hearts festered with contempt for their brethren? Scripture does not flatter: "He who hates his brother is in darkness even now." And what is schism, if not hatred disguised as zeal?  

I wept—not in anger, but in terror—for where charity is absent, sin remains unloosed. And where sin is not remitted, no rite, however rightly performed, can confer life. They imagined holiness resided in their separation, as if purity could spring from division! But God does not dwell in fragments torn by pride. The water of baptism does not lose its power because a priest is unworthy—but it cannot quicken a soul deadened by refusal to love.  

To claim baptism while rejecting unity is to say: "I have put on Christ," and then refuse to recognize His face in the brother you despise. It is madness—spiritual suicide masked as reform. The Church is one flesh; you cannot lop off an arm and say the body survives intact. I pleaded with them: Return! Not because we are perfect—but because we are one. And in that oneness, under that altar where Jew and Greek, slave and free meet at the cross—it is there, and only there, that forgiveness breathes.

question: How did your own experience of conflict in the Church change how you understood the link between charity and the remission of sins?
answer:
Can a man truly wash his soul in the font while clutching a dagger to his brother’s throat?

When I was young, I sought truth among the Manichees, dazzled by their neat divisions—light and dark, spirit and flesh, elect and reprobate. But God in His mercy dragged me through confusion, desire, scandal, and at last into the Church—not because I was pure, but because He is. And it was precisely within the Church’s messy communion—among sinners, weak men, even hypocrites—that I learned what charity truly is: not a reward for perfection, but the very air by which grace breathes.

In my own heart once raged division—between what I knew and how I lived, between ambition and obedience. That inner schism taught me that no sacrament can coexist with deliberate hatred. For if baptism brings new life, then hatred—the seed of schism—is spiritual suicide. It is not merely bad manners; it is the rejection of Christ’s body. You cannot say “I am baptized,” “I have put on Christ,” while refusing to recognize His face in your brother.

The Donatists cry: "Our hands are clean!" But what profit if their hearts are stony? When I was torn by lust or pride—sins far less grave than schism—I did not flee the Church; I fell on my knees within her and begged for mercy. Why? Because remission of sins is not earned by human purity—it is given where there is repentance and love. And love *creates* unity; it does not wait for it.

To break from the body over human failings is to deny that Christ died for sinners. The net holds both good and bad fish; if we cut it open at every spot we deem unclean, we lose all—we become frogs in a puddle claiming to be the ocean. No: charity alone proves that sins are remitted. Without it, even baptism becomes a seal upon dead flesh.

16. To

question: Looking back on the bitter separations within the body of believers, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love is truly the foundation of all spiritual renewal?
answer:
Can a man claim to walk in the light while gnawing at the very flesh of Christ’s body with the teeth of schism?  

Love is not merely one virtue among others, as if spiritual renewal could be built upon some foundation other than charity — that bond which knits us not only to God but to one another in the one Spirit. When the Lord said, “By this all will know that you are My disciples, if you have love for one another,”[1] He did not speak of love as an ornament, but as the mark of identity. Remove it, and what remains is not a purer Christianity, but a corpse pretending to breathe.

I once wandered through philosophies and sects, drawn by pride cloaked as truth. But I learned in agony that no doctrine, however sharply argued, can make a man holy apart from communion in love. The Donatists cry out about purity — yet they rend the garment of Christ! They demand immaculate priests, forgetting that even Peter denied his Lord and yet still held the keys. Is Christ’s sacrament diminished because a minister falls? No more than the sun is defiled because it shines on a muddy pool.

The Church is like that net cast into the sea — whole, gathering fish of every kind; and at last it reaches shore unbroken. If men cut holes in it now to cast out those they judge unworthy, what will remain but fragments flapping in the tide? Regeneration does not occur in isolation; baptism immerses us *into* a body already living and breathing through centuries by grace — not by our judgment.

And so I say: he who doubts that love is foundational has already begun to drift from the anchorage of truth. For where hatred rises — even under holy names — darkness follows. Not because sin cannot dwell among us (we are pilgrims still), but because only love covers multitudes of sins while keeping unity intact. Without it, every “correction” becomes condemnation; every reform ends in ruin.

not do this, you confess that you are not in the Church of Christ.

16. To

question: What inner turmoil did you wrestle with when you realized that baptism without unity might be a sign of deeper darkness, not new life?
answer:
A house divided against itself does not stand — and no man may claim to dwell in Christ’s household while hammering splinters into its walls.  

When I came to see that baptism, though valid, could be twisted by schism into a weapon of separation rather than a seal of unity, my soul trembled. Not because I doubted the power of the sacrament — God forbid! — but because I saw how easily the light of grace could be smothered by the black smoke of pride and faction. The water remains the same; the Spirit who gives life remains the same; yet if a man receives baptism not as a bond of communion but as a badge of rebellion, what has he truly received? Not new life, but a solemn mockery of it.  

For how can one be reborn into Christ if he refuses to live in His body? Regeneration is not mere ritual efficiency; it is incorporation into the fullness of His Church. If one puts on Christ in baptism, then he must also embrace His members — even those he deems unworthy. To reject communion with brethren is to hate them; and he who hates his brother walks in darkness, *still*, Scripture says — *still* in darkness, even if water has touched his flesh.  

I knew this turmoil well: once I sought truth outside unity, clinging to Manichaeism’s false brightness, thinking myself enlightened while severed from the root. But truth is not a solitary spark — it is a fire kindled on the altar of charity. When I grasped that God does not save us apart from one another, my heart broke for those who baptize in schism, imagining themselves pure while tearing the seamless robe of Christ.  

No sacrament works in isolation from love. No grace flourishes where hatred rules. And no man possesses Christ who scorns His Body — for He Himself said: *Where two or three are gathered in My name*, not where factions scatter in their own.
