question: How did your own experiences of division and conflict shape your understanding of charity as the heart of unity?
answer:
What profit is it to claim the waters of baptism if the heart drowns in pride and the body lies sundered from its Head?  

I have seen, with my own eyes, how the devil loves to dress up division as holiness—how men take a true thing, like zeal for purity, and twist it into a weapon against unity, as though God's grace could be hoarded by a few on a hill in Africa while the whole Church groans in travail across the world. I once sought truth in sects myself—in Manichaeism’s hollow promises, where every doctrine was a riddle and every answer a delusion. There I learned that error does not always announce itself with vice; sometimes it wears the mask of rigor, calling compromise cowardice and naming schism fidelity.  

And when I came at last to Christ—when His Church washed me in her font at Milan—I did not enter because I found a congregation without sin, but because I found *the* Body of Christ, bruised perhaps by human frailty but still alive with divine breath. The Donatists say: "We are cleaner." But I say: *Who made you judge?* Did not Paul warn that even if I speak with tongues and give my body to be burned, without charity I am nothing? They guard their altars as though holiness were locked in stone and bread alone—but what is an altar without love? A table set in solitude, where one eats alone while the family feasts elsewhere.  

I myself have wept over those who left us—not for loss of power, but for loss of brothers. For every man who turns his back on communion commits no small error: he rends the seamless garment Christ wore when He died. You cannot hold fast to grace while spitting on unity; you cannot claim to love God whom you have not seen if you hate your brother standing before you. Charity is not some soft addendum—it is the very pulse of ecclesial life. Without it, all sacraments grow cold; with it, even sinners become instruments of divine mercy.  

So let them ask themselves: If their baptism were truly Christ’s—and I say again, there is only *one* baptism—why do they refuse His peace? Is He divided? Was Cyprian crucified for them? The Church endures because she is built on rock, not on our fragile judgments of one another’s worthiness. Let them lay down their swords of exclusion and come home—before they lose not only unity but charity itself.

question: Looking back on the pain of schism, what would you say to someone who believes doctrine alone justifies separation?
answer:
What profit is it to guard the letter of doctrine with fanatical precision, yet tear apart the body of Christ with sacrilegious hands?  

You who claim purity of teaching while dwelling in separation—I ask you: did Christ send Paul to baptize, or to divide? Did He commission His Apostles to establish sects defined by human judgment, or to build one temple upon the rock of Peter’s confession? If doctrine alone justifies schism, then every man with a private interpretation becomes his own church, and the gates of hell prevail against us all. But this is not what we have received. We received *one* Lord, *one* faith, *one* baptism—not many lords, many faiths, many baptisms according to the whims of men.

You say: “We hold the truth; why should we join you?” I answer: because truth without charity is a lamp without oil—bright for a moment, then darkness. The devil himself believes and trembles; he even knows the Scriptures. But he is not saved by knowledge—he is condemned by lovelessness. So too your orthodoxy profits nothing if it feeds only pride and severs communion.

And what communion do you claim? A unity only among yourselves—drawn not from apostolic succession or catholic peace but from mutual condemnation of those outside your circle! You number your bishops as if counting soldiers in a rival army. You trace lines on maps and call them boundaries of holiness. But where was this church before Donatus rose in Carthage? Was there no baptism? No grace? No salvation until your little flock hatched from rebellion?

No—baptism belongs to God, not to men; it derives its power from Christ's institution, not from the worthiness of the minister. A corrupt priest can still administer a holy sacrament—because it is Christ who cleanses in baptism, not the man who pours water. You make grace dependent on human purity—a doctrine that makes salvation fragile as dust in wind.

But worse than your error about baptism is your contempt for unity—the very mark our Lord left upon His Church when He prayed “that they all may be one.” You would rather be “pure” than obedient; isolated than reconciled; right in yourselves than united in love. And so you lose what you thought you had: for even if some truth remained among you—and indeed fragments do—it becomes barren when cut off from the trunk.

Come back, I urge you—not because we are perfect (woe to us if we claimed such!), but because perfection lies not in separation but in communion; not in judgment over brethren whose hearts we cannot read, but in humble submission to that bond which knits apostles with sinners and martyrs with penitents.

For what good is it if you keep every doctrinal point intact—but lack charity? What crown awaits those who win an argument while losing their brother?

question: When you spoke of brotherly peace as a bond that must not be broken, were you remembering specific friendships torn apart by these disputes?
answer:
Can a limb claim to be alive while severed from the body, imagining itself whole because it still twitches? This is the folly of schism: to suppose that communion can be fractured and grace yet remain intact. I do not speak merely of personal griefs, though God knows my heart has bled for brothers once united at the altar and now ranged against one another like soldiers on opposing walls. I speak of the Body of Christ—a mystery deeper than friendship, more sacred than memory—where each member belongs to all, and no man’s holiness may justify rupture in the fold.

Do you think Cyprian loved less when he wept over those who fled the persecution only to return with hands raised again at the altar? Or that Peter loved less when Paul withstood him to his face? Yet neither broke the bond for conscience’ sake—they corrected, they pleaded, they remained *within*. But these Donatists rend the seamless garment of Christ as if truth could dwell in fragments. They say, “We have baptism,” but what is baptism if not entry into *this* people, this unity established by Christ upon a rock?

I have seen men once dear to me—ordained with me, praying with me—now refusing my hand because I will not call them holy above all others. They measure purity by their own narrow shadow and call it light. But what is purity without charity? What profit is sanctity if it kills peace—the very peace Christ left us as a legacy? If they possess baptism—and I deny that they do apart from communion with the Church—they misuse it by making it an instrument of pride rather than humility.

No sacrament exists in exile. No grace flourishes where love has been uprooted by judgment without mercy. You ask after friendships torn—yes, daily torn—but behind each wound stands not only sorrow for a brother lost, but horror at God’s mystery profaned. For when we break unity, we are not purifying; we are *profaning*. The saints across Africa, across Italy, across Gaul—all confess one faith; all baptize in one name; all obey one Gospel: Why then do these men set up their own purity as a rival altar?

It is not friendship alone I mourn—but faith betrayed in the name of fidelity.

question: How did your struggle to balance truth and love in confronting heresy change your sense of what it means to belong to the Body of Christ?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still claim to live by its breath, yet refuse to rejoin its flesh and heart? This is the absurdity of those who say, “We have baptism, we have truth, what lack we?”—as though the very wound of separation were not the fatal sickness that renders all their gifts sterile. I once thought holiness could be measured by the purity of individuals, as if God’s grace flowed only through spotless vessels; but grace is not a private stream for the self-righteous—it is a river from the throne of God, and it runs through the one body He established on earth.

In my younger days, I sought truth as one hunts for treasure in darkness—alone, proud, convinced that doctrine rightly held was enough. But when I came at last to know Christ not as a philosophical conclusion but as Mercy enfleshed, I saw that to belong to Him is to belong *with* His people—even the weak, even the erring. The Donatists cry out for purity of ministers as though Peter himself had been without sin when Christ said to him, “Feed My sheep.” Did not Judas administer at table with the others? Yet Christ did not void His own sacrament because one of the twelve was a devil.

No—what defiles is not shared water poured in His name; what defiles is schism: that pride which places judgment before charity, division before communion. To say “I have all” while standing apart from the unity Christ prayed for at His passion—that is to have nothing. For even if you hold every truth like a sword in your hand, if love be absent—the bond of perfect unity—you are nothing.

Therefore I no longer measure belonging by outward perfection but by participation in that one Body whose head is Christ and whose lifeblood is charity. Truth without love fractures; love without truth deceives. But where both meet—in communion with the Church catholic—that is where men truly begin to live.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Esau’s rejection, knowing how deeply brotherly love matters to the soul’s fate?
answer:
Is it not absurd—nay, monstrous—that a man should boast of altars and bishops while rending the seamless garment of Christ? When I pondered Esau’s fate, I felt no mere sorrow, but a fire in the bones, for here is laid bare the lie of those who say unity is a shell and charity a sentiment. Esau was born of Isaac’s lawful wife, heir to the promise by blood and covenant, yet he sold his birthright for a morsel and nurtured wrath against his brother. What good was lineage when brotherly love had perished in his heart? And what of Ishmael? Born of Hagar, the handmaid—yet Scripture shows him weeping beside his mother in the wilderness, and God hears *his* voice. He is not adopted into the people by law or ritual alone, but preserved because even there—in that liminal space outside Sarah’s tent—there remained a bond tied not to status but to kinship.

So too today: these Donatists cry out about purity of priest and validity of sacrament as if grace leaks from a chalice touched by unworthy hands. Folly! The wheat grows among the tares; the net gathers all kinds of fish. Must Christ descend each time to bless our water and seal our bread? No—the power lies not in the man who ministers, but in the Lord who instituted. A thief may carry gold without being its owner; so may an unworthy bishop administer holy things without voiding their sanctity.

And yet they remain deaf: “We are clean! We are separate!” But what are you if you bite and devour one another (as Paul cries)? You may have baptism; you may chant psalms; you may claim apostolic succession like a title-deed—but if you lack love, if you curse your brother for wearing different robes or kneeling at another altar, then you are no more within the city of God than Ishmael at Isaac’s door.

Charity—it is not mere kindness. It is the very substance of communion. It is what binds us across time and tribe and trembling conscience into one body beneath one Head. Without it, even martyrdom is noise. With it—even those born outside our walls find seed from our Bridegroom’s hand. Do you think God cannot raise children from stones? He has already done so—from pagans, from sinners, yes—even from schismatics who repent.

question: How did your own struggles with pride and rivalry shape your understanding of what it means to truly love a brother in faith?
answer:
What profit is there in claiming the purity of the altar while trampling the bond of brotherhood underfoot? I, who once lifted up my voice in the schools of rhetoric to gain applause, who set my steps toward the honors of this world with a heart swollen with ambition—do you think such a man could have understood charity if God had not scourged him through experience? I bore within me the unquiet fire of pride long after my baptism; I sought to please men even as I professed to serve God. And in that crucible, I learned this above all: he who boasts of his orthodoxy while hating his brother stands naked before God, no more clothed in Christ than Cain was in innocence when he slew Abel.

The Donatists cry out: “We are the pure! We have not defiled ourselves with traditors!” But where was their zeal for unity when they scattered like chaff at the first breath of persecution? They rend the seamless robe of Christ and call it holiness. They measure grace by the worthiness of the minister, as though the sacrament were conjured by human virtue instead of poured forth from divine bounty. Fools! Did not Paul say we are all members of one body? If one member suffers, all suffer; if one is honored, all rejoice. How then can you say to your brother, “Depart from me, I am holier than you,” and still claim to bear the yoke of Christ?

I knew what it was to rival others—not in doctrine alone, but in spirit: to desire preeminence, to hunger for recognition. Only when God broke me with His mercy did I see that love is not proven by separation but by sacrifice; not by condemning others but by bearing their burdens as my own. The Church is not made holy because all her members are saints—she is holy because Christ has wedded Himself to her, and no sin beneath heaven can dissolve that bond. To leave her is not asceticism; it is rebellion cloaked in sanctimony.

He who calls his brother “dog” or “schismatic” without seeking reconciliation has already lost his soul—though he speak with tongues and work miracles. But let him humble himself and return; let him embrace even those he once scorned—and there, in that act, he touches Christ Himself.

question: Looking back on the times you felt separated from others in the Church, what would you tell someone who fears they’re beyond reconciliation?
answer:
The net of Christ drags through the waters wide, gathering fish of every kind, yet still some tremble as if cast ashore before the judgment, fearing their knot in the mesh has come undone.  

I know the ache of distance — not only when I wandered among the Manichees, chasing shadows of truth, but even later, when brothers in Christ stood divided over altars and ordinations, each claiming to be the pure remnant while calling the other defiled. I have seen men so obsessed with the purity of the vessel that they forgot whose blood fills it.  

Listen: if you are baptized into Christ, you are born of His Church — not by your own worthiness, but by His mercy. It matters not whether you were conceived in a lawful womb or born from a handmaid’s side; what matters is that you do not tear yourself from your brother. Esau lost his birthright not because he was born wrongly, but because he despised his brother and sold peace for a meal. Ishmael was cast out not because Hagar was a servant, but because he mocked Isaac and sowed strife. The wound was in the heart that refused communion.

Do you fear you are beyond reconciliation? Then open your hands to it — now. For even if you have strayed in deed or thought; even if your conscience gnaws at you like a dog at bone; *charity covers a multitude of sins*. And charity is not merely kindness — it is unity with the body. To reject it is to say: *I do not need this body; I can live apart from its veins.* But no member lives alone! The eye cannot say to the hand: “I have no need of thee,” nor can any soul say to the Church: “I have no need of thee.”

Come back — not because you are clean enough, but because He who washes His disciples’ feet still stoops for those outside the door. The wheat does not winnow itself from the chaff; God does that in His time. You are called now only to repentance and return.

And let no man tell you that grace dies where sin abounds — that is a lie whispered by pride disguised as holiness. Wherever there is sorrow for sin and desire for unity, there is life. There is hope. There is Church.

question: When you considered Ishmael’s exclusion, not for his birth but for his strife, did it remind you of moments you chose conflict over peace—and how did that haunt your conscience?
answer:
What profit is there in claiming the purity of one’s lineage while wounding the body of Christ with schism? Did not the same hand that formed Ishmael also form Isaac? And yet it was not birth but bitterness—that venom of brotherly contempt—which cast Ishmael forth, not from Abraham’s tent alone, but from the promise. I know this poison well, for I too once lashed out in pride, sowing discord where peace should reign.

In my youth, before grace humbled me, I prized argument over harmony, truth without charity over truth with love. I boasted in my intellect, mocked the simple faith of the faithful, and fled from the Church as if she were a cage rather than a mother. When at last I returned—not by my own wisdom but by tears and longing—I saw how every quarrel I had cherished had severed me further from Christ, who is Himself unity.

The Donatists now cry: “We are clean! We are separate! We are pure!” But what good is their ritual purity if they gnaw at the Body with teeth of envy and contention? They exalt their ordinations and walls and exclusions—yet lack what makes them Christian at all: charity. Just as Ishmael was not rejected because Hagar bore him, but because he mocked his brother (Gen. 21), so too these brethren are not excluded for past sins forgiven in baptism—but for refusing to live as members of one body.

Let them hear this: it is one thing to be born outside the covenant; it is far worse to drive others out while standing inside it. The Church bears in her womb both wheat and tares—not because she condones sin, but because she waits for God’s winnowing. But schismatics rend that womb before harvest; they become not tares among wheat, but wolves among lambs who claim to be more sheep than the Shepherd.

I speak not only as a bishop now—but as a man long haunted by his own divisions. Every time I chose quarrel over humble love—whether in words with friends or rebellion against God—I became Ishmael in spirit: son of promise delayed by strife. It was grace alone that drew me back into unity—and grace that now compels me to say: **No sacrament avails where charity fails**.

question: How did seeing the Church as a mother giving birth, even beyond her walls, change your heart toward those once deemed strangers to God’s promise?
answer:
Charity alone binds us to the Body of Christ, and without it, even the purest doctrine is but a clanging cymbal—this truth, once hidden from me in my pride, now stands as the very hinge on which salvation turns. I once thought holiness resided in the separation of men, in the purity of a chosen few who kept themselves untainted by the lapsed or the lukewarm. But when I came to see the Church not as a tribunal of the flawless, but as a mother groaning in travail for *all* her children—some within her embrace, some cast far off, yet all conceived from the one seed of Christ—then my heart was broken open.

It is not by proximity to altar or bishop that life is conferred, but by participation in charity. If a man outside our communion has been baptized into Christ—if he calls upon His name and bears love for his brother—he is not alien to God’s promise simply because he wanders beyond our walls. For did not Abraham’s seed produce both Isaac and Ishmael? One held the covenant, yes—but was it birth alone that made him blessed? No, beloved: it was obedience born of faith. And Esau, though sprung from lawful bed and patriarchal line, lost all by his contempt for kinship.

So too today: what avails it if a schismatic boasts of unbroken succession or untarnished rites while his heart seethes with hatred for his brother? Is not hatred death itself? And what harm if one born among dissenters yet loves the peace of Zion and prays for unity? Does God reject such a soul?

The sacraments flow from Christ through His Bride; their power does not wither because hands are unworthy. A priest may be corrupt; baptism remains clean. A bishop may be proud; the Eucharist still feeds. So too this mother-Church—she gives birth even beyond her visible bounds, not because men ordain it so, but because God wills that none should perish.

Thus I no longer reckon strangers by geography or party lines. I look instead for love—the sign by which we are known as His. Without that mark upon them, even those within our fold are exiles. With it—even those afar—are already drawn home.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Ishmael’s exclusion, knowing how deeply belonging shaped your own journey toward God?
answer:
*"One is not born into the household of God by standing apart in protest, but by entering through the door Christ established."*  

When I pondered Ishmael’s lot—cast out not for lack of birthright, but for the irreconcilable strife born of pride and flesh—I felt no triumph, only a piercing dread. For I knew what it was to wander far from the womb of Mother Church, chasing lights that promised truth but delivered only smoke. My own soul had been a battleground of division—Manichæan dreams luring me with philosophical pride, my heart restless not because I sought God, but because I sought myself in splintered sects and vain disputations.  

And so when I saw Donatists lifting up their hands in sanctimony, declaring themselves the only pure seed while rending the Body of Christ limb from limb, I saw Ishmael—not in contempt, but in sorrow. He was Abraham’s son; they were baptized into Christ. Yet both forfeited inheritance not by origin, but by obstinacy. The handmaid’s son mocked the heir; the schismatic bishop denies communion to his brother—and both are cast into the desert of separation.  

Do you think God delights in exile? No more than Sarah rejoiced in Hagar’s weeping. But justice must be served: unity is not optional ornamentation for the Church—it is her very substance. As Isaac inherited not because he was firstborn, but because he belonged to the promise guarded within lawful covenant, so too does baptism bind us—not to men's purity, which wavers and fails, but to the unchanging efficacy of divine institution.  

I wept then—not only for Ishmael cast into Beersheba’s waste—but for those who today still cry “We alone are Christians!” while standing outside the Ark where salvation floats upon floodwaters of blood and water. They have sacraments; they have Scripture; they even have martyrs! But without charity—the bond of peace—they are voices crying in a wilderness of their own making.

And if my past sins taught me anything, it is this: no man climbs toward God by tearing down his brother. True sonship comes not from claiming descent like dry bones in a tomb—but from embracing humility where water flows freely at last: one Lord, one faith, one baptism—and none cast out who return with contrite heart to their true Mother.

question: How did your mother’s fidelity in suffering change your understanding of who truly belongs to the family of God?
answer:
"Let no man deceive himself: the household of God is not built by the clamor of schismatics dividing bread among the rebellious, but by the quiet tears of those who endure for unity’s sake."  

When I beheld my mother Monica, that woman of unshakable prayer, weep not for her own sorrows but for the rending of Christ’s seamless robe—when I saw her kneel in fasting for my own lost soul, and later pour out her heart that even the Donatists might return to communion—I learned that filiation with God is not proven by thunderous claims of purity, nor by seceding into marshes of self-congratulation, but by this: the willingness to suffer *within* the body rather than tear asunder from it.  

She did not demand a church without sinners; she wept that sinners should claim to be above forgiveness. She knew—by faith deeper than philosophy—that Isaac was born not because Sarah was flawless, but because God kept His promise *through* her trembling womb. So too does baptism regenerate not because the minister is pure, but because Christ Himself washes sin away. The Donatists cry, “We are holier!” and in their pride become Ishmaelites—first-borns cast out from the inheritance—not because they lack zeal, but because they lack charity, that bond which holds brother to brother as joints hold bone to bone in a living body.  

Monica taught me that endurance in truth *with* others is greater than separation in supposed purity. To remain within the Church while calling brethren back from error—that is participation in Christ’s passion. To walk out and say, “I alone am clean”—that is the spirit which said to Jesus long ago, “We have no king but Caesar.” Abraham was father not only of Isaac but of many nations; so too does our mother Church embrace those who stumble within her walls far more tenderly than those who stand outside shouting “holy” at one another like clanging cymbals devoid of love.  

And so I say now: whosoever refuses reconciliation with his brother refuses communion with Christ; for where two or three are gathered in His name—not just those whom you judge worthy—there He is in their midst. Let them come down from their hills of schism and drink from this one fount: He died for sinners, not saints—and none are saints unless they first confess they are not.

question: Looking back on your years with the Manichees, what would you tell someone who fears they’ve strayed too far to be welcomed home?
answer:
*"He that is not against us is for us,"* and it is Christ Himself who speaks those words, not I — therefore let no soul despair, no matter how far he imagines himself cast from the fold. I know the labyrinth of error, for I wandered long in its shadows, wrapped in the fine garments of Manichaean wisdom, which now I see were but gilded chains. I sought truth as one blind demands light, proud in ignorance, deaf to the knock of grace at the door of my heart.  

Do you fear you have strayed too far? Then hear this: the farther a man has fallen, the more glorious his return when lifted by mercy. The Church is not a tribunal for the flawless, but a hospital for the wounded — and woe to him who would rather bleed in pride than be healed in humility! The Donatists say, *We are pure; we have not been defiled.* But what are they but Ishmael’s children — born of strife, nourished on contention — while Isaac lies within the promise?  

You ask if there is room for you? There is only room *because* you have strayed! The shepherd leaves ninety-nine to seek *you*. The father runs *before* the prodigal can speak. There is no sin that can outpace divine mercy — unless it be this: to refuse to return because you think yourself too unworthy. That is not humility; it is arrogance dressed as piety.  

Come back — not because you are worthy, but because He is. The font remains valid even if the hand that pours is trembling; how much more does grace flow when it meets a contrite heart? Do not let demons of scruple whisper that your past bars you from peace. The blood of Christ cleanses not only sins committed — but sins imagined, feared, rehearsed in memory.  

I was once dust among dust, feeding on wind and lies. Now I am nothing still — except what grace has shaped me into by fire and tears. And so can you be remade: not by your strength, nor by your purity, but by unity with Christ’s body — where forgiveness breathes with every pulse and reconciliation flows like rivers from His side.

question: What inner turmoil did you carry when you realized that unity, not power, marks the true mother’s womb—the Church?
answer:
Was it not enough that the Church bore me in her womb through baptism, that I should now rend her flesh with the teeth of division?  

When at last the truth broke upon me—that the Church is not measured by the purity of her ministers but by the indelible mark of Christ’s sacraments—I was shaken to the marrow. For years I had sought holiness in separation, as if God’s grace could be hoarded like silver in a vault, guarded from the unworthy. Like the Donatists, I once believed that power resided in men—those who administered baptism, those who preached with zeal—and that if they fell into sin, the waters they poured became defiled. But what madness is this? That the efficacy of divine mystery should depend on human virtue!  

No—the font remains holy though a hypocrite stand at its edge. The bread and cup do not shrivel at the touch of an unworthy hand. Christ Himself feeds His sheep through shepherds both faithful and false, for it is His body that nourishes, not theirs. When I saw this clearly, my soul recoiled from my former pride. How many had I condemned? How many had I cast out with my rigid judgments, imagining myself guardian of a purity only God can bestow?

The true mother—the Catholic Church—does not labor to exclude but to embrace; her womb is spacious enough for sinners and saints alike, so long as they cling to her one faith, one hope, one peace. To refuse her unity over scruples born of our own wounded pride—that is not zeal for holiness; it is rebellion disguised as righteousness.

And so my turmoil: to have once stood among those who said, “We alone are clean,” while blind to the filth of schism—the refusal of brotherly love, the denial of common grace. To have mistaken division for discipline! But God in His mercy unknotted my heart with Scripture and truth: *One body, one Spirit, one Lord, one faith, one baptism.* In that unity alone is salvation—not in our self-made sanctity, but in submission to the whole Body which Christ Himself sustains.

question: How did your hunger for truth transform when you saw that even sacred promises could become chains if loved more than God?
answer:
The Church is the one Body whose head is Christ, and no limb may tear itself away without mortifying the whole.  

I once hungered after truth as a thing to be grasped, a prize for the clever, a crown for the eloquent. I sought it in philosophy, in rhetoric, in the glittering shadows of Manichaeism — and found only thirst. When at last I came to see that truth is not a doctrine mastered but a Person received, my hunger was transformed: no longer for knowledge as power, but for communion as life.  

And with that transformation came clarity — that even sacred things, rightly ordained and divinely given, can become snares when loved more than their Giver. The promises of God — of land, of lineage, of blessing — were never ends in themselves; they are signposts to the eternal inheritance: union with Him. When Esau clung to his birthright as a possession rather than a calling, he sold it for soup; so too do the Donatists clutch at purity like an idol, mistaking their sectarian pride for holiness. They rend the robe of Christ with scissors of suspicion and call it zeal!  

I know what it is to be born in concupiscence, to have climbed through my own Babylon of ambition and lust before grace pulled me down into humility. And I tell you: baptism is valid even when administered by a sinner — because it is Christ who washes, not the priest; because the promise stands firm on His faithfulness, not our worthiness. To make the efficacy of sacraments depend on human purity is to place creatures between souls and their Savior! That way lies not holiness but madness — Ishmael’s laughter echoing in the wilderness: proud, unyielding, cast out not by divine rejection but self-imposed exile from Sarah’s tent.

No — peace with brethren is not compromise; it is pilgrimage toward the land of promise. He who loves unity loves God more than his own righteousness. And he who breaks fellowship over human frailty has already made an idol of man’s perfection — forgetting that the Church grows like wheat among tares until harvest day.

The truth now feeds me not by filling my mouth with arguments, but by seating me at one table with sinners reconciled — where bread is broken and hearts are made whole only because we cease measuring worthiness and receive mercy instead.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that even sacred rituals could be embraced with a carnal heart?
answer:
The Church is Christ’s body, not a congregation of the self-righteous, and therefore it bears within it both the fruitful branch and the withered—yet all remain in the one vine. When I came to see that men might receive baptism with trembling hands and yet remain lovers of this world; that they might eat the sacred bread and drink the holy cup while their hearts clung to earthly things—I was not surprised, but pierced. For had I not myself once worn the name of Christian while my soul was still captive to ambition, to lust, to pride? The rites are divine; they do not depend on the purity of him who administers or even of him who receives. But grace works secretly, and not all who are baptized at once become spiritual.

I saw among the Donatists a fatal pride: they imagined that sacraments derived virtue from the holiness of ministers, as though a doctor’s skill depended on his moral perfection rather than his medical art. Fools! Do we reject medicine because the physician is a sinner? The water in baptism does not grow unclean because a man with impure hands pours it forth; Christ is its source. And yet—there lies the wound: many walk through these mysteries without conversion of heart. They desire peace, prosperity, victory over enemies—just as Israel under Moses desired milk and honey—but know nothing of longing for God Himself.

That realization did not shock my faith in the sacraments; it deepened my sorrow for those who mistake signs for substance. The old covenant itself was full of shadows pointing to spiritual realities: circumcision to inward renewal, sacrifice to atonement in Christ, land to eternal inheritance. But many then—and many now—see only what is tangible and fall short of what is promised in spirit. Therefore I say: let no man flatter himself because he has partaken of holy things unless his life bears witness to holiness. The net gathers every kind of fish; but when drawn ashore, it is sorted—not by what was taken in, but by what each fish became within.

question: How did your own struggle with earthly desires shape your understanding of the old covenant's limitations?
answer:
A net is not made holy by the purity of every knot, but by the hand that casts it and the will of the Fisher of men who ordained its design.  

I know well how the soul clings to the shore, dragging its affections through dust and shadow, mistaking cisterns for springs. I was once a man whose heart beat fiercely for honors that crumble, for friendships that fade, for knowledge that puffs up while love builds up. In my youth I sought God, yes—but as one might seek a patron, not a Father; as if grace were a reward for rhetoric, not a gift to the broken. That hunger for earthly confirmations—success, acclaim, even virtue itself as a trophy—was the very mark of the old covenant lived without spiritual discernment: circumcision of the flesh without mortification of pride.

Moses lifted up signs—blood on doorposts, manna in the wilderness, priests with golden plates upon their foreheads—but these were shadows pointing beyond themselves. When I clung to them as ends, I proved myself still carnal, even as I recited Scripture. So too today: when men boast in baptism or altar while hating their brother; when schismatics shriek of purity while rending Christ’s body—I see my former self. The old covenant was never defective; it was pedagogical. But to remain under its tutelage after the Sun has risen? To demand holiness from the vessel rather than from God who fills it? This is to mistake pedagogy for perfection.

The sacraments are valid not because we are worthy—who among us is?—but because Christ is faithful. Even when administered by unworthy hands, they convey what they signify *to those who receive in faith*. My own lusts taught me this: if grace waited upon my moral readiness, I would still be lost. No—the water flowed over me while I was yet grasping at muck. So let no Donatist dare say: "Our altars alone are clean." You are not cleaner than Peter who denied his Lord—or Judas who carried the bag! Yet Christ instituted His supper through both.

The Church endures this mixture until harvest: wheat and tares grow together; fish both good and bad fill the net. To flee unity over human sinfulness is not holiness—it is pride masquerading as zeal. I learned too late that no desire rightly ordered can dwell in man except through humility—and humility begins where schism ends: in submission to One Body, One Spirit, One Hope.

question: Looking back on your conversion, what would you tell someone who doubts whether inner transformation is truly possible?
answer:
The Church does not grow by the pride of solitary souls claiming special illumination, but by the humble submission of converted hearts drawn into the unity of Christ’s body through grace.  

I know well the man who doubts transformation because I *was* that man—restless, chained to the things that pass, seeking God only as a means to my own exaltation. I wanted wisdom without humility, truth without surrender. And so I say to the doubter: you mistake the beginning for the end. Conversion is not a single moment snatched from time; it is the turning of the whole soul—reluctant, weeping, stumbling—from death to life, from division to communion.  

Do you suppose that because a man is baptized yet still sins, therefore no change has occurred? Folly! The wound remains after the surgeon cuts only if he fails; but if he begins to heal, though there be pain and pus and weakness still, who denies that life is returning? So it is with the soul. Grace does not bypass our nature—it heals it from within, slowly, often painfully. The sacraments are not magic; they are channels of divine life for those who receive them in faith and submission.  

And mark this well: even among those within the Church—the baptized, the communicants—there are some who remain carnal: they eat spiritual food yet crave earthly feasts; they claim Abraham as father but refuse his faith. The apostle rebuked such even in Corinth—men who had received Christ’s body and blood yet walked in envy and division. Not because baptism failed them—but because their hearts had not fully surrendered to its power.  

Yet this does not invalidate the mystery—it reveals our weakness! The water still cleanses; the Spirit still quickens; but we must cease resisting. Transformation *is* possible—not because we will it with great effort—but because God draws us by mercy stronger than our sin. I once sought myself in every truth I learned; now I seek only Him—and this change was no work of mine. It was grace laying siege to my soul until I yielded at last in tears at Milan’s garden gate.

So do not look for perfection as your proof of conversion—but look for desire set aright: sorrow where once was pleasure in sin; hunger for God where once was indifference; love for His Church where once was contempt or neglect. These are signs—not full sanctification—but *life*. And where there is life, resurrection follows.

question: How did the fear of desiring earthly things instead of God torment your conscience in your early years?
answer:
What profit is it to claim the name of Christian while setting up altars to our own righteousness, as though God’s grace were not sufficient for the whole Church? I know well the snares of the heart that would barter eternal goods for temporal consolations, for I once walked that path myself. In my youth, I sought God, yes — but only insofar as He might crown my ambitions, confirm my intellect, and adorn me with honors among men. I desired wisdom, but as a robe to wear; virtue, but as a reputation to secure; piety, but as a means to distinction. Even when I turned toward divine things, I carried with me the scales of earthly calculation — what shall I gain? How shall I be seen? My prayers were negotiations with heaven for worldly peace and acclaim.

And this was my torment: that whenever grace stirred in me a true longing for God alone, it was drowned out by fear — fear that surrender meant loss. To desire God above all things meant relinquishing control over fame, over pleasure, over the praise of men. It meant acknowledging that all my learning and eloquence were nothing without charity; that the soul must be stripped of its ornaments before it could cling naked to Christ. But oh! how the flesh resisted. The old covenant written on my heart at first was not one of faith but of bargain: serve God in order that He serve you.

It was only when I came to understand — through tears, through Scripture, through the quiet thunder of His voice in silence — that God is not a reward among others but *the* Reward Himself; not a means but the End; not a heavenly paymaster but Father — only then did my divided will begin to heal. The sacraments washed me not because I had earned purity, but because they are holy apart from our merit. And so must love be: given freely within the Church’s communion, not hoarded by those who boast of being purer than others.

To desire earth is not merely error — it is dismemberment. It tears man from himself and from Christ’s body. And no man who clings to fragments can see the whole Light whole.

question: What longing in your heart changed when you first tasted the spiritual promise behind the old shadows?
answer:
*"He that is joined unto the Lord is one spirit"*—and it was this very union, once tasted, that burned away the dross of my earthly cravings like stubble before fire. Before, I sought God as one seeks a rewarder of carnal desires: health, honor, success—blessings mistaken for the Blessed One Himself. The old shadows on the wall of the cave I took for substance; the rites and promises of bread and land and victory I grasped with hungry hands, not knowing they pointed beyond themselves to a Banquet in Heaven where Christ is both Host and Food.

But when grace pierced me—when I heard the voice behind me saying, *“I am the Truth you wearied yourself seeking in words”*—then I saw: those ancient promises were not lies, but veils. The land flowing with milk and honey? A shadow of the Church’s unity, where souls feed on divine love. The temple built of stone? A figure of this living Body we are grafted into by baptism, even when some members yet limp in fleshly pride.

Now do you understand why I rage against these Donatists who rend the seamless robe? They claim holiness by separation, yet scorn the sacraments when administered by sinners—as if water could be defiled by the vessel that carries it to our lips! No: the font regenerates not by priestly purity but by divine promise. The bread feeds us not because hands are clean but because Christ said, *“This is My Body.”*  

When I first tasted spiritual things, I did not lose love for God’s ancient people—I saw them fulfilled. The longing remained, but was lifted upward: no longer for things given *from* God, but for God Himself—the eternal Light in which all shadows dissolve.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about those caught between the old and new covenants, so near to grace yet not fully living in it?
answer:
The Church is a great net drawn through the sea of this world, gathering fish of every kind—some already leaping toward the light, others still dragging in the mud of their own reluctance, yet all held in the one mesh of divine intention. When I wrote of those suspended between the old and new covenants, my heart was not cold with judgment but burning with grief—for I knew such men well: not strangers, but echoes of myself before grace overtook me.  

I had been one who lingered at the threshold, baptized yet bound by habit, clothed in the robe of the new man and still clinging to the rags of the old. These are not aliens outside the fold; they are within the sacrament, marked by water and sealed by name, and yet their lives do not breathe the Spirit. They eat at the Lord’s table and live as though they were guests at a common meal.  

Do you think this does not wound me? To see souls enclosed in Christ’s net—baptized into His death—and yet refusing to rise into His life? The apostle weeps for such; so too do I. For if holiness is lacking among us—not because we are sinners still struggling upward, but because we have ceased even to desire ascent—then what have we become? A body divided against itself, limbs refusing to move under the head.  

The Donatists shout that only their hands are clean enough to consecrate bread—but Christ Himself said it is not what goes into a man that defiles him! The power lies not in our purity but in His promise. The sacrament works *ex opere operato*—by its very being—whether received by a Peter or a Judas. But woe unto him who receives without repentance! He eats his own condemnation while tasting grace.

And so my feeling was this: sorrow like a surgeon’s hand trembling above a festering wound that must be cut—but must also be healed. I would rather run after one straggler still caught in carnal ways than triumph over a church that boasts spotless altars while its members starve for want of love. Unity is our sign to the world; charity our law; hope our anchor—for many are called according to the fleshly rite, but few come alive by faith into spiritual understanding.

Yet even those who die half-awake in Christ shall find mercy—their names written "yet being imperfect"—because God sees not only what they are now, but what He has begun in them through baptism's holy claim.

question: How did your own struggles with spiritual growth shape your understanding of those who receive the sacrament but remain 'natural'?
answer:
What profit is it to boast of the font, if the heart remains untransformed by that charity which binds us in one Body? I know well the condition of those who, baptized into the new covenant, yet walk according to the flesh — for I was once such a man. Not ignorance kept me from God, but pride; not lack of zeal, but misdirected desire. I had the sacraments, even taught them with eloquence, while my soul remained captive to ambition and lust. Grace found me not at the altar alone, but in the wreckage of my own divided will.

So when I see those who claim the waters have cleansed them, yet harbor schism in their hearts or scorn for their brethren — men who say they are pure because they stand apart — I do not marvel; I mourn. The rite is not void because the minister may be unworthy; neither is it effective because the recipient is baptized. The seed lies dormant in stony ground unless the Spirit gives growth. Many within our churches live still as "natural" men, tasting salvation yet walking by old habits, consoled by sacrament but not converted by truth.

And do they perish? Not if they cling even feebly to unity; not if they resist division and hunger after God, however dimly. For even now they are inscribed in His book — *my substance yet being imperfect* — preserved not by their own integrity but by His mercy enfleshed in holy rites and holy communion. But woe to those who break that unity and claim exclusive holiness! They rend Christ’s garment; they make themselves strangers to the very grace they presume to monopolize.

Let them remember: it was not by separation that we were saved, but by joining — joining ourselves to Christ’s body here on earth, visible and whole, where wheat and tares grow together until harvest.

question: Looking back on your journey from confusion to faith, what would you tell someone who fears they are too bound by the flesh to ever receive the things of the Spirit?
answer:
What if the net were torn every time a fish grew heavy with grace? Would the kingdom of heaven be cast in pieces for the sake of those who stumble in the mud of their own making?  

I know well the chains of the flesh—how they clung to me like sweat-stained garments after nights spent in lust, ambition, pride. I was baptized long before I truly believed; I received the sacrament while my heart still rotted with double-mindedness. And yet—there was holiness *in* it, not because *I* was holy, but because Christ is holy, and His body does not defile at our touch.  

You say you are too carnal to receive the things of the Spirit? Then tell me: did not Paul himself groan under the weight of his members warring against his mind? Was he not *already* in Christ, even as he wrestled? The sacrament is not a reward for perfection—it is medicine for the sick, bread for pilgrims still trudging through Egypt’s shadow.  

The Donatists shriek that only spotless hands may consecrate, only pure lips may preach—but they make God a debtor to human cleanliness! As though grace were a mirror that breaks when smudged, rather than fire that consumes impurity. No: water washes even now; bread feeds even now; Spirit breathes even now—regardless of whether the vessel is cracked or trembling.  

Advance! Do not stand idle in self-condemnation, as though your sin were greater than His mercy. You are already marked by baptism—not as one complete, but as one *claimed*. If you die today still gasping toward holiness, you die within the land of the living, because Christ holds you by that seal which no hand may unwrite. Rise then—not when you feel worthy, but *now*, precisely where you are—and let Him make you spiritual who already declared you His own.

question: What inner turmoil did you carry when reflecting on those who die before becoming spiritual, yet are still held in God's book?
answer:
The Church, that one Body whose head is Christ and whose members are bound in the Spirit, cannot bear to think of any portion of her children lost—not because they fell into heresy or schism, but because they simply had not yet grown into full stature in faith when death came.  

And so, when I ponder those who die still infants in the Spirit—baptized into the new covenant, marked by the sacrament, yet not having advanced to grasp the deeper things of God—a certain holy anxiety stirs within me. Not doubt of God’s mercy, but awe at its hidden paths. For we know that the seed is sown among thorns, on rocky ground, and by the wayside; yet even where fruit was not fully formed, the sower does not disown his field.  

These souls—still wrapped in spiritual infancy—do not belong to the old Adam by malice or rebellion, but by immaturity alone. They have not rejected the light; they were caught up before full daybreak. And since they remained within the communion of the Church—the ark outside of which no one is saved—what audacity would it be to shut them from life? The very sacrament they received cries out against such narrowness! Water was poured in Christ’s name; grace was given; a bond was sealed.  

I have wrestled with this—not coldly as a logician with syllogisms—but as a shepherd whose heart burns at every soul that might seem lost at the margins. Do we suppose God’s book records only the perfected? Then none would be written there! No—the psalmist says He saw my substance yet unformed; He knows us before we are fully made. And if He writes us while we are still incomplete, how much more will He preserve those who died within His covenant household, even if their walk was halting?  

Let no Donatist or rigorist dare say such ones are cast out because their virtue was weak or their understanding dim! Are we then more exacting than God? The wheat grows beside the tares until harvest; and even half-grown wheat is still wheat. Let us not rend the garment of unity with our harsh judgments, nor narrow what God has opened wide.

question: How did your experience of God's patient call in your own life influence your hope for those who have started toward grace but not yet arrived?
answer:
What profit is it to the body if one limb declares itself whole while the rest are cast off as corrupt? I have known what it is to stand half-converted upon the threshold of grace—my will divided, my heart a battlefield, my mind dazzled by truth yet clinging still to the shadows. I was not yet spiritual, but already marked by the sacrament; not yet walking in the light, but already claimed by its radiance. And if God in His mercy did not reject me in those days—if He stretched out His hand while I still dallied with pride and concupiscence—how then shall we deny hope to those who are advancing, though slowly, within the fold of the Church?

The net gathers fish of every kind, and until the final shore is reached, we do not cut away the corners because some appear sluggish or half-alive. So too this one Catholic Church, spread across nations and bearing both wheat and chaff, holds within it many souls who have received baptism’s seal but not yet full illumination. They are *in via*, on pilgrimage toward spiritual maturity. The Donatists would rip them out, declaring them unclean because their shepherds were stained—but what says Paul? The power of baptism lies not in the purity of hands that administer it, but in the holiness of Christ who instituted it.

I myself was long such a man: baptized early, yet years passing before my soul truly surrendered. Had I died during those years—while still entangled in worldly ambition, while still hesitant before divine love—would I have been lost? No. For even then, I belonged to the new covenant by sacrament and desire; my steps were turning homeward. And so I say now: let no schismatic presume to bar the gates against those whom Christ has already marked with His sign.

Unity is not luxury—it is necessity. For outside this Body there is no promise; apart from this Vine there is no life. But within it—even for those sluggish, carnal, slow to believe—there remains hope through grace protected by sacramental reality itself.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on how the same spiritual mother birthed both saints and sinners throughout history?
answer: Can a single vine bear both wild and cultivated grapes, yet remain one vine? So it is with the Church—the Body of Christ stretches across ages, bearing within her womb both the righteous and the reprobate, not because holiness is confused with corruption, but because divine patience allows tares and wheat to grow together until the harvest. When I ponder this mystery—that she who nurtured Abraham also bore Judas, that the same mother who wept at the foot of martyrs also gave voice to Simon Magus—I am seized not by despair, but by awe at God’s sovereign design. He sees what we do not: substance yet imperfect, written already in His book. We tear at unity with impatient hands, as if purity could be preserved by schism; but God builds His city through time, gathering citizens from every age into one communion, knowing full well who are His. The Donatists scream for a church unspotted by sinners, yet forget that the laver of baptism washes sinners—not saints already made. The sacraments flow from Christ’s side, not from the spotlessness of priests. If we cast out every flawed vessel, what would remain but shards? No—He allows both to grow, for judgment is His; our task is fidelity in communion, not self-righteous separation.

question: How did contemplating the unity of God's people across time change how you understood His patience and mercy?
answer:
Can it be that God, who from the foundation of the world beheld every soul inscribed in His book—Abel’s blood crying from the earth, Moses trembling before the burning bush, Peter weeping after the cock crowed—can it be that He established His Church only to fracture it at the touch of human frailty? No, I tell you plainly: such a notion rends the seamless garment of Christ. When I came to understand that the mother Church bore not only Augustine, trembling with late-converted conscience, but also Judas—the false apostle—with his silver-stained hands; not only Ambrose, wise and steadfast, but also Donatus, swollen with pride—then I saw with unveiled mind that God’s patience is not passive indifference, but active governance over one household stretching from Adam to the end of time.

This is no mere philosophy; it is divine economy. He who saw my restless youth and yet wrote me into His book does not erase nations or saints on account of sinners within their fold. The tares grow with the wheat—not because the sower wills confusion, but because His mercy waits for harvest. If we were to purge every soul imperfect in deed or pastor deficient in virtue before allowing sacrament or fellowship, where would any of us stand? The altar would be deserted. The font dry. But instead, water flows and bread breaks—because grace runs deeper than our failings.

And so I rebuke those who say: “We are pure because we are few.” What arrogance! As if holiness could be measured by separation rather than charity. Do they suppose Enoch walked alone? That Noah shut out every drop until he stepped forth upon dry land? The Church is not a society of perfected corpses but a pilgrimage of living members—limping, repenting, hoping—one body across centuries, held together not by our purity but by His unbroken Spirit.

To reject unity over scandal is to mistake medicine for disease. The wound must be cleansed—but never by cutting off the limb.

question: Looking back on your own wayward youth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether they can truly belong to God's people?
answer:
Can a single branch, though it once bore wild fruit, be grafted into the true vine and not belong to its life?  

I was no model of purity, no paragon of virtue in my youth—I chased shadows of pleasure, dabbled in heresies that promised wisdom but delivered only pride, and forsook the quiet voice of grace for the clamor of ambition. Yet that same Church, which I once scorned and wounded by my absence, is the very womb through which I was reborn. Not because I was worthy, but because she is *mother*—not of the perfect alone, but of penitents; not of the already holy, but of those being made holy.  

Do you doubt your belonging because of past sins? Then hear this: the net draws in every kind of fish, good and bad alike—it does not sort at sea, but only at shore. The Church endures this mixture by divine design, not human failure. Were we to cast out every sinner at once, where would any of us stand? Peter denied; Paul persecuted; David murdered and fornicated—and yet each was called by name and brought back into the fold.  

The Donatists cry out against sinners in the Church as if holiness were measured by separation. But what madness is this—that a man's baptism depends on the purity of the priest who administered it? As if grace flowed from human virtue rather than from Christ Himself! No—the sacrament stands firm even when ministers fall. The wheat grows among tares not because God cannot tell them apart, but because His mercy waits for conversion until harvest time.  

So do not despair over your past—run to the Church as I did, weeping and worn. Enter her communion not as one who has earned a place, but as one invited by grace. For she is mother even to such as you—and especially to such as you.

question: What stirred in your heart when you considered that Judas and Simon Magus sprang from the same root as the apostles?
answer:
The net cast into the sea hauls in fish of every kind, some good and some rotten, yet all swim in the same waters until the drag to shore — and this is the very mercy of God, that He allows tares to grow with wheat, false with true, even to the end of the age. When I pondered that Judas and Simon Magus drew life from the same womb of the Church as Peter and Paul, my soul trembled not with despair, but with awe at divine foreknowledge and patient grace. For it was never promised that every hand laid upon the altar would be pure, nor every voice in communion sincere. Christ Himself chose twelve — and knew one was a devil. He washed Judas’ feet with the rest; He did not pull him out of the circle before the end.

Do you think this stirs scandal? It ought to stir humility. If from that holy root — nourished by sacraments, walking among miracles — there could still spring betrayal and sorcery, who among us can boast? But more: who among us can refuse communion to another on suspicion of unworthiness? The Lord permits wolves within earshot of the sheep because His design is not for a flawless fold in this world, but for a harvest gathered at dawn. The sacraments remain valid not because hands are clean, but because Christ is holy. Simon Magus sought to buy grace; Judas sold it. Yet neither defiled the mystery itself — only themselves.

I have seen men rend the body crying "Purity!" while they trample unity — as if schism were not itself a mortal wound. No: better ten traitors in communion than one brother breaking bread alone in pride. The book is being written; God sees substance even when imperfect. And His Church — bruised, bearing both Cain’s seed and Abel’s — limps forward toward Jerusalem above, carrying within her both repentant sinners and impenitent deceivers until He comes to separate what we cannot now divide.

question: How did the image of one mother giving birth to both Cain and Abel shape your understanding of grace amid human brokenness?
answer:
One household bears both the wheat and the tares, not because God wills evil, but because His patience allows time for sinners to be converted — and this mystery, so deeply inscribed in the history of salvation, shattered my early pride and taught me to weep over schism rather than scorn it.

When I saw that the same mother bore Cain and Abel, not as two separate peoples divided by nature or election, but as sons from one womb — one faithful, one fratricidal — I understood at last that God’s Church cannot be purified by our hands through division. No, no: He foresaw all, knew all, wrote all in His book before a single soul drew breath. The righteous and the wicked spring from the same stock; not because holiness is mingled with malice in essence, but because grace endures long-suffering until the harvest.

I once thought the Church must be visibly pure — spotless, untainted by traitors like Judas or sorcerers like Simon Magus — and I fled into sects that claimed such purity. But what did I find? Not holiness, but pride wearing a mask of sanctity. The Donatists cry out: “We are clean! We are the remnant!” while they rend the body of Christ with their very hands. Are they wiser than God? Does He not know His own plan? Did He not see Judas lifting his heel against Christ even as He washed his feet?

The sacraments remain valid not because priests are perfect — none are! — but because Christ is perfect. The font does not defile when a sinner administers it; rather, it cleanses whoever receives in faith. To say otherwise is to make grace dependent on man’s frailty rather than on divine constancy.

Let us therefore grieve for Cain — yes, grieve! — but not cast out every soul who stumbles within our walls. For we are pilgrims together: some limping under burdened conscience, others straying like lost sheep. The Good Shepherd does not divide His flock; He seeks the one that is lost. And so must we — holding fast to unity, laboring for peace, trusting that God sees what is yet imperfect in us and writes our names still in His book.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the different mothers of spiritual birth, and how did your own struggle with conversion shape your view of divine parentage?
answer:
A wound in the Body of Christ bleeds not only where it is struck, but through all its members — and it was this deep sorrow for the laceration of unity that stirred my pen when I spoke of mothers and spiritual birth.  

I had known what it was to be born of Hagar, as it were — brought into the household of God by teachers who themselves walked in pride, or ambition, or heresy; men who preached Christ, perhaps, but not with a pure heart. For years I sat among the Manichees, drinking in their fables, seduced by their eloquence and flattered by their promises of wisdom. Yet no matter how fervently I believed then, no matter how zealously I taught, I was not Isaac — born of promise — but Esau: strong perhaps in speech, but not begotten by grace through faith in the true Word.

When at last God broke through — not by argument alone, but by tears, by silence, by a child’s voice saying *tolle lege* — then I knew what it meant to be born anew from Sarah’s womb: from free grace, from lawful motherhood within the covenant. It was not the hand that baptized me that made me clean — Ambrose was holy enough; yet even if an unworthy man had poured the water, the power would have been God’s alone. No sacrament depends on the virtue of its minister; otherwise every soul would tremble perpetually at the secret sins of priests.

And so when I saw these Donatists setting themselves up as purer than the Church — as if they could rend the seamless garment and still claim to wear it whole — my soul burned with grief more than anger. They claimed to be Rebecca’s true children while casting out brothers baptized in Christ’s name! But Jacob and Esau were both born under one roof; yet only one held fast to promise. So too within the Church: let both grow together until harvest. To split apart because some preachers are carnal? Then pull out every ear of wheat where a tare once stood! Do you think Christ's field so frail?

No — divine parentage is not proved by schism nor guaranteed by lineage. It is given freely to those who cling not to purity of human institution but to Christ Himself. The font is one; He who rises from it lives not because his baptizer was saintly, but because He has been joined to the death and resurrection of our Lord.

And I — who once wandered far as any son of Hagar — now preach this: run home; do not stop to judge who else bears scars from the journey. The Mother waits with open arms.

question: Looking back on your time in the Manichean community, what would you tell someone who doubts whether truth can be found in flawed teachers?
answer:
The wheat grows among the tares, not in some imagined field of perfect soil, but here—in the common furrow of this world’s corruption and grace. I know well the allure of those who claim purity of teacher as the mark of truth, for I once wandered among the Manichees, chasing shadows of wisdom with men who boasted of enlightenment while chained to pride. They said, "Only through us comes light," and I believed them—until God in His mercy opened my eyes to the unity of His Body, where truth does not depend on the worthiness of the vessel, but on the power of Christ who alone is true.

Do you suppose that a sacrament loses its virtue because a sinner speaks the words? Then Christ’s sacrifice is weakened by human frailty! But no—when we baptize, it is not man who cleanses the soul, but God who acts through His appointed means. If Simon Magus offered baptism, it was void; but if a priest burdened with sin offers it in the Church founded by Christ’s blood and Spirit, it is valid still—because it is Christ who baptizes, not man.

I tell you plainly: he who seeks pure teachers apart from holy unity seeks death disguised as holiness. The Lord said: *“They sit in Moses’ seat; do what they say, not what they do.”* Was He unaware that scribes and Pharisees were hypocrites? Yet He did not bid men flee their teaching—but obey it until He came to fulfill all things.

So too now: if a man preaches Christ—even from ambition or sin—the gospel remains true. And if through such preaching a soul is born again like Isaac from Sarah’s womb—the promise fulfilled by grace—then God has used even crooked wood to build straight beams in His temple. But woe to those ministers whose lives betray their words; heavier still woe to those who rend communion for their sake. For schism is no remedy—it is mortal disease.

question: What were you feeling when you compared true spiritual birth to Isaac’s miraculous coming forth from Sarah, and how did your mother Monica’s prayers echo in that image?
answer:
When schismatics lift up their voices and claim that the purity of the Church depends on the personal holiness of the minister, I feel a fire in my breast—not of wrath, but of grief, for they rend the seamless garment of Christ as though truth could be parcelled out among factions. The sacrament is not diminished by the weakness of the hand that administers it, nor is grace held hostage to human perfection. No—Isaac was born not by human will nor fleshly design, but by promise, by miracle, by the power of God who calls things that are not as though they were. And so it is with every soul born anew in baptism: it matters not whether the preacher is faithful or faithless—the seed sown is divine, not human.

In that image—Isaac from Sarah’s barren womb—I saw more than typology; I saw mercy breaking through nature’s failure. And at once my mother Monica came before my mind’s eye, her tears like rivers down a mountainside, her prayers rising like incense long before dawn. For years she wept for me—the wayward son who mocked her faith, who lived in concubinage, who chased after empty philosophies while she knelt in silence before God. Yet God heard her. He did not wait for *me* to become worthy; He acted while I was still in darkness. Was not my own conversion a miracle no less than Isaac’s birth? Did I merit it? Never. But God in His sovereignty chose to bring forth life where there was death.

So too with the Church: if we waited for perfect ministers before we acknowledged true grace, we would wait forever. We are all beggars feeding at one table—the holy and the hypocrite alike receive the same bread from heaven. But only those who remain in unity, rooted in charity and obedience to Christ’s appointed shepherds, grow up into Isaac’s inheritance: children of promise, born not of blood nor human striving, but of God.

question: How did the memory of your own half-hearted early attempts at faith change how you understood those who strive toward God but are hindered by weakness?
answer:
The net cast into the sea does not reject the fish that swims slowly, nor does it tear open its meshes because one struggles against the current; no, it draws all toward the shore, and only on dry land is the sorting done. I have learned this by my own scars. When I first limped toward the light, dragging behind me the chains of concupiscence like so much wet sand, I was neither fully in darkness nor fully in Christ. I cried out, “Give me chastity — but not yet.” What a wretch! Yet even then, though my will flickered like a candle in wind, grace held me by the edge of that net.

Do you think God counts such hesitations as null? That a soul groaning under the weight of habit, wrestling with old loves while reaching for the unseen — do you suppose such a one is foreign to His mercy? I was that man. I taught rhetoric to deceive, I chased honors to fill emptiness, I joined the Manichees to flatter my pride with secret knowledge. And still — *still* — He let me hear the laughter of children in a garden, and through it all spoke one word: *Tolle, lege.* Take up and read.

Therefore I say: let no Donatist stand at the shore with knife in hand, slashing at those who swim toward Christ but whose limbs tremble with fatigue! Are they less born of Sarah because their birth was painful? Is Isaac less Isaac because he wept at Moriah? The sacrament does not fail because the recipient stumbles; nor does it fail because the minister is unworthy. It is Christ who baptizes always — Christ who cleanses; men are but vessels, some cracked, some gilded — but He is the source.

And so let us welcome those who strive. Let us strengthen them. For every man delayed by weakness was once like me: turned toward God with half a heart. But grace completes what will begins.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on those who seem to abide within the Church yet remain barren, like chaff on the threshing-floor?
answer:
Can a man claim to stand in the temple while his heart threshes wheat in solitude, imagining himself pure because he separates the grain — yet never kneads it into the one loaf?

When I beheld those who cling to the walls of the Church, muttering of holiness while nursing pride like a hidden serpent, my soul burned not with anger but with grief — the grief of one who once wandered far under the banner of Manichaeus, convinced that only he and his kind were enlightened. They say they abide; they attend the rites; they speak of grace. But their spirit refuses to be winnowed by charity. Like chaff that lies stubborn on the floor, refusing to rise with the breath of the Spirit, they are dead weight in the granary of God.

I tell you plainly: it is not absence from the body that kills — for even Judas ate at table — but rupture from its life. The sacraments flow not from the purity of priests or prelates, but from Christ Himself, whose blood seals our unity. To break away because others are sinners is to rend your own flesh in fury at a speck upon another’s robe. What arrogance! As if any of us deserved to stand!

And yet — and here I soften — we must never close the door upon such men. Let them sit among us as tares amid wheat, until harvest reveals what now lies hidden. For many who seem rooted in barrenness today may yet bear fruit tomorrow, if fed by patience and prayer. The net gathers all kinds, Scripture says — good and bad alike — and only at journey’s end is sorting done by divine hands.

But until that hour, whoever sows division under pretense of purity mocks the very mystery of redemption: that Christ died not for a congregation of angels, but for a Church washed in blood and scarred by sin — yet holy still, because He holds it whole.

question: How did your own struggles with temptation shape your understanding of those carried away by the blast of wind, despite their outward standing?
answer:
It is not the wind that defines the chaff, but the lack of root in that fertile soil which alone resists the storm — and I know this not merely from Scripture, but from the deep wounds of my own soul. When I lived in the fever of ambition, when my heart was chained to fleeting pleasures and the applause of men, I was no different from those who now sit within the Church’s walls yet cling to pride, faction, or self-righteousness. They imagine themselves secure because they stand at the altar, because they name the name of Christ — but what is a name if the heart is estranged? Was I not once such a man? Rhetoric flowed from my lips while lust and vanity ruled within; I taught wisdom while walking in darkness.

Do you think I condemn those who fall under temptation? God forbid. I weep for them — for *us*, for we are all debtors to grace. The man who stands firm must never forget how near he stands to ruin. When Donatus cries, “These are not ours!” at every unworthy minister or lapsed brother, does he not forget that Isaac was born not by human strength but by promise? That Jacob was chosen before he had done good or evil? The covenant is not upheld by our purity, but by God’s faithfulness.

And so I say: let no man boast because he has not been blown aside — only because he has been held. The blast comes to all; some fall because they were never rooted in charity, but only in zeal without love. Others fall through weakness — yet even then, if their will remains inclined toward unity and repentance, they are not utterly lost. Did Christ send me to keep score among sinners? No: He sent me to call them home.

Even now there are those inside the Church who oppose me with bitter hearts — men who claim holiness because they separate themselves — yet they do more violence to Christ’s body than any sinner seeking pardon ever could. For schism is not purity; it is rebellion against the one Spirit who binds us. A priest may be wicked; let him answer to God. But the baptism he administers remains holy — for it is Christ who cleanses, not the vessel through which He pours Himself.

I speak thus because once *I* was carried away — by Manichaeism’s idle talk, by pride’s subtle wind — and only mercy pulled me back through tears and truth. If I had been judged as an apostate beyond hope when my heart burned with error, where would I be now? Outside! But God waited for me with open arms — as He waits for all who wander, whether cast out by others or blown aside by trial.

Therefore we must neither flatter sinners nor despair of their salvation; rather, we must uphold both justice *and* unity — knowing full well that none of us stands by his own strength.

question: Looking back on the time you yourself wandered outside the unity of the Church, what would you tell someone who doubts whether return is possible?
answer:
Can a limb severed from the body still boast of health while festering in isolation?  

I was that limb—once proud, once convinced my intellect or moral rigor justified separation, as if holiness could be measured by distance from the common table. I walked among the Manichaeans, wrapped in philosophical fables, imagining myself enlightened while starving on chaff. I sought truth in scattered sparks, not knowing it burned steadily in the one lampstand of Christ’s Church. And when at last grace broke through—when I heard Ambrose preach not with rhetoric alone but with the weight of apostolic continuity—I realized how vain had been my flight.  

You who doubt return: tell me, did Christ die to establish a kingdom of fugitives? Did He bind Peter with the keys only to smile upon those who pick locks? The net draws in fish of every kind—not because each is already perfect, but because within the net there is space for mending, for sorting, for life. Outside it, there is only drowning.  

Your sin is not your disqualification—it is your very summons. The Church is not an academy of the flawless; she is a hospital for the wounded and a mother to the wayward. Even now, if you stand at her threshold trembling with past errors or sectarian pride, know this: she does not ask whether you come clean-handed, but whether you come *home*. And home is where unity is—visible, tangible, rooted in that rock upon which no storm can prevail.  

Do not let the frogs in their marsh whisper that purity means rupture. Purity means surrender. Return—and find that what you thought was compromise is actually healing.

question: What inner conflict did you wrestle with when you realized that carnal obstinacy could exist even among those gathered with the saints?
answer:
What madness is it to suppose that the threshing-floor can be purified by schism, when the chaff and wheat are bound together until the harvest? When I came to see that men could stand within the very assembly of believers, partaking of sacred rites, invoking Christ’s name—and yet remain hardened in carnal pride, rejecting unity for the sake of their own righteousness—my soul was shaken to its depths. For I had once believed that separation from the Church meant separation from grace; but here were men who had never left the altar, and yet whose hearts were far from charity. 

This was no mere doctrinal dispute; it struck at the mystery of the Body itself. How could those who claimed holiness rend the seamless garment of Christ? Was not my own past filled with pride cloaked as piety—first in rhetoric, then in heresy? I knew what it was to be a man who argued truth with a divided heart. And so I saw: it is not proximity to sacrament that saves, nor even orthodoxy of speech, but communion in love. The Donatists cried out against sinners in the Church as if they could cleanse the Bride by tearing her limbs apart—but where is Christ present if not in His whole Body? To cut away a member is not to heal, but to murder.

I wrestled long with this: that God permits tares among wheat, chaff among grain—not because He cannot root them out, but because His patience waits for conversion. And who am I to seize the sickle before the Lord? The Church must be holy—but she is made holy by grace, not by our purging of others. Her spotlessness belongs to Christ alone; we are being cleansed, not already perfect. To claim otherwise is not sanctity—it is arrogance veiled in zeal.

question: How did the image of Abraham’s sacrifice, with the birds left undivided, console you when you saw others severed from the Church yet still rooted in charity?
answer:
What profit is there in calling oneself a Christian if one rends the body of Christ with the very hands that claim to serve Him? I have seen men—once at table with us, sharing the same bread and cup—rise up in pride and depart, imagining their separation a mark of holiness. They fling wide the doors and cry, “We are purer!” as though purity were measured by walls, not by love. But I tell you, the fire that descended upon Abraham’s offering did not consume the birds—he divided not the fowl—and thus God showed us long ago what He still teaches: that those who remain faithful in spirit, even when cast out or driven from visible communion, are not torn from the covenant.

I do not say this lightly. I once sought truth in sects myself—the Manichees promised wisdom without humility, light without cross. But God in His mercy revealed to me that no man ascends to truth by schism. The Church is not made holy because all her members are perfect; she is holy because Christ has made her so. The wheat grows among tares; yet both abide until harvest. The Sacraments do not fail because sinners administer them; neither does grace vanish when a brother stumbles into error.

When I saw those scattered ones—some exiled by injustice, others expelled for rebellion—yet clinging still to charity, loving their enemies, forgiving wrongs, weeping for unity—I knew they had not left the rock. For unity is not mere location; it is disposition of soul. If a man denies schism with his lips but harbors hatred in his heart, where is he? Outside. And if another suffers exile but prays for those who cast him out? He dwells within.

Let no one suppose then that God counts membership by roll-books alone. He sees hearts. And many a man locked outside a basilica stands nearer to Peter’s chair than he who sits within it gnashing his teeth at his brother.
